questions.question: Physical quantities are
questions.question_raw: Physical quantities are

options.label: A
options.text: quantities such as length, mass and time.

options.label: B
options.text: quantities such as kilos, pounds and gallons.

options.label: C
options.text: quantities such as pounds, dollars and rupees.

options.label: D
options.text: quantities such as degrees, radians and steradians.

correct_option_texts: quantities such as length, mass and time.
questions.explanation: A physical quantity is a physical property of a phenomenon, body, or substance, that can be quantified by measurement. A physical quantity can be expressed as the combination of a magnitude expressed by a number – usually a real number – and a unit. All these given above can be expressed as explained so these are physical quantities.

questions.question: Measurement of a physical quantity is essentially the
questions.question_raw: Measurement of a physical quantity is essentially the

options.label: A
options.text: process of observing the physical quantity

options.label: B
options.text: process of comparing with a standard using an instrument

options.label: C
options.text: process of taking readings on an instrument

options.label: D
options.text: process of subdividing the physical quantity

correct_option_texts: process of comparing with a standard using an instrument
questions.explanation: The Measurement of a given quantity is essentially an act or result of comparison between a quantity whose magnitude (amount) is unknown, with a similar quantity whose magnitude (amount) is known, the latter quantity being called a Standard.

questions.question: Fundamental or base quantities are arbitrary. In SI system these are ______, mass, time, electric current, thermodynamic temperature, amount of substance, and luminous intensity.
questions.question_raw: Fundamental or base quantities are arbitrary. In SI system these are ______, mass, time, electric current, thermodynamic temperature, amount of substance, and luminous intensity.
questions.explanation:
A base quantity is chosen and arbitrarily defined, rather than being derived from a combination of other physical quantities. The 7 base quantities are:
 PHYSICAL QUANTITY
 BASE SI UNIT
 Mass (m)
 Kilogram (Kg)
 Length (ll)
 Metre (m)
 Time (t)
 Second (s)
 Current (II)
 Ampere (A)
 Temperature (T)
 Kelvin (K)
 Amount of sub. (n)
 Molar (mol)
 Luminous Intensity (L)
 Candela (cd

questions.question: Unit for a fundamental physical quantity is
questions.question_raw: Unit for a fundamental physical quantity is

options.label: A
options.text: defined as average various reference standards

options.label: B
options.text: the smallest measurable value of the physical quantity

options.label: C
options.text: defined as best of various reference standards

options.label: D
options.text: reference standard for the given physical quantity

correct_option_texts: reference standard for the given physical quantity
questions.explanation: Unit is the reference used as the standard measurement of a physical quantity. The unit in which the fundamental quantities are measured are called fundamental unit and the units used to measure derived quantities are called derived units.

questions.question: In SI system the fundamental units are
questions.question_raw: In SI system the fundamental units are

options.label: A
options.text: meter, kilogram, second, coulomb, Kelvin, mole, candela and horse power

options.label: B
options.text: meter, kilogram, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and candela

options.label: C
options.text: meter, Newton, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and candela

options.label: D
options.text: meter, kilogram, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and watt

correct_option_texts: meter, kilogram, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and candela
questions.explanation: The SI base units and their physical quantities are the metre for measurement of length, the kilogram for mass, the second for time, the ampere for electric current, the kelvin for temperature, the candela for luminous intensity, and the mole for amount of substance.

questions.question: _________ are units of physical quantity that can be expressed as a combination of fundamental physical quantities.
questions.question_raw: _________ are units of physical quantity that can be expressed as a combination of fundamental physical quantities.
questions.explanation: Derived units are units which may be expressed in terms of base units by means of mathematical symbols of multiplication and division.

questions.question: Joule is the SI unit of
questions.question_raw: Joule is the SI unit of
questions.explanation: The joule (symbol J), is a derived unit of energy in the International System of Units. It is equal to the energy transferred to (or work done on) an object when a force of one newton acts on that object in the direction of its motion through a distance of one metre (1 newton metre or N⋅m).

questions.question: Newton is the SI unit of
questions.question_raw: Newton is the SI unit of
questions.explanation: The newton is the SI unit for force; it is equal to the amount of net force required to accelerate a mass of one kilogram at a rate of one meter per second squared.

questions.question: The least count of any measuring equipment is
questions.question_raw: The least count of any measuring equipment is

options.label: A
options.text: the smallest quantity that can be measured using that instrument.

options.label: B
options.text: the smallest reading on the instrument.

options.label: C
options.text: the smallest effect in any instrument.

options.label: D
options.text: the smallest range in any instrument.

correct_option_texts: the smallest quantity that can be measured using that instrument.
questions.explanation: The smallest value that can be measured by the measuring instrument is called its least count. Measured values are good only up to this value. The least count error is the error associated with the resolution of the instrument.

questions.question: Resolution is
questions.question_raw: Resolution is

options.label: A
options.text: a measure of the bias in the instrument

options.label: B
options.text: a measure of the functional errors

options.label: C
options.text: the smallest amount of input signal change that the instrument can detect reliably

options.label: D
options.text: a measure of the systematic errors

correct_option_texts: the smallest amount of input signal change that the instrument can detect reliably
questions.explanation: Resolution is the smallest amount of input signal change that the instrument can detect reliably.

questions.question: If $\theta $ is the parallax angle of a planet at a distance 'D', when observed from two different positions on the Earth, separated by distance 'b', the expression for 'D' is
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\theta {/tex} is the parallax angle of a planet at a distance 'D', when observed from two different positions on the Earth, separated by distance 'b', the expression for 'D' is
questions.explanation:
Parallax Method of Measurement: Astronomers use an effect called parallax to measure distances to nearby stars. Parallax is the apparent displacement of an object because of a change in the observer's point of view.
To measure the distance D of a far away planet S by the parallax method, We observe it from two different positions (observatories) A and B on the Earth, separated by distance AB = b at the same time as shown in the given figure.
We measure the angle between the two directions along which the planet is viewed at these two points. The $\angle$ASB in the figure represented by symbol θ is called the parallax angle or parallactic angle.
As the planet is very far away, bD<<1 and therefore, θ is very small. Then we approximately take AB as an arc of length b of a circle with center at S and the distance D as the radius AS = BS so that AB = b = Dθ where θ is in radians.
D = $\frac{b}{\theta} $

questions.question: Absolute error of the measurement is
questions.question_raw: Absolute error of the measurement is

options.label: A
options.text: the difference between two individual measurements and their mean.

options.label: B
options.text: the difference between the individual measurement and the true value of the quantity.

options.label: C
options.text: the difference between the individual measurement and the true value of the quantity squared.

options.label: D
options.text: the difference between the individual measurement and the true value of the quantity cubed.

correct_option_texts: the difference between the individual measurement and the true value of the quantity.
questions.explanation:
Absolute error is defined as the magnitude of difference between the actual and the individual values of any quantity in question.
Say we measure any given quantity for n number of times and a_1, a_2, a_3 ….. a_ _n are the individual values, then arithmetic mean is given by:
a_ _ m =( [a_ 1+a_2+a_ 3+ ….. + a_ n]/n)
Now absolute error formula as per definition will be:

questions.question: The arithmetic mean of all the absolute errors $\Delta {{\rm{a}}_{{\rm{mean}}}}$ is given by
questions.question_raw: The arithmetic mean of all the absolute errors {tex}\Delta {{\rm{a}}_{{\rm{mean}}}}{/tex} is given by
questions.explanation: We know that mean is the average of all numbers. So mean of all the absolute errors $=\frac{\text { sum of all absolute errors }}{\text { number of errors }}$

questions.question: The relative error is given by
questions.question_raw: The relative error is given by
questions.explanation:
Relative Error or fractional error: It is defined as the ration of mean absolute error to the mean value of the measured quantity.
$\delta a=\frac{\text { mean absolute value }}{\text { mean value }}$
Relative error = $\frac{\Delta a_{\text {mean }}}{a_{\text {mean }}}$

questions.question: Percentage error δa is given by
questions.question_raw: Percentage error δa is given by
questions.explanation:
Percentage Error: It is the relative error measured in percentage.
So Percentage Error $\delta a ={ mean \ absolute\ value\over mean\ value} \times 100$%
$\delta a ={ Δa_{mean}\over a_{mean}}\times 100$%

questions.question: When two quantities are added or subtracted, the absolute error in the final result is
questions.question_raw: When two quantities are added or subtracted, the absolute error in the final result is

options.label: A
options.text: absolute value of the sum of errors of individual quantities

options.label: B
options.text: absolute value of the difference of errors of individual quantities

options.label: C
options.text: absolute value of the sum of individual quantities

options.label: D
options.text: the sum of the absolute errors in the individual quantities

correct_option_texts: the sum of the absolute errors in the individual quantities
questions.explanation:
When two quantities are added or subtracted, the absolute error in the final result is the sum of the absolute errors in the individual quantities.
For e.g: error $∆x$ in the sum x = a + b is $∆x = \pm (∆a+∆b)$
Maximum value of error, $∆x$ = $(∆a+∆b)$

questions.question: When two quantities are multiplied or divided, the relative error in the result is
questions.question_raw: When two quantities are multiplied or divided, the relative error in the result is

options.label: A
options.text: the sum of the absolute errors in the multipliers

options.label: B
options.text: the difference of the absolute errors in the multipliers

options.label: C
options.text: the difference of the relative errors in the multipliers

options.label: D
options.text: the sum of the relative errors in the multipliers

correct_option_texts: the sum of the relative errors in the multipliers
questions.explanation:
When two quantities are multiplied or divided, the relative error in the result is the sum of the relative errors in the multipliers.
e.g x = a x b
Maximum possible error is: ${∆x\over x} = \pm ({{∆a\over a} + {∆b\over b}})$
Similarly, for error in quotient: x = $a\over b$
Maximum possible error is: ${∆x\over x} = \pm ({{∆a\over a} + {∆b\over b}}) $

questions.question: The relative error in a physical quantity raised to the power k is
questions.question_raw: The relative error in a physical quantity raised to the power k is

options.label: A
options.text: (k – 1) times the relative error in the individual quantity

options.label: B
options.text: k times the absolute error in the individual quantity

options.label: C
options.text: k times the relative error in the individual quantity.

options.label: D
options.text: (k – 1) times the absolute error in the individual quantity

correct_option_texts: k times the relative error in the individual quantity.
questions.explanation:
The relative error in a physical quantity raised to a power k is the k times the relative error in the individual quantity.
for e.g. $x = {a^n\over b^m}$
Maximun relative error, ${∆x\over x} = \pm ({n{∆a\over a}+m{∆b\over b}})$

questions.question: The significant figures of a number are those digits that
questions.question_raw: The significant figures of a number are those digits that

options.label: A
options.text: carry meaning contributing to its measurement resolution.

options.label: C
options.text: are at even positions after decimal.

options.label: D
options.text: are at odd positions after decimal.

correct_option_texts: carry meaning contributing to its measurement resolution.
questions.explanation:
The significant figures of a number are digits that carry meaning contributing to its measurement resolution. This includes all digits except:
 All leading zeros;
 Trailing zeros when they are merely placeholders to indicate the scale of the number (exact rules are explained at identifying significant figures); and
 Spurious digits introduced, for example, by calculations carried out to greater precision than that of the original data, or measurements reported to a greater precision than the equipment supports.

questions.question: The power (or exponent) to which the base quantities are raised to represent that quantity is called
questions.question_raw: The power (or exponent) to which the base quantities are raised to represent that quantity is called
questions.explanation: The dimension of the units of a derived physical quantity may be defined as the number of times the fundamental units of mass, length and time appear in the physical quantity.

questions.question: According to the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, we can add or subtract
questions.question_raw: According to the principle of homogeneity of dimensions, we can add or subtract
questions.explanation: It would be more exact to say that "Quantities with the same units can be added and subtracted with no problem." Quantities with the same dimensions can often be added and subtracted as long as the units are correctly converted.

questions.question: In an equation, the dimensions of the LHS and RHS of the equation are the same. The equation is
questions.question_raw: In an equation, the dimensions of the LHS and RHS of the equation are the same. The equation is
questions.explanation: Principle of homogenity of dimensions states that “For an equation to be dimansionally correct, the dimensions of each term on LHS must be equal to the dimensions of each term on RHS.”

questions.question: The dimensional formula for Planck’s constant is
questions.question_raw: The dimensional formula for Planck’s constant is
questions.explanation: [ML^2T^-1] (Hint use E = hν)

questions.question: A dimensionally consistent equation
questions.question_raw: A dimensionally consistent equation
questions.explanation: If an equation fails this consistency test, it is proved wrong, but if it passes, it is not proved right. Thus, a dimensionally correct equation need not be actually an exact (correct) equation, but a dimensionally wrong (incorrect) or inconsistent equation is definitely wrong.

questions.question: Electric charge is expressed in SI units as
questions.question_raw: Electric charge is expressed in SI units as
questions.explanation: The SI derived unit of electric charge is the coulomb (C). The symbol Q often denotes charge.

questions.question: The units of pressure are the same as the units of _______.
questions.question_raw: The units of pressure are the same as the units of _______.
questions.explanation: Pressure is not the same physical thing as stress, but they do use the same definition and thus produce the same units, so the same calculation can be used for both. They both measure force per unit area. The SI unit of both pressure and stress is Pascal.

questions.question: The volume of a cube of side 2 cm is equal (in ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$) to
questions.question_raw: The volume of a cube of side 2 cm is equal (in {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex}) to
questions.explanation:
Side of cube (a) = 2 cm = $2\times 10^{-2}m$
Volume of cube (V) = $a^3 = (2\times 10^{-2})^3$ = $8.0 \times 10^{-6}m^3$

questions.question: The surface area of a solid cylinder of radius 2.0 cm and height 10.0 cm (in ${\rm{m}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$) is equal to
questions.question_raw: The surface area of a solid cylinder of radius 2.0 cm and height 10.0 cm (in {tex}{\rm{m}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}) is equal to
questions.explanation:
Radius of cylinder r = 2 cm = 20 mm
Height of cylinder h = 10 cm = 100 mm
Surface area of cylinder = $2\pi r(r+h)$
= $2\times 3.14 \times 20 (20+100)$ 
= $2\times 3.14 \times 20 \times 120 $
= 15072 mm^​​​​​2 $\approx 1.5\times 10^4 mm^2$

questions.question: How many meters does a vehicle moving with a speed of 18 km ${{\rm{h}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}$ covers in 1 s
questions.question_raw: How many meters does a vehicle moving with a speed of 18 km {tex}{{\rm{h}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex} covers in 1 s
questions.explanation:
Speed of vehicle = 18 km h^​​​​​​-1
Mean it cover 18 km in 1 hour {3600 seconds)
Distance covered in 3600 sec = 18km = 18000 m
Distance covered in 1 sec = ${18000\over 3600 }= 5m$

questions.question: The relative density of lead is 11.3. Its density in g ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - {\rm{3}}}}$ is
questions.question_raw: The relative density of lead is 11.3. Its density in g {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{ - {\rm{3}}}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
Relative density means how much times a substance is denser than water.
Now relative density of lead = 11.3 means lead is 11.3 times denser than water.
Now water has a density 1 g cm^–3 in cgs units
Thus lead will have a density = 11.3 x 1 g cm^–3 = 11.3 g cm^–3

questions.question: How many g ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ does 1 kg ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ equal?
questions.question_raw: How many g {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} does 1 kg {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} equal?
questions.explanation:
We know 1 kg = 10^3 g
1 m^​​​​​​2 = 10^4 cm^2
1 s^​​​​​​-2 = 1s^-2
Multiply all these quantities we get
$\Rightarrow$ 1 kg m^​​​​​​2 s^​​​​​​-2 = $10^3\times 10^4 gcm^2s^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow 1kgm^2s^{-2}= 10^7 gcm^2s^{-2}$

questions.question: Approximately how many light years is a meter?
questions.question_raw: Approximately how many light years is a meter?
questions.explanation:
A light-year is a unit of distance. It is the distance that light can travel in one year. Light moves at a velocity of about 300,000 kilometers (km) each second. So in one year, it can travel about 10 trillion km. More precisely, one light-year is equal to $9.5\times 10^{15}m$.
$\Rightarrow$ 1 Light Year = $9.5\times 10^{15}m $
$\Rightarrow$ 1 m = ${1\over 9.5\times 10^{15} } Light\ year $
$\Rightarrow 1 m \approx 10^{-16}Light \ year $

questions.question: Approximately how many km ${{\rm{h}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ is 3 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ ?
questions.question_raw: Approximately how many km {tex}{{\rm{h}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} is 3 m {tex}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
1 km h^​​​​​​-2 = ${1000m\over 3600\times 3600 \;s^2} = {1\over 360\times 36}ms^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $1ms^{-2} = 360\times 36 km\ h^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $3ms^{-2} =3\times 360\times 36 km\ h^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $3ms^{-2}= 38880km\ h^{-2} $
$\Rightarrow 3ms^{-2} \approx 3.9\times 10^4 km \ h^{-2}$

questions.question: A new unit of length is chosen such that the speed of light in vacuum is unity. What is the distance between the Sun and the Earth in terms of the new unit if light takes 8 min and 20 s to cover this distance?
questions.question_raw: A new unit of length is chosen such that the speed of light in vacuum is unity. What is the distance between the Sun and the Earth in terms of the new unit if light takes 8 min and 20 s to cover this distance?
questions.explanation:
Distance between the Sun and the Earth $=$ Speed of light $\times$ Time taken by light to cover the distance
It is given that in the new unit system, speed of light = 1 unit.
Time taken, t = 8 min 20 s = 500 s
Theref​​​​​​ore, Distance between the Sun and the Earth = 1 x 500 = 500 new units of length

questions.question: Which of the following is the most precise device for measuring length?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the most precise device for measuring length?

options.label: A

options.label: B
options.text: A screw gauge of pitch 1 mm and 100 divisions on the circular scale.

options.label: D
options.text: An optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light.

correct_option_texts: An optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light.
questions.explanation: An optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light.

questions.question: A student measures the thickness of a human hair by looking at it through a microscope of magnification 100. He makes 20 observations and finds that the average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope is 3.5 mm. What is the estimate on the thickness of hair ?
questions.question_raw: A student measures the thickness of a human hair by looking at it through a microscope of magnification 100. He makes 20 observations and finds that the average width of the hair in the field of view of the microscope is 3.5 mm. What is the estimate on the thickness of hair ?
questions.explanation:
Magnification of microscope = 100
Observed width of the hair = 3.5 mm
Estimate on the thickness of hair is given by
Magnification = $Observed \ width \over Real \ width $
Real width = $Observed\ width \over Magnification $= $3.5 \over 100$ = 0.035 mm

questions.question: The photograph of a house occupies an area of 1.75 ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$ on a 35 mm slide. The slide is projected on to a screen, and the area of the house on the screen is 1.55 ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$ . What is the linear magnification of the projector-screen arrangement?
questions.question_raw: The photograph of a house occupies an area of 1.75 {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} on a 35 mm slide. The slide is projected on to a screen, and the area of the house on the screen is 1.55 {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} . What is the linear magnification of the projector-screen arrangement?
questions.explanation:
Area of the house on the slide = 1.75 cm^​​2
Area of the image of the house formed on the screen = 1.55 m^​​​​​​2 = 1.55 × 10^4 cm^​​​​​2
Arial magnification, m_​​​​​​a = $Area \ of \ image\over area \ of \ object $ = ${1.55\times 10^4\over 1.75}$= 8857
Linear magnification, m_$l$ = $\sqrt{a_m}= \sqrt{8857}$ = 94.1

questions.question: The number of significant digits in 0.007 is
questions.question_raw: The number of significant digits in 0.007 is
questions.explanation:
There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
So keeping these rules in mind, there are 1 significant digit.

So keeping these rules in mind, there are 3 significant digits.

questions.question: The mass of a box measured by a grocer's balance is 2.300 kg. Two gold pieces of masses 20.15 g and 20.17 g are added to the box. What is the total mass of the box and the difference in the masses of the pieces to correct significant figures?
questions.question_raw: The mass of a box measured by a grocer's balance is 2.300 kg. Two gold pieces of masses 20.15 g and 20.17 g are added to the box. What is the total mass of the box and the difference in the masses of the pieces to correct significant figures?
questions.explanation:
Total mass = 2.3 kg + 0.02015 kg + 0.02017 kg = 2.34032 kg = 2.3 kg
Difference of mass of the gold pieces = 20.17 g - 20.15 g = 0.02 g

questions.question: The length, breadth and thickness of a rectangular sheet of metal are 4.234 m, 1.005 m, and 2.01 cm respectively. Give the area of the sheet to correct significant figures.
questions.question_raw: The length, breadth and thickness of a rectangular sheet of metal are 4.234 m, 1.005 m, and 2.01 cm respectively. Give the area of the sheet to correct significant figures.
questions.explanation:
Length of sheet is given by, l = 4.234 m
Breadth of sheet is given by, b = 1.005 m
Thickness of sheet is given by, t = 2.01 cm = 2.01 × 10^-2 m
Area of the sheet = 2 (l × b + b × t + t × l) = 2 (4.234 × 1.005 + 1.005 × 0.0201 + 0.0201 × 4.234) = 2 (4.3604739) = 8.7209478 m^​​​​​​2 
As area can contain a maximum of three significant digits, therefore, on rounding off, we get area = 8.72 m^​​​​​​2

questions.question: The length, breadth and thickness of a rectangular sheet of metal are 4.234 m, 1.005 m, and 2.01 cm respectively. Give the volume of the sheet to correct significant figures.
questions.question_raw: The length, breadth and thickness of a rectangular sheet of metal are 4.234 m, 1.005 m, and 2.01 cm respectively. Give the volume of the sheet to correct significant figures.
questions.explanation:
length of sheet, l = 4.234 m
breadth of sheet, b = 1.005 m
thickness of sheet, t = 2.01 cm = 2.01 × 10^-2 m
volume = l × b × t
$\Rightarrow$ V = 4.234 × 1.005 × 0.0201 = 0.0855289 = 0.0855 m^​​​​​​3 (This number has only 3 significant figures i.e., 8, 5, and 5)

questions.question: A physical quantity P is related to four observables a, b, c and d as follows : $P = \frac{a^3 b^2}{\sqrt{c} d}$. The percentage errors of measurement in a, b, c and d are 1$\% $, 3$\% $, 4$\% $ and 2$\% $, respectively. What is the percentage error in the quantity P?
questions.question_raw: A physical quantity P is related to four observables a, b, c and d as follows : {tex}P = \frac{a^3 b^2}{\sqrt{c} d}{/tex}. The percentage errors of measurement in a, b, c and d are 1{tex}\% {/tex}, 3{tex}\% {/tex}, 4{tex}\% {/tex} and 2{tex}\% {/tex}, respectively. What is the percentage error in the quantity P?
questions.explanation:
Given that, P = $\frac {a^3b^2}{ \sqrt c \ d}$
Maximum fractional error in P is given by 
$\frac{\Delta P}{P}= \pm\left(3 \frac{\Delta a}{a}+2 \frac{\Delta b}{b}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta c}{c}+\frac{\Delta d}{d}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta P}{P}= \pm\left(3 \frac{1}{100}+2 \frac{3}{100}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{4}{100}+\frac{2}{100}\right)$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta P}{P}= \pm 0.13$
Percentage error in P is given by= $\frac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100=0.13 \times 100$ = 13%

questions.question: A physical quantity P is related to four observables a, b, c and d as follows: $P=\frac{a^3 b^2}{\sqrt{c} d}$. The percentage errors of measurement in a, b, c and d are 1$\% $, 3$\% $, 4$\% $ and 2$\% $, respectively. If the value of P calculated using the above relation turns out to be 3.763, to what value should you round off the result?
questions.question_raw: A physical quantity P is related to four observables a, b, c and d as follows: {tex}P=\frac{a^3 b^2}{\sqrt{c} d}{/tex}. The percentage errors of measurement in a, b, c and d are 1{tex}\% {/tex}, 3{tex}\% {/tex}, 4{tex}\% {/tex} and 2{tex}\% {/tex}, respectively. If the value of P calculated using the above relation turns out to be 3.763, to what value should you round off the result?
questions.explanation:
Value of P is given as 3.763.
By rounding off the given value to the first decimal place, we get
P = 3.8

questions.question: A book with many printing errors contains four different formulas for the displacement y of a particle undergoing a certain periodic motion. Choose the correct formula
questions.question_raw: A book with many printing errors contains four different formulas for the displacement y of a particle undergoing a certain periodic motion. Choose the correct formula
questions.explanation:
Dimension of y is given by = M^​​​​​​0 L^​​​​​​1 T^0
Dimension of a is given by = M^0L^​​1T^​​​​​​0 
Dimension of $sin\frac{2\pi t}{T}$= $M^0L^0T^0$
Dimension of L.H.S = Dimension of R.H.S. Hence, the given formula is dimensionally correct.

questions.question: The unit of length convenient on the atomic scale is known as an angstrom and is denoted by ${\mathop A\limits^0}:{\rm{ 1 }}{\mathop A\limits^0}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ 1}}{0^{ - {\rm{1}}0}}$ m. The size of a hydrogen atom is about $0.{\rm{5 }}{\mathop A\limits^0}$ . What is the total atomic volume in ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of a mole of hydrogen atoms ?
questions.question_raw: The unit of length convenient on the atomic scale is known as an angstrom and is denoted by {tex}{\mathop A\limits^0}:{\rm{ 1 }}{\mathop A\limits^0}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ 1}}{0^{ - {\rm{1}}0}}{/tex} m. The size of a hydrogen atom is about {tex}0.{\rm{5 }}{\mathop A\limits^0}{/tex} . What is the total atomic volume in {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex} of a mole of hydrogen atoms ?
questions.explanation:
Radius of hydrogen atom, r = 0.5 Å = 0.5 × 10^-10 m
Volume of hydrogen atom is given by = (4/3) $\pi$ r^3
= (4/3) × (22/7) × (0.5 × 10^-10)^3
= 0.524 × 10^-30 m^3
1 mole of hydrogen contains 6.023 × 10^23 hydrogen atoms.
∴ Volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms is given by = 6.023 × 10^23 × 0.524 × 10^–30
= 3.16 × 10^–7 m^3
$\approx 3\times 10^{-7} m^3$

questions.question: One mole of an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure occupies 22.4 L (molar volume). What is the ratio of molar volume to the atomic volume of a mole of hydrogen? (Take the size of hydrogen molecule to be about 1 Å).
questions.question_raw: One mole of an ideal gas at standard temperature and pressure occupies 22.4 L (molar volume). What is the ratio of molar volume to the atomic volume of a mole of hydrogen? (Take the size of hydrogen molecule to be about 1 Å).
questions.explanation:
Radius of hydrogen atom, r = 0.5 = 0.5 × 10^–10 m
Volume of hydrogen atom = ${4\over 3} \times {22\over 7} \times (0.5\times 10^{-10})^3$
= $0.524\times 10^{-30}m^3$
Now, 1 mole of hydrogen contains 6.023 × 10^23 hydrogen atoms.
Volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms, V_a = 6.023 × 10^23 × 0.524 × 10^–30
= 3.16 × 10^–7 m^3
Molar volume of 1 mole of hydrogen atoms at STP,
V_m = 22.4 L = 22.4 × 10^–3 m^3
$\Rightarrow$ ${V_m\over V_a}= {22.4\times 10^{-3}\over 3.16\times 10^{-7}}$= 7.08 $\times 10^{4}$
= $\cong 10^{4}$

questions.question: When the planet Jupiter is at a distance of 824.7 million kilometers from the Earth, its angular diameter is measured to be 35.72” of arc. Calculate the diameter of Jupiter.
questions.question_raw: When the planet Jupiter is at a distance of 824.7 million kilometers from the Earth, its angular diameter is measured to be 35.72” of arc. Calculate the diameter of Jupiter.
questions.explanation:
Distance of Jupiter from the Earth, D = 824.7 × 10^6 km
Angular diameter = 35.72“ = 35.72 × 4.874 × 10^-6 rad
Diameter of Jupiter = d
Using the relation, θ = $d\over D$
d = θ$\times$ D = 824.7 × 10^6 × 35.72 × 4.872 × 10^-6 $\cong 1.429\times 10^5 km$

questions.question: The significant digits in 0.000532 are

There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
Keeping these rules in mind, we can say that only 5,3,2 are significant digits.

questions.question: Population of a town is reported as 157,900. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_raw: Population of a town is reported as 157,900. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.explanation: 157900 implies that the population is believed to be within the range of about 157850 to about 157950. In other words, the population is 157900±50. The “plus-or-minus 50” appended to this number means that we consider the absolute uncertainty of the population measurement to be 50 – (–50) = 100. We can also say that the relative uncertainty is 100/157900, which we can also express as 1 part in 1579, or 1/1579 = 0.000633, or about 0.06 percent.

questions.question: Population of a town is reported as 158000. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_raw: Population of a town is reported as 158000. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.explanation: The value 158000 implies that the population is likely between about 157500 and 158500, or 158000±500. The absolute uncertainty of 1000 translates into a relative uncertainty of $1000\over 158000$ or 1 part in 158, or about 0.6 percent.

$1 cm =10^{-2}\;m$ hence its value in MKS is given by :-
$70 dyne/cm= 70 \times \large\frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-2}}$ = 7 ✕ 10^-2 N/m

questions.question: The result of rounding off 34.216 to 3 digits is
questions.question_raw: The result of rounding off 34.216 to 3 digits is
questions.explanation:
The rules for rounding off are following. 
 If the first non-significant digit is less than 5, then the least significant digit remains unchanged.
 If the first non-significant digit is greater than 5, the least significant digit is incremented by 1.
 If the first non-significant digit is 5, the least significant digit can either be incremented or left unchanged.
 All non-significant digits are removed.
So rounding off 34.216 upto 4 digits is 34.22 and upto 3 digits is 34.2

questions.question: Dimensional analysis can be used to
questions.question_raw: Dimensional analysis can be used to

options.label: C

options.label: D

questions.explanation: Dimensional analysis is also used to deduce the relation between two or more physical quantities. If we know the degree of dependence of a physical quantity on another, that is the degree to which one quantity changes with the change in another, we can use the principle of consistency of two expressions to find the equation relating these two quantities.

questions.question: An object has a length of 0.42 cm. Which of the following statements is correct?

Suppose, you are simply told that an object has a measured length with no indication of its precision as in the question.
In this case, all you have to go on is the number of digits contained in the data. Thus the quantity “0.42 cm” is specified to 0.01.
So the uncertainty in the length is 0.01.

questions.question: The position of an object moving in a straight line can be specified with reference to
questions.question_raw: The position of an object moving in a straight line can be specified with reference to
questions.explanation: To describe motion along a straight line, we can choose an axis, say X-axis, so that it coincides with the path of the object. We then measure the position of the object with reference to a conveniently chosen origin, say O. Positions to the right of O are taken as positive and to the left of O, as negative.

questions.question: For motion in a straight line
questions.question_raw: For motion in a straight line

options.label: B
options.text: position to the left of the origin is taken as positive and to the right as negative.

options.label: C
options.text: motion away from origin is positive

options.label: D
options.text: position to the right of the origin is taken as positive and to the left as negative.

correct_option_texts: position to the right of the origin is taken as positive and to the left as negative.
questions.explanation: To describe motion along a straight line, we can choose any axis, let us consider X-axis, so that it coincides with the path of the object. We then measure the position of the object with reference to a origin, say O. Positions to the right of O are taken as positive and to the left of O, as negative.

questions.question: Path length is defined as
questions.question_raw: Path length is defined as

options.label: B
options.text: the distance from origin to maximum point.

options.label: C
options.text: the total length of the path traversed by an object.

options.label: D
options.text: the distance from origin to minimum point.

correct_option_texts: the total length of the path traversed by an object.
questions.explanation: The path length is defined as the total length of the path traversed by an object. Unlike displacement, which is the shortest distance between two points, the path length is the total distance travelled, regardless of where it travelled.

questions.question: Path length is a________________.
questions.question_raw: Path length is a________________.
questions.explanation: Path length has no particular direction and it depends upon the path chosen to reach the destination, so it is scaler.

questions.question: In Kinematics we study ways to
questions.question_raw: In Kinematics we study ways to

options.label: A
options.text: describe motion without going into the causes of motion.

options.label: B
options.text: find jerk without going into the causes of motion.

options.label: C
options.text: find acceleration without going into the causes of motion.

options.label: D
options.text: find velocity without going into the causes of motion.

correct_option_texts: describe motion without going into the causes of motion.
questions.explanation: Kinematics is a branch of classical mechanics that describes the motion of points, bodies (objects), and systems of bodies (groups of objects) without considering the mass of each or the forces that caused the motion.

questions.question: For motion in 3 dimensions we need
questions.question_raw: For motion in 3 dimensions we need

options.label: A
options.text: frame of reference consisting of a clock and a Cartesian system having two mutually $\perp$ axes (X and Y)

options.label: B
options.text: frame of reference consisting of a clock and a Cartesian system having three mutually $\perp$ axes, (X,Y, and Z)

options.label: C
options.text: No frame of reference is required a single point will do.

options.label: D
options.text: No frame of reference is required ; a set of 3 points will do.

correct_option_texts: frame of reference consisting of a clock and a Cartesian system having three mutually $\perp$ axes, (X,Y, and Z)
questions.explanation:
Motion is a change in position of an object with time. In order to specify the position, we need to use a reference point and a set of axes. It is convenient to choose a rectangular coordinate system consisting of three mutually perpendicular axes, labelled X-, Y-, and Z- axes.
The point of intersection of these three axes is called origin (O) and serves as the reference point. The coordinates (x, y. z) of an object describe the position of the object with respect to this coordinate system.
To measure time, we position a clock in this system. This coordinate system along with a clock constitutes a frame of reference.

questions.question: For one-dimensional motion displacement is the change in position and is given by
questions.question_raw: For one-dimensional motion displacement is the change in position and is given by
questions.explanation:
Displacement is defined to be the change in position of an object. It can be defined mathematically with the following equation:
$Displacement = ∆x = x_2-x_1$
where $x_2$ refers to the value of the final position, $x_1 \ \ $refers to the value of the initial position and $∆x$ is the symbol used to represent displacement.

questions.question: Displacement is a
questions.question_raw: Displacement is a
questions.explanation: Displacement vector is a vector which gives the position of a point with reference to a point other than the origin of the coordinate system.

questions.question: An object is said to be in uniform motion in a straight line if its displacement
questions.question_raw: An object is said to be in uniform motion in a straight line if its displacement

options.label: A
options.text: is equal in equal intervals of time.

options.label: B
options.text: is increasing in equal intervals of time.

options.label: C
options.text: is equal in not equal intervals of time.

options.label: D
options.text: is decreasing in equal intervals of time.

correct_option_texts: is equal in equal intervals of time.
questions.explanation:
Uniform motion is the kind of motion in which a body covers equal displacement in equal intervals of time. It does
not matter how small the time intervals are, as long as the displacements covered are equal. If a body is involved in rectilinear motion and the motion is uniform, then the acceleration of the body must be zero.

questions.question: The change in position or displacement ($\Delta {\rm{x}}$) divided by the time intervals ($\Delta {\rm{t}}$), in which the displacement occurs is known as
questions.question_raw: The change in position or displacement ({tex}\Delta {\rm{x}}{/tex}) divided by the time intervals ({tex}\Delta {\rm{t}}{/tex}), in which the displacement occurs is known as
questions.explanation:
Average velocity is the displacement of an object, divided by the time it took to cover that distance.
$V_{average}= {∆x\over ∆t}$
Displacement is the straight line distance between the starting point and ending point of an object's motion.
Velocity is referred to as a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

questions.question: Average speed is defined as the total path length travelled divided by the total time interval during which
questions.question_raw: Average speed is defined as the total path length travelled divided by the total time interval during which
questions.explanation: The average speed is the ratio of distance traveled to the time spent. Average speed is defined as the total path length travelled divided by the total time interval during which the motion has taken place.

questions.question: Average speed is
questions.question_raw: Average speed is
questions.explanation: Average speed is the total distance traveled divided by the elapsed time. Average speed is the absolute value of the average velocity or the magnitude of the velocity. Therefore, average Speed is always positive.

questions.question: Instantaneous velocity or simply velocity v at an instant t equals
questions.question_raw: Instantaneous velocity or simply velocity v at an instant t equals
questions.explanation:
Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of an object in motion at a specific point in time. This is determined similarly to average velocity, but we narrow the period of time so that it approaches zero. The formula for instantaneous velocity is the limit as t approaches zero of the change in position over the change in t. Mathematically,
$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\rm{t}} \to 0} \frac{{\Delta x}}{{\Delta t}}$
The Instantaneous Velocity is expressed in m/s.

questions.question: While determining velocity from the position-time graph, velocity at any time is the slope of the _______ to the point on the graph at that instant.
questions.question_raw: While determining velocity from the position-time graph, velocity at any time is the slope of the  _______ to the point on the graph at that instant.
questions.explanation: Velocity is defined as the rate of change of position with respect to time, which may also be referred to as the instantaneous velocity to emphasize the distinction from the average velocity. In terms of a displacement-time (x vs. t) graph, the instantaneous velocity (or, simply, velocity) can be thought of as the slope of the tangent line to the curve at any point.

questions.question: If the position- time graph is a straight line then
questions.question_raw: If the position- time graph is a straight line then
questions.explanation: The principle is that the slope of the line on a position-time graph reveals useful information about the velocity of the object. If the velocity is constant, then the slope is constant (i.e., a straight line).

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Motion in A Straight Line

questions.question: If the position- time graph is a straight line parallel to the time axis
questions.question_raw: If the position- time graph is a straight line parallel to the time axis

options.label: C
options.text: the velocity is constant but non zero.

questions.explanation:
Position-time graph of horizontal straight line parallel to time axis represents that the position of the body does not changes with the passage of time. So it represents the rest state of motion.
It means velocity of object is zero.

questions.question: The average acceleration 'a' over a time interval is defined as
questions.question_raw: The average acceleration 'a' over a time interval is defined as

options.label: A
options.text: average speed divided by the time interval.

options.label: B
options.text: the change in velocity divided by the time interval.

options.label: C
options.text: average velocity divided by the time interval.

options.label: D
options.text: average displacement divided by the time interval.

correct_option_texts: the change in velocity divided by the time interval.
questions.explanation:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time. It is a vector quantity. Average acceleration is the change in velocity per unit time over an interval of time.Let initial velocity at time interval t_​​​​​​1 is v_​​​​​​1 and final velocity at time interval t_​​​​​2 is v_​​​​​​2, then
$\vec a_{avg}={∆\vec v\over ∆t}={\vec v_2-\vec v_1\over t_2-t_1}$

questions.question: Instantaneous acceleration is defined as
questions.question_raw: Instantaneous acceleration is defined as
questions.explanation:
Instantaneous acceleration is measured over a "short" time interval. The word short in this context means infinitely small or infinitesimal — having no duration or extent whatsoever. It's a mathematical ideal that can can only be realized as a limit. The limit of a rate as the denominator approaches zero is called a derivative.
Instantaneous acceleration is then the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero — or alternatively, acceleration is the derivative of velocity.
$a=\lim _\limits{\mathrm{t} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}=\frac{d v}{d t}$

questions.question: For uniformly accelerated motion velocity at time t is given by
questions.question_raw: For uniformly accelerated motion velocity at time t is given by
questions.explanation:
Consider a particle moving along a straight line with uniform acceleration 'a'. At t = 0, let the particle be at A and $v_o$ be its initial velocity and when t = t, v be its final velocity.
Acceleration = $Change\ in\ velocity \over time\ taken$ 
$\Rightarrow a ={v-v_o\over t-0}$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $at= v-v_o$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $v= v_o+at$

questions.question: A car moving at 60 km/h comes to a stop in 4.0 s. What was its deceleration?
questions.question_raw: A car moving at 60 km/h comes to a stop in 4.0 s. What was its deceleration?
questions.explanation:
initial velocity u = 60 km/h = 60 $\times$1000/(60 $\times$ 60) = 16.667m/s
As it stop final velocity v = 0
Time taken t = 4 s
We know,
a = $ {v-u\over t }$
$= {0-16.667\over 4}$ = −4.1667m/s^2
A negative sign shows it is a decelaration.

questions.question: A car is traveling due west at 20.0 m/s. Find the velocity of the car after 37.00 s if its constant acceleration is 1.0 m/s^2 due east. Assume the acceleration remains constant.
questions.question_raw: A car is traveling due west at 20.0 m/s. Find the velocity of the car after 37.00 s if its constant acceleration is 1.0 m/s2 due east. Assume the acceleration remains constant.
questions.explanation:
Assume west direction as +ve. Initial velocity, u = + 20.0 m/s
Final velocity = ?
Acceleration a = - 1.0 m/s^​​​​​​2
Time taken t = 37.0 s
We know, v = u + at
$v = 20.0 - 37.0\times 1$
v = -17.0
The negative sign shows direction of velocity is opposite. So velocity is 17.0 m/s due east.

questions.question: For motion with uniform acceleration, x-t graph is
questions.question_raw: For motion with uniform acceleration, x-t graph is
questions.explanation:
We know that , s = ut + 0.5at^2
The slope of the x-t graph will give you velocity. So, if u=0, the slope of the graph at t=0 is 0. For u>0, the initial slope is +ve and for negative u, the initial slope is negative. Hence you will get parabolic shapes.

Point object is used in kinematics branch of physics. It is an object whose dimensions are ignored or neglected while considering its motion.
The size of a carriage is very small as compared to the distance between two stations. Thus, the carriage can be treated as a point sized object.

questions.question:
The distance(s) in metres travelled by a particle is related to time(t) in seconds by the equation of motion:
S = 10t + 4t^2. What is the initial velocity of the body?
questions.question_raw:
The distance(s) in metres travelled by a particle is related to time(t) in seconds by the equation of motion:
S = 10t + 4t2. What is the initial velocity of the body?
questions.explanation:
Velocity is given by:
v = $ds\over dt$= $d(10t+4t^2)\over dt$= 10 + 8t
Velocity at t = 0, v_0 = 10 + 8 $\times$ 0 = 10 m/s

questions.question: SI unit of acceleration is
questions.question_raw: SI unit of acceleration is
questions.explanation:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity of an object with respect to time.
a = $change \ in \ velocity \over time \ taken $
SI unit of velocity = m/s
SI unit of time = s
So SI unit of acceleration = ${m\over s\times s}= m/s^2$

questions.question: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000m on a straight road at time t=0. At time t=10 seconds it is at a distance of 900 m. The average velocity in m/sec is
questions.question_raw: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000m on a straight road at time t=0. At time t=10 seconds it is at a distance of 900 m. The average velocity in m/sec is
questions.explanation:
Initial distance x_​​​​​​1 = 1000 m
Initial time t_​​​​​​1= 0 sec
Final distance, x_​​​​​​2 = 900 m
Final time t_​​​​​​2 = 10 sec
Average velocity, $v_{avg}={distance \ covered \over time \ taken}$ = ${1000-900\over 10-0}={100\over 10}= 10\ m/s$

questions.question: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000 m on a straight road, at time t = 0. It reaches you in 100 seconds. The average speed in m/s is:

Average speed, $v_{avg}={total \ distance \ covered\over total \ time \ taken}$ $= {1000\over 100}$ = 10 m/s

questions.question: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000 m on a straight road. At time t=0 it starts from rest. At time t=10 seconds its velocity is 20m/s. What is the average acceleration in m/ ${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$?
questions.question_raw: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000 m on a straight road. At time t=0 it starts from rest. At time t=10 seconds its velocity is 20m/s. What is the average acceleration in m/ {tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Average acceleration is the change in velocity divided by an elapsed time.
Initial velocity, u = 0 m/s
Final velocity, v = 20 m/s
Time elapsed = 10-0 = 10 s
Average acceleration, $a_{avg} = {change \ in \ veloctiy \over time \ elapsed}$ $= {20-0\over 10}= 2\ m/s^2$

questions.question: With reference to the figure which shows a particle moving along a straight line, y axis represents the position and x axis represents time. If x_1 = 18m, the average velocity at if x_1 in m/s is

Total displacement, d = 18 m
Time taken t = 1 s
average velocity, $v_{avg}={Total \ displacement \over time \ taken }$
$= {18\over 1} = 18 \ m/s$

questions.question: A bus starts from rest with an acceleration of 1 m/sec^2. A man who is 48 meter behind the bus with a uniform velocity of 10 m/sec. Then the minimum time after which the man will catch the bus is
questions.question_raw: A bus starts from rest with an acceleration of 1 m/sec2. A man who is 48 meter behind the bus with a uniform velocity of 10 m/sec. Then the minimum time after which the man will catch the bus is
questions.explanation:
Let the distance traveled by the bus when man catches the bus be S meter. Distance traveled by the man when he catch the bus, S' = S+48 meter
Given: Initial velocity of bus u = 0
Initial velocity of man, u' = 10m/sec
Acceleration of Bus = 1 m/s^2 
Acceleration of Man = 0
We will use the formula for displacement i.e.,
$S = ut+{1\over 2}at^2$
Displacement of Bus, $S= {1\over 2}t^2 \ ......(1)$
Displacement of Man, $S+48=10t \ ....... (2)$
By substituting the value of S from eq(1) in eq(2) we get
${1\over 2}t^2+48=10 t $
$\Rightarrow t^2-20t+96=0$
$\Rightarrow t^2-12t-8t+96=0$
$\Rightarrow (t-8)(t-12)=0$
 $\Rightarrow$ t = 8, 12
Taking minimum value we have t = 8 sec.

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone reaches its maximum height. (Take g = 9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone reaches its maximum height. (Take g = 9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by , u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height it will stop, so final velocity is given by , v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity g = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2
Time taken to reach maximum height is = t
We know that, v = u + at
$\Rightarrow$ $0 = 20 +(-9.8)t$
$\Rightarrow$$​​ t = {-20\over -9.8}=2.04\ s$ [g is taken as negative because it is in the opposite direction of motion]

questions.question: Motion along a straight line is called __________.
questions.question_raw: Motion along a straight line is called __________.
questions.explanation: Rectilinear motion is another name for straight-line motion. This type of motion describes the movement of a particle or a body. A body is said to experience rectilinear motion if any two particles of the body travel the same distance along two parallel straight lines.

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 60 m/s. What is its acceleration if it stops in 2.0 s?
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 60 m/s. What is its acceleration if it stops in 2.0 s?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 60 m/s
As it stops then final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Time taken t = 2.0 s
We know, v - u = at
$\Rightarrow$ 0 - 60 = 2a
$\Rightarrow$ a = ${-60\over 2}= -30 \ m/s^2$
-ve sign shows velocity is decreasing.

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 30 m/s. What is its acceleration if it stops in 2.0 s?
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 30 m/s. What is its acceleration if it stops in 2.0 s?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 30 m/s
As it stops then final velocity v = 0 m/s
Time taken t = 2.0 s
We know, v - u = at
$\Rightarrow$ 0 - 30= 2a
$\Rightarrow$ a = ${-30\over 2}= -15 \ m/s^2$
$​​-$ve sign shows velocity is decreasing.

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 60 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 60 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 60 m/s
As it stops so final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Time taken t = 2 seconds
We know, v - u = at
$\Rightarrow a = {v-u\over t}\ \ .....(1)$
Also, $s = ut+{1\over 2}at^2$
From (1), we have
$s = ut+{1\over 2}({v-u\over t})t^2$
$\Rightarrow s = ut+{1\over 2}({v-u})t$
After putting given values, we have 
$\Rightarrow s = (60\times 2)+{1\over 2}(0-60)\times 2$
$\Rightarrow$ s = 120-60 = 60 m

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 30 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?

questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u, = 30 m/s
As it stop so final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Time t = 2 s
Distance covered = s
we know that ,v= u +at
0= 30 + a(2)
a= - 15m/s^2
We know, s = $ut+{1\over 2}at^2$
$\Rightarrow s = 30\times 2+{1\over 2}(-15)\times2^2$
$\Rightarrow$ s = 30 m

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone reaches its maximum height. (Take g = 9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone reaches its maximum height. (Take g = 9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by , u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height it will stop, so final velocity is given by , v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity g = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2
Time taken to reach maximum height is = t
We know that, v = u + at
$\Rightarrow$ $0 = 20 +(-9.8)t$
$\Rightarrow$$​​ t = {-20\over -9.8}=2.04\ s$ [g is taken as negative because it is in the opposite direction of motion]

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the maximum height it travels in meters. (Take g = 9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the maximum height it travels in meters. (Take g = 9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height stone will be stopped, So final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity, a = g = -9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 (-ve sign show the opposite direction of motion)
Let maximum height = s
We know, $v^2-u^2=2as$
$\Rightarrow$ $0^2-(20)^2= 2\times(- 9.8)s$
$\Rightarrow -400= -19.6s$
$\Rightarrow s = {-400\over -19.6}= 20.4\ m$

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone returns to the height from which it was thrown. g = 9.8 m/sec^2.
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone returns to the height from which it was thrown. g = 9.8 m/sec2.
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height stone will be stopped, 
So final velocity v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity a = g = -9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 (-ve Because it is in opposite direction of motion)
Let maximum height = s
We know,
v^2 − u^2 = 2as
$\Rightarrow$ 0^2 − (20)^2 = 2 $\times$ ( − 9.8)s
$\Rightarrow$ − 400 = − 19.6s
$\Rightarrow s = {-400\over -19.6}$ = 20.4 m
Time to cover this distance upward is given by 
$\Rightarrow$ v − u = at
$\Rightarrow$ 0-20 = (-9.8)t
$\Rightarrow$ t = ${-20\over -9.8}$ = 2.04 sec 
Again to reach the same level from where it has been thrown it has to cover the same distance downward.
For this initial velocity u = 0 
s = 20.4 m
a = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 (+ve because motion is in same direction).
So,
$\Rightarrow$ 20.4 = 0 $\times$ t + $1\over 2$ $\times$ 9.8t^2
$\Rightarrow t^2 = {20.4\over 4.9}= 4.16$ 
$\Rightarrow t =\sqrt {4.16}$ = 2.04 
Total time = 2.04 + 2.04 = 4.08 s

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a 50 m tall building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the velocity in m/sec when the stone returns to the height from which it was thrown. g = 9.8 m/sec^2.
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a 50 m tall building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the velocity in m/sec when the stone returns to the height from which it was thrown. g = 9.8 m/sec2.
questions.explanation:
t_A = 0
y_A = 50m
v_A = 20$\frac{m}{s}$
a = -g = -9.80$\frac{m}{s^2}$ 
(v_C)^2 − (vA)^2 = 2a(y_C− y_A) 
With y_C = y_A we get
(VC)^2 = (v_A)^2
$\Rightarrow$ v_C = $\pm$v_A
As the motion of the stone is downward, and the”+”sign was assigned for the upward motion,
we get for v_ C = −v_A= −20 m/s.

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. The height of the building is 50.0 m. Determine the velocity in m/sec when the stone hits the ground. (g =9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. The height of the building is 50.0 m. Determine the velocity in m/sec when the stone hits the ground. (g =9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by u = 20 m/s
Final velocity before hitting ground is given by = v
Distance covered is, s = 50 m
Acceleration due to gravity, a = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2
We know that, $v^2-u^2= 2as$
$\Rightarrow v^2-(-20)^2= 2\times 9.8\times 50$
$\Rightarrow v^2-400= 980$
$\Rightarrow v^2= 1380$
$\Rightarrow v= \sqrt {1380}= \pm 37.1$
As this velocity is in the opposite direction, so v = - 37.1 m/s

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/sec? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north.
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/sec? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north.
questions.explanation:
Velocity of A is given by , $v_A = +54\ km h^{–1} = + 15\ ms^{–1 }$
Velocity of B is given by , $v_B = ~– 90\ km h^{–1} =\ – 25\ m s^{–1 }$
Relative velocity of B with respect to A, $v_{BA}= v_B – v_A $$=-25-15 =\ – 40\ m s^{–1 }$
i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 40 ms^–1 from north to south.

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 27 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 45 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to north:
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 27 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 45 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to north:
questions.explanation:
The velocity of A is given by, v_A = +27 kmh^–1 = +7.5 ms^–1
The velocity of B is given by , v_B = -45 kmh^–1 = -12.5 ms^–1
The relative velocity of B with respect to A is given by, v_BA = v_B – v_A = −7.5−12.5 = –20 ms^–1
i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 20 ms^–1 from north to south.

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of ground with respect to B in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north.
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of ground with respect to B in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north.
questions.explanation:
Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north. 
Then, v_A = +54 km h^–1= +15 ms^–1
v_B= –90 km h^–1= –25 ms^–1
The relative velocity of B with respect to A = v_B – v_A = -25 − (15) = -40 ms^–1, i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 40 ms^–1 from north to south.
The relative velocity of ground with respect to B = 0 – v_B = 0 − (−25) = 25ms^–1.

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground? questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from the south to north.
questions.explanation:
Let the velocity of the monkey with respect to the ground be v_M. The relative velocity of the monkey with respect to A,
$v_{M A}=v_{M}-v_{A}=-18 k m h^{-1}=-5 m s^{-1}$
Therefore velocity of monkey will be given by , $v_{M}=(15-5) m s^{-1}=10 m s^{-1}$ [$\because v_{A}=+54 k m h^{-1}=15 m s^{-1}$]

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground in m/s ? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground in m/s ? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north
questions.explanation:
Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north. 
Then, v_A = +54 km h^-1 = +15 ms^-1
v_B = -90 km h^-1 = - 25 ms^-1
The relative velocity of B with respect to A
= v_B - v_A = (-25) -15= -40 ms^-1, i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 40 ms^-1 from north to south.
The relative velocity of ground with respect to B = 0 - v_B = 0 - (-25) = 25ms^-1
Now, let the velocity of the monkey with respect to the ground be v_M. 
The relative velocity of the monkey with respect to A,
v_MA = v_M - v_A = 18 -54 kmh^-1 = - 10ms^-1.

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 63 m/s. What is its acceleration in ${\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ if it stops in 2.0 s?
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 63 m/s. What is its acceleration in {tex}{\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} if it stops in 2.0 s?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 63 m/s
As it stops, so final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Time t = 2.0 s
We know that, v - u = at
|$\Rightarrow$ $a = {v-u\over t}$
$\Rightarrow$ $ a = {0-63\over 2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $ a = -31.5\ m/s^2$

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 63 m/s. What is the displacement of the plane in m while it stopped in 2 s:
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 63 m/s. What is the displacement of the plane in m while it stopped in 2 s:
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u = 63 m/s
At it stops final velocity v = 0 m/s
Time taken t = 2 s
v = u+ at
0 = 63 + 2a
a = -31.5 m/s^2
We know,
$s = ut +{1\over 2}at^2$
s = 63 $\times$2 + 1/2 $\times$ (-31.5)$\times$ 2^2
s = 63 m

questions.question: A car traveling at a constant speed of 45.0 m/s passes a trooper hidden behind a billboard. One second after the speeding car passes the billboard; the trooper sets out from the billboard to catch it, accelerating at a constant rate of 3 m/s^2. How long does it take her to overtake the car?
questions.question_raw: A car traveling at a constant speed of 45.0 m/s passes a trooper hidden behind a billboard. One second after the speeding car passes the billboard; the trooper sets out from the billboard to catch it, accelerating at a constant rate of 3 m/s2. How long does it take her to overtake the car?
questions.explanation:
The initial speed of the car u = 45 m/s
Let t = 0 s when the car passes the trooper.
The trooper starts from rest 1 s after the car passes the billboard.
In this 1 s the car would have covered a distance of 45 m.
Let y be the time at which the trooper overtakes the car.
Distance covered by car at time y
S = 45 + uy = 45 + 45y......(1)
(The distance is measured from the billboard)
The same distance is covered by the trooper also.
S = 0 + $1\over 2$ $\times$ 3 $\times$(y)^2....(2)
(Initial speed of trooper = 0).
Equating (1) and (2)
45 + 45y = $3\over 2$(y)^2
$\Rightarrow$ 3y^2 - 90y - 90 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ y^2 - 30y - 30 = 0
Using quadratic formula, we get:
$\Rightarrow$ $ {30 ± \sqrt {900+120} \over 2}$ = ${30 \pm 31.93\over 2 }$ = 30.97 s $\approx$ 31 s 
neglected negative value of y.

questions.question: Jules Verne in 1865 proposed sending people to the Moon by firing a space capsule from 220-m-long cannon with a final velocity of 10.97 km/s. What would have been the unrealistically large acceleration experienced by the space travelers during launch?
questions.question_raw: Jules Verne in 1865 proposed sending people to the Moon by firing a space capsule from 220-m-long cannon with a final velocity of 10.97 km/s. What would have been the unrealistically large acceleration experienced by the space travelers during launch?
questions.explanation:
We know the equation of motion
v^2 − u^2 = 2as 
Given: v = 10.97 km/s = 10.97 $\times$ 10^3 m/s
u = 0
S = 220 m
Acceleration $a = {v^2 - u^2 \over 2s}$ =$ (10.97 \times 10^3 )^2- (0)^2\over 2 \times 220 $ = $ (10.97 \times 10^3 )^2\over 440 $ 
= 2.74 $\times$ 10^5 m/s^2
