questions.question: Find the angle between the force ${\bf{F}} = 3{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} - 5{\bf{\hat k}}$ and displacement ${\bf{d}} = 5{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} + 3{\bf{\hat k}}$.
questions.question_raw: Find the angle between the force {tex}{\bf{F}} = 3{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} - 5{\bf{\hat k}}{/tex} and displacement {tex}{\bf{d}} = 5{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} + 3{\bf{\hat k}}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
$W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$
$\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$ = Fd cos$ \theta$
$\cos \theta$ = $\frac{\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}}{F d}$ = $\frac{(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}+(-5)^{2}} \times \sqrt{5^{2}+4^{2}+3^{2}}}$ $=\frac{15+16-15}{\sqrt{50} \times \sqrt{50}}=\frac{16}{50}=\frac{8}{25}$
$\theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{25}\right)$ = 71^o

questions.question: Find the projection of F on d, if force ${\bf{F}} = 3{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} - 5{\bf{\hat k}}$ and displacement ${\bf{d}} = 5{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} + 3{\bf{\hat k}}$
questions.question_raw: Find the projection of F on d, if force {tex}{\bf{F}} = 3{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} - 5{\bf{\hat k}}{/tex} and displacement {tex}{\bf{d}} = 5{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} + 3{\bf{\hat k}}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
The vector projection of a vector $\vec A$ on a nonzero vector $\vec B$ is also known as the vector component $\vec A$ in the direction of $\vec B$ and is equal to $\left( {\vec A.\hat B} \right).\hat B$ 
Here projection of $\vec F$ on vector $\vec d$ = $\left( {\vec F.\hat d} \right).\hat d $ = $(\vec{F} \cdot \hat{d}) \cdot \hat{d}=\left[(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot \frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{5^2+4^2+3^2}}\right] \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{5^2+4^2+3^2}}\right]$
$=\left[(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot \frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right] \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right]$
$=\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{50}}(15+16-15)\right] \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right]$
$=\frac{16}{\sqrt{50}} \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right]=\frac{8}{25}(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$

questions.question: Balram exerts a steady force of magnitude 150 N on the stalled car as shown in the figure below; he pushes it a distance of 20 m. The car also has a flat tyre, so to make the car move straight. He pushes at an angle of 30^o to the direction of motion. How much work does he do?
questions.question_raw: Balram exerts a steady force of magnitude 150 N on the stalled car as shown in the figure below; he pushes it a distance of 20 m. The car also has a flat tyre, so to make the car move straight. He pushes at an angle of 30o to the direction of motion. How much work does he do?
questions.explanation: W = Fs cos$ \theta$ = $ 150 \times 20 \times {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2} $ = 2598J

questions.question: Balram exerts a steady force of magnitude F = 200${\bf{\hat i}}$+ 2.0$\hat j$ on the stalled car as shown in the figure above, he pushes it a distance s = 10${\bf{\hat i}}$ + 10$\hat j$ m. How much work does he do?
questions.question_raw: Balram exerts a steady force of magnitude F = 200{tex}{\bf{\hat i}}{/tex}+ 2.0{tex}\hat j{/tex} on the stalled car as shown in the figure above, he pushes it a distance s = 10{tex}{\bf{\hat i}}{/tex} + 10{tex}\hat j{/tex} m. How much work does he do?
questions.explanation:
$\vec{F}=200 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}$
$\vec{s}=10 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j}$
$W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}=(200 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}) \cdot(10 \hat{i}+10 \hat{j})$
W = 2000 + 20 = 2020 J

questions.question: A railway porter is holding a weight of 100 kgs on his head and is standing still. What is the work done by him?
questions.question_raw: A railway porter is holding a weight of 100 kgs on his head and is standing still. What is the work done by him?
questions.explanation:
W = Force $\times$ displacement in the direction of the force
Work done will be zero because porter is stationary (i.e. displacement is zero)

questions.question: Balram pushes 500kg weight on a horizontal frictionless surface a distance of 10 m. The work done by the gravitational force is:
questions.question_raw: Balram pushes 500kg weight on a horizontal frictionless surface a distance of 10 m. The work done by the gravitational force is:
questions.explanation:
the angle between force and displacement is 90^o
W = Fscos90^o = 0

questions.question: Weight of 20kg falls from a height of 10 m. The work done by the gravitational force is (Take g = 10 ms^−2)
questions.question_raw: Weight of 20kg falls from a height of 10 m. The work done by the gravitational force is (Take g = 10 ms−2)
questions.explanation:
W = Fscos$\theta$ = mghcos0^o
W = 20 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 1 = 2000 J

questions.question: A raindrop of mass 2.00 g falls from a height of 1.00 km. It hits the ground with a speed of 50.0 m/s. What is the work done by the gravitational force? (Take g = 10 ms^−2)
questions.question_raw: A raindrop of mass 2.00 g falls from a height of 1.00 km. It hits the ground with a speed of 50.0 m/s. What is the work done by the gravitational force? (Take g = 10 ms−2)
questions.explanation:
W = Fscos$\theta$ = mghcos0^o
W = 2 $\times$ 10^ − 3 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 1000 $\times$ 1 = 20J

questions.question: A raindrop of mass 2.00 g falls from a height of 1.00 km. It hits the ground with a speed of 50.0 m/s. What is the work done by the resistive forces if any? (Take g = 10 ms^−2)
questions.question_raw: A raindrop of mass 2.00 g falls from a height of 1.00 km. It hits the ground with a speed of 50.0 m/s. What is the work done by the resistive forces if any? (Take g = 10 ms−2)
questions.explanation:
From work kinetic energy theorem
$\Delta$K = W_mg + W_air
W_mg = Fscos$\theta$ = mghcos0^o
W_mg = 2 $\times$ 10 ^- 3 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 1000 $\times$ 1 = 20J
$1\over2$ $\times$2 $\times$ 10^ - 3(50^2 − 0) = 20 + W_air
2.5 = 20 + W_air
W_air = − 17.5J

questions.question: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal frictionless surface by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.question_raw: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal frictionless surface by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.explanation:
From work kinetic energy theorem
K.E. = $\frac{1}{2}$m v^2
$\Delta$K = W
$\frac{1}{2}$mv^2 - $\frac{1}{2}$mu^2 = Fs
$\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times v^{2}\right)$ − 0 = 12 $\times$ 3
3 v^2 = 36
v^2 = 12
v = 3.5m/sec

questions.question: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal, surface having a coefficient of kinetic friction of 0.15, by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.question_raw: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal, surface having a coefficient of kinetic friction of 0.15, by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta$K = W
F = 12N
f = $\mu$R = $\mu$mg = 0.15 $\times$ 6 $\times$ 9.8 = 8.82N
F_net = F - f = 12 - 8.82 = 3.18N
1/2mv^2 - 1/2mu^2 = W
1/2mv^2-0 = F_net x $x$
$\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times v^{2}\right)$ = 3.18 $\times$ 3
3v^2 = 3.18 $\times$ 3
v^2 = 3.18v = 1.8m/sec

questions.question: A pump on the ground floor of a building can pump up water to fill a tank of volume 30m^3 in 15 min. If the tank is 40 m above the ground, and the efficiency of the pump is 30%, how much electric power is consumed by the pump?
questions.question_raw: A pump on the ground floor of a building can pump up water to fill a tank of volume 30m3 in 15 min. If the tank is 40 m above the ground, and the efficiency of the pump is 30%, how much electric power is consumed by the pump?
questions.explanation:
P = $W\over t$
W = mgh
30m^3 = 30000 = 3 $\times$ 10^4 lit
mass of 30000 lit water = 30000 Kg
$P = {{mgh} \over t} = {{3 \times {{10}^4} \times 9.8 \times 40} \over {15 \times 60}} = 1.30 \times {10^4}W$ 
efficiency = power output / power consumption
$\eta=\frac{P}{P_{\mathrm{c}}}$
$P_{c}=\frac{P}{\eta}=\frac{1.30 \times 10^{4}}{0.3}$ = 4.33 × 10^4 = 43.3KW.

questions.question: The bob of a pendulum is released from a horizontal position. If the length of the pendulum is 1.5 m, what is the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowermost point, given that it dissipated 5$\% $ of its initial energy against air resistance?
questions.question_raw: The bob of a pendulum is released from a horizontal position. If the length of the pendulum is 1.5 m, what is the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowermost point, given that it dissipated 5{tex}\% {/tex} of its initial energy against air resistance?
questions.explanation:
95% of potential energy is converted into kinetic energy.
applying conservation of mechanical energy between horizontal and lowermost points
$mgl \times {{95} \over {100}} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
$gl \times {{95} \over {100}} = {1 \over 2}{v^2}$
v = $\sqrt {{{2 \times gl \times 95} \over {100}}}$ = $\sqrt {{{2 \times 9.8 \times 1.5 \times 95} \over {100}}}$ = 5.3m/s

questions.question: Consider a collision between two pool balls. Ball 1 is at rest and ball 2 is moving towards it with a speed of 2 ms^-1. The mass of each ball is 0.3 kg. After the balls collide elastically, ball 2 comes to an immediate stop and ball 1 move off. What is the final velocity of ball 1?
questions.question_raw: Consider a collision between two pool balls. Ball 1 is at rest and ball 2 is moving towards it with a speed of 2 ms-1. The mass of each ball is 0.3 kg. After the balls collide elastically, ball 2 comes to an immediate stop and ball 1 move off. What is the final velocity of ball 1?
questions.explanation:
Inelastic collision (e = 1) if the mass of colliding bodies is the same then their velocities after collision interchanged.
m_1 = m_2 = m
u_1 = 0
u_2 = 2m/s
${v_1} = \left( {{{{m_1} - {m_2}} \over {{m_1} + {m_2}}}} \right){u_1} + \left( {{{2{m_2}} \over {{m_1} + {m_2}}}} \right){u_2} $ = 2m/s
${v_2} = \left( {{{2{m_1}} \over {{m_1} + {m_2}}}} \right){u_1} + \left( {{{{m_2} - {m_1}} \over {{m_1} - {m_2}}}} \right){u_2} $ = 0

questions.question: Consider two 2 marbles. Marble 1 has mass 100 g and marble 2 has mass 50 g. Edward rolls marble 1 along the ground towards marble 2 in the positive x-direction. Marble 2 is initially at rest and marble 1 has a velocity of 3 m /s in the positive x-direction. After they collide elastically, both marbles are moving. What is the final velocity of each marble?
questions.question_raw: Consider two 2 marbles. Marble 1 has mass 100 g and marble 2 has mass 50 g. Edward rolls marble 1 along the ground towards marble 2 in the positive x-direction. Marble 2 is initially at rest and marble 1 has a velocity of 3 m /s in the positive x-direction. After they collide elastically, both marbles are moving. What is the final velocity of each marble?

options.label: A
options.text: ball 1 moves to the right at 2 m /s and ball 2 moves to the left with a velocity of 2m /s

options.label: B
options.text: ball 1 moves to the right at 5 m /s and ball 2 moves to the left with a velocity of 3m /s

options.label: C
options.text: ball 1 moves to the right with a velocity of 1m/s and ball 2 also moves to the right at 4 m/s

options.label: D
options.text: ball 1 moves to the right at 3 m /s and ball 2 moves to the left with a velocity of 2m /s

correct_option_texts: ball 1 moves to the right with a velocity of 1m/s and ball 2 also moves to the right at 4 m/s

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Work Energy and Power

questions.question: Two billiard balls each with a mass of 150g collide head-on in an elastic collision. Ball 1 was travelling at a speed of 2 m /s and ball 2 at a speed of 1.5 m /s. After the collision, ball 1 travels away from ball 2 at a velocity of 1.5 m /s .What is the velocity of ball 2?
questions.question_raw: Two billiard balls each with a mass of 150g collide head-on in an elastic collision. Ball 1 was travelling at a speed of 2 m /s and ball 2 at a speed of 1.5 m /s. After the collision, ball 1 travels away from ball 2 at a velocity of 1.5 m /s .What is the velocity of ball 2?

options.label: A
options.text: ball 2 moves with a velocity of 2.5 m /s

options.label: B
options.text: ball 2 moves with a velocity of 2 m/s

options.label: C
options.text: ball 2 moves with a velocity of 3.5 m/s

options.label: D
options.text: ball 2 moves with a velocity of 3.7 m/s

correct_option_texts: ball 2 moves with a velocity of 2 m/s
questions.explanation:
In the elastic collision (e = 1) if the mass of colliding bodies is the same then their velocities after collision interchanged.
m_1 = 150gm
m_2 = 150gm
u_1 = 2m/s
u_2 = −1.5m/s
$v_{1}=\left(\frac{m_{1}-m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right) u_{1}+\left(\frac{2 m_{2}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right) u_{2}$ = −u_2 = −1.5m/s
v_2 = $\left(\frac{2 m_{1}}{m_{1}+m_{2}}\right) u_{1}+\left(\frac{m_{2}-m_{1}}{m_{1}-m_{2}}\right) u_{2}$ = u_1 = 2 m/s
so that ball 2 moves with a velocity of 2 m/s

questions.question: Consider the collision of two cars. Car 1 is at rest and Car 2 is moving at a speed of 2 m /s in the negative x-direction. Both cars each have a mass of 500 kg. The cars collide inelastically and stick together. What is the resulting velocity of the resulting mass of metal?
questions.question_raw: Consider the collision of two cars. Car 1 is at rest and Car 2 is moving at a speed of 2 m /s in the negative x-direction. Both cars each have a mass of 500 kg. The cars collide inelastically and stick together. What is the resulting velocity of the resulting mass of metal?
questions.explanation:
from conservation of linear momentum initial momentum = final momentum
$\vec{p}_{i}=\vec{p}_{f}$
(500 $\times$ 0) + [500 $\times$ (−2)] = (500 + 500)v
- 1000 = 1000v
v = −1m/s negative sign indicates that cars move to the left.

questions.question: A body of mass 0.5 kg travels in a straight line with velocity v = $a x^{\frac 3 2}$ where a = $5 m^{\frac {−1}{2}}s^{−1}$. What is the work done by the net force during its displacement from x = 0 to x = 2 m is:
questions.question_raw: A body of mass 0.5 kg travels in a straight line with velocity v = {tex}a x^{\frac 3 2}{/tex} where a = {tex}5 m^{\frac {−1}{2}}s^{−1}{/tex}. What is the work done by the net force during its displacement from x = 0 to x = 2 m is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of the body, m = 0.5 kg
The velocity of the body v = $ax^{\frac 3 2}$
a = 5 $m^{\frac {-1}{2}}$s^-1
Initial velocity at x = 0 is u = 0
Final velocity at x = 2 m is v = 10$\sqrt2$ m/s
work done = Change in kinetic energy
W = K_f - K_i
W =$\frac 12$mv^2 - 0
W = $\frac 12 \times$ 0.5 $\times$ (10$\sqrt 2$)^2 = $\frac 12 \times$0.5 $\times$ 200 = 50J

questions.question: A person trying to lose weight (dieter) lifts a 10 kg mass, one thousand times, to a height of 0.5 m each time. Assume that the potential energy lost each time she lowers the mass is dissipated. How much work does she do against the gravitational force?
questions.question_raw: A person trying to lose weight (dieter) lifts a 10 kg mass, one thousand times, to a height of 0.5 m each time. Assume that the potential energy lost each time she lowers the mass is dissipated. How much work does she do against the gravitational force?
questions.explanation:
Work done by force applied against gravity for one lift will be
W = Fs = mgh = 10 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 0.5 = 49J
So work done for 1000 lifts = 49 $\times$ 1000 = 49000 J

questions.question: A person trying to lose weight (dieter) lifts a 10 kg mass, one thousand times, to a height of 0.5 m each time. Assume that the potential energy lost each time she lowers the mass is dissipated. Fat supplies 3.8 ${\times }$ 10^7 J of energy per kilogram which is converted to mechanical energy with a 20% efficiency rate. How much fat will the dieter use up?
questions.question_raw: A person trying to lose weight (dieter) lifts a 10 kg mass, one thousand times, to a height of 0.5 m each time. Assume that the potential energy lost each time she lowers the mass is dissipated. Fat supplies 3.8 {tex}{\times }{/tex} 107 J of energy per kilogram which is converted to mechanical energy with a 20% efficiency rate. How much fat will the dieter use up?
questions.explanation:
Work done by force applied against gravity for one lift will be
W = Fs = mgh = 10 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 0.5 = 49J
So work done for 1000 lifts = 49 $\times$ 1000 = 49000 J
efficiency = $Work \ done \over energy \ from \ fat$
energy from fat used = $Work \ done \over efficiency$
E = $ {W \over \eta } = {{49000} \over {0.2}} $ = 245000J
Energy from per kilogram fat = 3.8 ${\times }$ 10^7 J
Fat used $ = {{245000} \over {3.8 \times {{10}^7}}} $ = 64473 $\times$ 10^-7 = 6.45 $\times$ 10^-3 Kg

questions.question: The Sun converts an enormous amount of matter to energy. Each second, 4.19 ${\times }$ 10^9 kg-approximately the capacity of 400 average-sized cargo ships-is changed to energy. What is the power output of the Sun?
questions.question_raw: The Sun converts an enormous amount of matter to energy. Each second, 4.19 {tex}{\times }{/tex} 109 kg-approximately the capacity of 400 average-sized cargo ships-is changed to energy. What is the power output of the Sun?
questions.explanation:
The energy liberated per second 
E = mc^2 = 4.19 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 10^8 $\times$3 $\times$ 10^8 = 37.71 $\times$ 10^25 J
power output of the sun is equal to energy output per second 
P = ${W \over t} = {{37.71 \times {{10}^{25}}} \over 1} $ = 3.77 $\times$ 10^26 W

questions.question: A block having a mass of 0.80 kg is given an initial velocity of 1.2 m/s to the right and collides with a spring of negligible mass and force constant k = 50 N/m. Assuming the surface to be frictionless, calculate the maximum compression of the spring after the collision.
questions.question_raw: A block having a mass of 0.80 kg is given an initial velocity of 1.2 m/s to the right and collides with a spring of negligible mass and force constant k = 50 N/m. Assuming the surface to be frictionless, calculate the maximum compression of the spring after the collision.
questions.explanation:
for maximum compression 
${1 \over 2}k{x^2} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
x = $ \sqrt {{{m{v^2}} \over k}} = \sqrt {{{0.8 \times 1.2 \times 1.2} \over {50}}} $ = 0.15m
the maximum compression of the spring after the collision = 0.15m.

questions.question: The launching mechanism of a toy gun consists of a spring of unknown spring constant. When the spring is compressed 0.120 m, the gun, when fired vertically, is able to launch a 35.0-g projectile to a maximum height of 20.0 m above the position of the projectile before firing. Neglecting all resistive forces, determine the spring constant.
questions.question_raw: The launching mechanism of a toy gun consists of a spring of unknown spring constant. When the spring is compressed 0.120 m, the gun, when fired vertically, is able to launch a 35.0-g projectile to a maximum height of 20.0 m above the position of the projectile before firing. Neglecting all resistive forces, determine the spring constant.
questions.explanation:
The potential energy of spring converted into potential energy
${1 \over 2}k{x^2} $ = mgh
k = ${{2mgh} \over {{x^2}}}$ =$ {{2 \times 35 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 9.8 \times 20} \over {0.12 \times 0.12}} $ = 953N/m

questions.question: A 3.00-kg crate slides down a ramp. The ramp is 1.00 m in length and inclined at an angle of 30.0^o. The crate starts from rest at the top, experiences a constant frictional force of magnitude 5.00 N, and continues to move a short distance on the flat floor after it leaves the ramp. Use energy methods to determine the speed of the crate at the bottom of the ramp?
questions.question_raw: A 3.00-kg crate slides down a ramp. The ramp is 1.00 m in length and inclined at an angle of 30.0o. The crate starts from rest at the top, experiences a constant frictional force of magnitude 5.00 N, and continues to move a short distance on the flat floor after it leaves the ramp. Use energy methods to determine the speed of the crate at the bottom of the ramp?
questions.explanation:
Height of ramp 
h = lsin30^o = 1 $\times$ 1/2 = 0.5m
from work kinetic energy theorem
K_f - K_i = W_mg + W_f
$1\over 2$mv^2 - 0 = mgh + fd
$1\over 2$ $\times$ 3v^2 = (3 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$0.5) + [5 $\times$ ( - 1)]
$\frac{3}{2}$v^2 = 14.7 - 5
v^2 = 9.7 $\times$ $\frac{2}{3}$
v = $\sqrt{\frac{9.7 \times 2}{3}}$ = 2.54m/s

questions.question: A bullet of mass 0.012 kg and horizontal speed 70 m ${\rm{s}}{-^{\rm{1}}}$ strikes a block of wood of mass 0.4 kg and instantly comes to rest with respect to the block. The block is suspended from the ceiling by means of thin wires. Calculate the height to which the block rises. Also, estimate the amount of heat produced in the bloc?.
questions.question_raw: A bullet of mass 0.012 kg and horizontal speed 70 m {tex}{\rm{s}}{-^{\rm{1}}}{/tex} strikes a block of wood of mass 0.4 kg and instantly comes to rest with respect to the block. The block is suspended from the ceiling by means of thin wires. Calculate the height to which the block rises. Also, estimate the amount of heat produced in the bloc?.
questions.explanation:
mass of bullet m = 0.012 kg
mass of block = 0.4 kg
The initial speed of a bullet, u =70m/s
Let the final speed of block and bullet which move together = v m/s
According to the law of conservation of momentum,
$v\sqrt{\frac{9.7 \times 2}{3}}$
v =$ {{mu} \over {M + m}} = {{0.012 \times 70} \over {0.412}} $ = 2.04m/s
Let block rises to a height h after the collision, then
Loss in K.E. of block and bullet = Gain in P.E. after the collision.
${1 \over 2}\left( {m + M} \right){v^2} $ = (m + M)gh
h = $ {{{v^2}} \over {2g}} = {{2.04 \times 2.04} \over {2 \times 9.8}} $ = 0.212m = 21.2 cm
Heat produced in the block = loss of K.E. = K.E. of bullet - K.E. of block and bullet
Heat produced in the block = ${1 \over 2}m{u^2} - {1 \over 2}\left( {m + M} \right){v^2}$ = $ \left( {{1 \over 2} \times 0.012 \times 70 \times 70} \right) - \left( {{1 \over 2} \times 0.412 \times 2.04 \times 2.04} \right) $ = 28.5J

questions.question: A 1 kg block situated on a rough incline is connected to a spring of spring constant 100 Nm^-1 as shown in Figure. The block is released from rest with the spring in the unstretched position. The block moves 10 cm down the incline before coming to rest. Find the coefficient of friction between the block and the incline. Assume that the spring has a negligible mass and the pulley is frictionless.
questions.question_raw: A 1 kg block situated on a rough incline is connected to a spring of spring constant 100 Nm-1 as shown in Figure. The block is released from rest with the spring in the unstretched position. The block moves 10 cm down the incline before coming to rest. Find the coefficient of friction between the block and the incline. Assume that the spring has a negligible mass and the pulley is frictionless.
questions.explanation:
The weight of the block can be resolved in two components.
the component parallel to incline plane (mgsin37^o)and a component perpendicular to the plane (mgcos37^o) 
at equilibrium
R = mg cos 37^o 
f = $\mu$R = $\mu$mg cos 37
The net force acting on the block = mgsin37^o - f 
= mgsin37^o − $\mu $mgcos37^o 
At equilibrium, the work done by the block is equal to the potential energy of the spring, i.e.,
= (mg sin 37^o - $\mu$mg cos 37^o) $x$ = $\frac{1}{2}$kx^2 
$(1 \times 9.8 \times 0.6)-(\mu \times 1 \times 9.8 \times 0.8)$ $=\frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 0.1$
5.88 - 7.84$\mu$ = 5
$\mu=\frac{0.88}{0.784}$ = 0.112

questions.question: A bolt of mass 0.3 kg falls from the ceiling of an elevator moving down with a uniform speed of 7 m/s. It hits the floor of the elevator (length of the elevator = 3 m) and does not rebound. What is the heat produced by the impact?
questions.question_raw: A bolt of mass 0.3 kg falls from the ceiling of an elevator moving down with a uniform speed of 7 m/s. It hits the floor of the elevator (length of the elevator = 3 m) and does not rebound. What is the heat produced by the impact?
questions.explanation:
The whole of the potential energy of bolt converted into heat energy 
heat produced by the impact = mgh = 0.3 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 3 = 8.82J

questions.question: A trolley of mass 200 kg moves with a uniform speed of 36 km/h on a frictionless track. A child of mass 20 kg runs on the trolley from one end to the other (10 m away) with a speed of 4 ms^-1 relatives to the trolley in a direction opposite to its motion, and jumps out of the trolley. What is the final speed of the trolley?
questions.question_raw: A trolley of mass 200 kg moves with a uniform speed of 36 km/h on a frictionless track. A child of mass 20 kg runs on the trolley from one end to the other (10 m away) with a speed of 4 ms-1 relatives to the trolley in a direction opposite to its motion, and jumps out of the trolley. What is the final speed of the trolley?
questions.explanation:
Mass of trolley M = 200Kg
mass of child m = 20Kg
speed of trolley v = 36Km/hr = 36 $\times$ $5\over 18$ = 10m/s
Let v' be the final velocity of the trolley with respect to the ground.
The final velocity of the boy with respect to the ground = v' - 4
from conservation of linear momentum
p_i = p_f
(M + m)v = Mv' +m(v' − 4)
(200 + 20) $\times$ 10 = 200v' + 20(v' − 4)
2200 = 220v' - 80
= $2280\over 220$ = 10.36m/s

questions.question: A trolley of mass 200 kg moves with a uniform speed of 36 km/h on a frictionless track. A child of mass 20 kg runs on the trolley from one end to the other (10 m away) with a speed of 4 ms^−1 relatives to the trolley in a direction opposite to its motion, and jumps out of the trolley. How much has the trolley moved from the time the child begins to run?
questions.question_raw: A trolley of mass 200 kg moves with a uniform speed of 36 km/h on a frictionless track. A child of mass 20 kg runs on the trolley from one end to the other (10 m away) with a speed of 4 ms−1 relatives to the trolley in a direction opposite to its motion, and jumps out of the trolley. How much has the trolley moved from the time the child begins to run?
questions.explanation:
Mass of trolly M = 200Kg
mass of child m = 20Kg
speed of trolley v = 36Km/hr = 36 $\times$ $5\over 18$ = 10m/s
Let v' be the final velocity of the trolley with respect to the ground.
The final velocity of the boy with respect to the ground = v' - 4 
from conservation of linear momentum
p_i = p_f
(M + m)v = Mv' + m(v' − 4)
(200 + 20) $\times$ 10 = 200v' + 20(v' − 4)
2200 = 220v' - 80
v' = $2280\over 220$ = 10.36m/s
Time taken by the boy to run, t = $10\over 4$ = 2.5 sec
Distance moved by the trolley = v't = 10.36 $\times$ 2.5 = 25.9 m

questions.question: A 50.0-kg marathon runner runs up the stairs to the top of a 443-m-tall Tower. To lift herself to the top in 15.0 minutes, what must be her average power output?
questions.question_raw: A 50.0-kg marathon runner runs up the stairs to the top of a 443-m-tall Tower. To lift herself to the top in 15.0 minutes, what must be her average power output?
questions.explanation:
P = $ {W \over t}$
W = mgh = 50 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 443
t = 15 $\times$ 60sec
P = ${{50 \times 9.8 \times 443} \over {15 \times 60}} $ = 241 W.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Work Energy and Power

questions.question: A 75.0-kg painter climbs a ladder that is 2.75 m long leaning against a vertical wall. The ladder makes an angle of $30^\circ $ with the wall. How much work does gravity do on the painter?
questions.question_raw: A 75.0-kg painter climbs a ladder that is 2.75 m long leaning against a vertical wall. The ladder makes an angle of  {tex}30^\circ {/tex} with the wall. How much work does gravity do on the painter?
questions.explanation:
W = −mgh
h = $ x\cos 30^\circ = 2.75 \times {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}$
$W = - 75 \times 9.8 \times 2.75 \times {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2} $ = - 1750J

questions.question:
A 0.800-kg ball is tied to the end of a string 1.60 m long and swung in a vertical circle. (a) During one complete circle, starting anywhere, calculate the total work done on the ball by
 the tension in the string and
 gravity
questions.question_raw:
A 0.800-kg ball is tied to the end of a string 1.60 m long and swung in a vertical circle. (a) During one complete circle, starting anywhere, calculate the total work done on the ball by

  1. the tension in the string and
  2. gravity
questions.explanation:
Work done by the conservative force in round trip will be zero (displacement = 0).
So that work done by tension and gravity will be zero.

questions.question:
A 0.800-kg ball is tied to the end of a string 1.60 m long and swung in a vertical circle. Calculate the total work done on the ball by
 the tension in the string and
 gravity for motion along the semicircle from the lowest to the highest point on the path.
questions.question_raw:
A 0.800-kg ball is tied to the end of a string 1.60 m long and swung in a vertical circle. Calculate the total work done on the ball by

  1. the tension in the string and
  2. gravity for motion along the semicircle from the lowest to the highest point on the path.
questions.explanation:
work done by tension will be zero because tension is perpendicular to the displacement.
W = Tscos90^o = 0
work is done by gravity in a semicircle from the lowest to the highest point on the path
W = mghcos180^o = 0.8 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 3.2 $\times$ (−1) = −25.1J

questions.question: Adult cheetahs, the fastest of the great cats, have a mass of about 70 kg and have been clocked running at up to 72 mph (32 m/s). How many joules of kinetic energy does such a swift cheetah have?
questions.question_raw: Adult cheetahs, the fastest of the great cats, have a mass of about 70 kg and have been clocked running at up to 72 mph (32 m/s). How many joules of kinetic energy does such a swift cheetah have?
questions.explanation:
The kinetic energy of a cheetah 
K = $ {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
m = 70Kg
v = 32m/s
K = $\frac{1}{2}$ $\times$ 70 $\times$ 32 $\times$ 32 = 35840J

questions.question: A sled with mass 8.00 kg moves in a straight line on a frictionless horizontal surface. At one point in its path, its speed is 4.00 m/s; after it has traveled 2.50 m beyond this point, its speed is 6.00 m/s. Use the work-energy theorem to find the force acting on the sled, assuming that this force is constant and that it acts in the direction of the sled’s motion.
questions.question_raw: A sled with mass 8.00 kg moves in a straight line on a frictionless horizontal surface. At one point in its path, its speed is 4.00 m/s; after it has traveled 2.50 m beyond this point, its speed is 6.00 m/s. Use the work-energy theorem to find the force acting on the sled, assuming that this force is constant and that it acts in the direction of the sled’s motion.
questions.explanation:
from work-energy theorem
change in kinetic energy = work done
$\Delta K$ = W
K_f − K_i = F_s
${1 \over 2}m{v_f}^2 - {1 \over 2}m{v_i}^2 = Fs $
${1 \over 2}m\left( {{v_f}^2 - {v_i}^2} \right) = Fs$ 
m = 8Kg, s = 2.5m
v_f = 6m/s, v_i = 4m/s
$1 \over 2$ $\times$ 8(6^2 − 4^2) = F $\times$ 2.5
4 $\times$20 = F $\times$ 2.5
F = ${{80} \over {2.5}}$ = 32N

questions.question: A 12-pack of Omni-Cola (mass 4.30 kg) is initially at rest on a horizontal floor. It is then pushed in a straight line for 1.20 m by a trained dog that exerts a horizontal force with magnitude 36.0 N. Use the work-energy theorem to find the final speed of the 12-pack if there is no friction between the 12-pack and the floor.
questions.question_raw: A 12-pack of Omni-Cola (mass 4.30 kg) is initially at rest on a horizontal floor. It is then pushed in a straight line for 1.20 m by a trained dog that exerts a horizontal force with magnitude 36.0 N. Use the work-energy theorem to find the final speed of the 12-pack if there is no friction between the 12-pack and the floor.
questions.explanation:
from work-energy theorem
change in kinetic energy = work done
$\Delta K$ = W
K_f - K_i = Fs
${1 \over 2}m{v^2} - 0 $ = Fs 
m = 4.30Kg, s = 1.2m, F = 36N
v_f = vm/s, v_i = 0
${1 \over 2}m{v^2} = Fs$
$1\over 2$ $\times$ 4.3 $\times$ v^2 = 36 $\times$ 1.2
$v = \sqrt {{{36 \times 1.2 \times 2} \over {4.3}}} $ = 4.48m/s

questions.question: A12-pack of Omni-Cola (mass 4.30 kg) is initially at rest on a horizontal floor. It is then pushed in a straight line for 1.20 m by a trained dog that exerts a horizontal force with magnitude 36.0 N. Use the work-energy theorem to find the final speed of the 12-pack if the coefficient of kinetic friction between the 12-pack and the floor is 0.30?
questions.question_raw: A12-pack of Omni-Cola (mass 4.30 kg) is initially at rest on a horizontal floor. It is then pushed in a straight line for 1.20 m by a trained dog that exerts a horizontal force with magnitude 36.0 N. Use the work-energy theorem to find the final speed of the 12-pack if the coefficient of kinetic friction between the 12-pack and the floor is 0.30?

questions.question: A 6.0-kg box moving at 3.0 m/s on a horizontal, frictionless surface runs into a light spring of force constant 75 N/cm Use the work–energy theorem to find the maximum compression of the spring.
questions.question_raw: A 6.0-kg box moving at 3.0 m/s on a horizontal, frictionless surface runs into a light spring of force constant 75 N/cm Use the work–energy theorem to find the maximum compression of the spring.
questions.explanation:
For maximum compression of spring, kinetic energy will be converted into the potential energy of spring.
${1 \over 2}k{x^2} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
${x^2} = {{m{v^2}} \over k} = {{6 \times 3 \times 3} \over {75 \times {{10}^2}}}$
$x = \sqrt {{{6 \times 3 \times 3} \over {75 \times {{10}^2}}}} $ = 0.085m = 8.5cm

questions.question: How many joules of energy does a 100-watt light bulb use per hour? How fast would a 70-kg person have to run to have that amount of kinetic energy?
questions.question_raw: How many joules of energy does a 100-watt light bulb use per hour? How fast would a 70-kg person have to run to have that amount of kinetic energy?
questions.explanation:
Energy = P $\times$ Time = 100 $\times$ 1Hr
Energy = 100 $\times$ 1 $\times$ 60 $\times$ 60 = 360000 J
for a 70 Kg man $K = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$ 
speed of man v = $ \sqrt {{{2K} \over m}} = \sqrt {{{2 \times 360000} \over {70}}} $ = 101m/s

questions.question: A tandem (two-person) bicycle team must overcome a force of 165 N to maintain a speed of 9.00 m/s. Find the power required per rider, assuming that each contributes equally?
questions.question_raw: A tandem (two-person) bicycle team must overcome a force of 165 N to maintain a speed of 9.00 m/s. Find the power required per rider, assuming that each contributes equally?
questions.explanation:
Total power required to overcome a force of 165 N and to maintain a speed of 9.00 m/s
F = 165N
v = 9m/s
P = Fv = 165 $\times$ 9 = 1485W
if each rider contribute equal power, then the power required per rider will be $P\over 2$ = $1485\over 2$ = 742.5W

questions.question: You are asked to design spring bumpers for the walls of a parking garage. A freely rolling 1200-kg car moving at 0.65 m/s is to compress the spring no more than 0.090 m before stopping. What should be the force constant of the spring? Assume that the spring has negligible mass.
questions.question_raw: You are asked to design spring bumpers for the walls of a parking garage. A freely rolling 1200-kg car moving at 0.65 m/s is to compress the spring no more than 0.090 m before stopping. What should be the force constant of the spring? Assume that the spring has negligible mass.
questions.explanation:
The kinetic energy of the car will be converted into the potential energy of spring.
${1 \over 2}k{x^2} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
$k = {{m{v^2}} \over {{x^2}}}$
m = 1200Kg
v = 0.65m/s
x = 0.09m
$k = {{1200 \times 0.65 \times 0.65} \over {0.09 \times 0.09}} $ = 6.25 $\times$ 10^4N/m

questions.question: On an essentially frictionless, horizontal ice rink, a skater moving at 3.0 m/s encounters a rough patch that reduces her speed to 1.65 m/s due to a friction force that is 25$\% $ of her weight. Use the work-energy theorem to find the length of this rough patch?
questions.question_raw: On an essentially frictionless, horizontal ice rink, a skater moving at 3.0 m/s encounters a rough patch that reduces her speed to 1.65 m/s due to a friction force that is 25{tex}\% {/tex} of her weight. Use the work-energy theorem to find the length of this rough patch?
questions.explanation:
Using the work-energy theorem, change in kinetic energy = work done by the friction force
$\Delta K$ =W_f
$\frac{1}{2} m\left(v_{f}^{2}-v_{i}^{2}\right)$ = -fd
$\frac{1}{2} m\left(v_{f}^{2}-v_{i}^{2}\right)$ = $-m g \times \frac{25}{100} \times d$
$\frac{1}{2} m\left[(1.65)^{2}-(3)^{2}\right]$ = $-m \times 9.8 \times \frac{25}{100} \times d$
$\frac{1}{2} \times(2.7225-9)=-9.8 \times \frac{25}{100} \times d$
$-\frac{6.2775}{2}=-9.8 \times \frac{25}{100} \times d$
d = $\frac{6.2775 \times 100}{2 \times 9.8 \times 25}$ = 1.3 m

questions.question: A pump is required to lift 800 kg of water per minute from a well 14.0 m deep and eject it with a speed of 18.0 m/s. How much work is done per minute in lifting the water?
questions.question_raw: A pump is required to lift 800 kg of water per minute from a well 14.0 m deep and eject it with a speed of 18.0 m/s. How much work is done per minute in lifting the water?
questions.explanation:
mass is given by ,m = 800Kg per minute
h = 14m
work done per minute in lifting the water is given by :-
W = mgh = 800 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 14 = 109760J = 1.1 $\times$ 10^5J

questions.question: A pump is required to lift 800 kg of water (about 210 gallons) per minute from a well 14.0 m deep and eject it with a speed of 18.0 m/s. How much work is done in giving the water the kinetic energy it has when ejected?
questions.question_raw: A pump is required to lift 800 kg of water (about 210 gallons) per minute from a well 14.0 m deep and eject it with a speed of 18.0 m/s. How much work is done in giving the water the kinetic energy it has when ejected?
questions.explanation:
Work is done in giving kinetic energy is equal to change in kinetic energy
W = $ \Delta K$
$W = {1 \over 2}m{v^2} - 0$
W = $\frac{1}{2}$ $\times$ 800 $\times$ 18 $\times$ 18 = 129600J = 1.3 $\times$ 10^5J

questions.question: In which of the following cases is the work done positively?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following cases is the work done positively?

options.label: A
options.text: Work is done by friction on a body sliding down an inclined plane.

options.label: B
options.text: Work is done by gravitational force while a man in lifts a bucket out of a well by means of a rope tied to the bucket.

options.label: C
options.text: Work is done by the resistive force of air on a vibrating pendulum in bringing it to rest.

options.label: D
options.text: Work is done by an applied force on a body moving on a rough horizontal plane with uniform velocity.

correct_option_texts: Work is done by an applied force on a body moving on a rough horizontal plane with uniform velocity.
questions.explanation:
When a body is moving on a rough horizontal surface then there will be 2 forces acting on the body 
 Applied force ( in the direction of motion)
 friction ( opposite to the direction of motion)
As applied force is in the same direction as displacement so work is done will be positive.

chapters.name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
chapters.full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > System of Particles and Rotational Motion

questions.question: A rigid body is a body which:
questions.question_raw: A rigid body is a body which:

options.label: A
options.text: deforms on the application of force.

options.label: B
options.text: becomes longer or shorter if force is applied.

options.label: C
options.text: does not deform on the application of force.

options.label: D
options.text: does not maintain the same distance between any two points on it if force is applied.

correct_option_texts: does not deform on the application of force.
questions.explanation: A rigid body is an idealization of a solid body in which deformation or change in shape is neglected on the application of deforming force.

questions.question: In the pure translational motion of a rigid body:
questions.question_raw: In the pure translational motion of a rigid body:

options.label: A
options.text: at different instants of time, every particle of the body has the same velocity.

options.label: B
options.text: at any instant of time different particles of the body have different velocities.

options.label: C
options.text: at any instant of time velocity is dependent on the position vector of a point on the body.

options.label: D
options.text: at any instant of time, every particle of the body has the same velocity.

correct_option_texts: at any instant of time, every particle of the body has the same velocity.
questions.explanation: In translational motion when the body moves along a straight line or more exactly when every point of the body travels on parallel lines, thus at any instant of time every particle of the body has the same velocity.

questions.question: In rotation of a rigid body about a fixed axis is that in which:
questions.question_raw: In rotation of a rigid body about a fixed axis is that in which:

options.label: A
options.text: every particle of the body moves in a circle, which lies in a plane perpendicular to the axis and has its centre on the axis.

options.label: B
options.text: every particle of the body moves in an ellipse, which lies in a plane perpendicular to the axis and has its focii on the axis.

options.label: C
options.text: every particle of the body moves at the same speed.

options.label: D
options.text: particles close to the axis have larger velocities.

correct_option_texts: every particle of the body moves in a circle, which lies in a plane perpendicular to the axis and has its centre on the axis.
questions.explanation: When a rigid body rotates about a fixed axis, all particles of the body except those which lies on the axis of rotation, move along circular paths in a plane perpendicular to the axis.

questions.question: In precession such as that of a top:
questions.question_raw: In precession such as that of a top:

options.label: A
options.text: the axis of rotation is fixed

options.label: C
options.text: the axis of rotation oscillates vertically

options.label: D
options.text: the axis of rotation oscillates horizontally

questions.explanation: Precession is a change in the orientation of the rotational axis of a rotating body, so the orientation of the axis of rotation of Top change.

questions.question: In general motion of a rigid body:
questions.question_raw: In general motion of a rigid body:

options.label: A
options.text: particles on the body always move around an axis in circles

options.label: B
options.text: both translation and rotation can be present

correct_option_texts: both translation and rotation can be present
questions.explanation: In the general motion of the body can be seen as the combination of pure translation of the center of mass and pure rotation of the body about the center of mass like the motion of a wheel.

questions.question: Let r_i be the position vector of the i^th particle having mass m_i and R be the position vector of the centre of mass. The formula for R is:
questions.question_raw: Let ri be the position vector of the ith particle having mass mi and R be the position vector of the centre of mass. The formula for R is:
questions.explanation:
Let us consider a system consisting of N – particles of masses m_1, m_2,….. m_N having position vectors ${\vec r_1},{\vec r_2}$,...... $ {\vec r_N} $ respectively.
The total mass M of the system is given by 
M = m­_1 +m_2 +…….+ m_N 
We can generalize the definition of the position of centre of mass consisting of N particles, hence the position vector of centre of mass is given below:-
$\vec{R}=\frac{m_1 \vec{r}_1+m_2 \vec{r}_2+\ldots \ldots \ldots+m_N \vec{r}_N}{m_1+m_2+\ldots \ldots+m_N}=\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^N m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_\limits{i=1}^N m_i}=\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^N m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}$

questions.question: the centre of mass of a system of particles moves
questions.question_raw: the centre of mass of a system of particles moves

options.label: A
options.text: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the axis of rotation and all the external forces were absent

options.label: B
options.text: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were zero

options.label: C
options.text: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were applied at that point

options.label: D
options.text: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated in a sphere around the centre of mass and all the external forces were zero

correct_option_texts: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were applied at that point
questions.explanation: The centre of mass of a system of particles moves as if all the mass of the system is concentrated at the centre and all the external forces were applied at that point. In order to ease the calculations involved in the dynamics of an extended body, we treat any extended body as the system of particles, and the motion of centre of mass of the system is calculated by taking the mass of the whole system to be concentrated at the centre of mass and the external forces acting on system are said to be acting on the centre of mass.

questions.question: The total momentum of a system of particles is equal to:
questions.question_raw: The total momentum of a system of particles is equal to:

options.label: A
options.text: the product of half the total mass of the system and the velocity of its centre of mass

options.label: B
options.text: the product of the total mass of the system and the speed of its centre of mass

options.label: C
options.text: the product of the total mass of the system and the velocity of its centre of mass

options.label: D
options.text: the product of the total mass of the system and the average velocity of its centre of mass

correct_option_texts: the product of the total mass of the system and the velocity of its centre of mass
questions.explanation:
Let us consider a system of n particles of masses m_1, m_2, ….m_N. If M is the total mass of the system.
M = m_1 + m_2 +……..+ m_N
If ${\vec R}$ is the position vector of the centre of mass and ${\vec r_1},{\vec r_2},{\vec r_3} . ..... {\vec r_n}$those of constituent particles then
$\mathop R\limits^ \to = {{{m_1}{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_1} + {m_2}{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_2} + .... + {m_N}{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_N}} \over {{m_1} + {m_2} + ..... + {m_N}}} $ = $ {{{m_1}{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_1} + {m_2}{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_2} + {m_N}{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_N}} \over M}$ 
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. time t, we get
${{d\mathop R\limits^ \to } \over {dt}} $ = $ {1 \over M}\left[ {{m_1}{{d{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_1}} \over {dt}} + {m_2}{{d{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_2}} \over {dt}} + .......... + {m_N}{{d{{\mathop r\limits^ \to }_N}} \over {dt}}} \right]$ 
Let the velocity of centre of mass is ${{d\mathop R\limits^ \to } \over {dt}} = \mathop {{V_{CM}}}\limits^ \to $
${{d\mathop {{r_1}}\limits^ \to } \over {dt}} = {\mathop v\limits^ \to _1},{{d\mathop {{r_2}}\limits^ \to } \over {dt}} = {\mathop v\limits^ \to _{2,}}.....{{d\mathop {{r_n}}\limits^ \to } \over {dt}} = \mathop {{v_n}}\limits^ \to $ 
$M\mathop {{V_{CM}}}\limits^ \to $ = $ {m_1}{\mathop v\limits^ \to _1} + {m_2}{\mathop v\limits^ \to _2} + .... + {m_N}{\mathop v\limits^ \to _N} = \sum\limits_{i = 1}^N {{m_i}\,\,\,{{\mathop v\limits^ \to }_i}} $ 
Hence the total momentum of a system of particles is equal to the product of the total mass of the system and the velocity of its centre of mass.

questions.question: Considering binary (double) stars in our frame of reference, the trajectories of the stars are a combination of
questions.question_raw: Considering binary (double) stars in our frame of reference, the trajectories of the stars are a combination of

options.label: A
options.text:
uniform motion in a straight line and
 elliptical orbits of the stars

options.label: B
options.text:
uniform motion in a straight line and
 circular orbits of the stars

options.label: C
options.text:
uniform motion in a straight line and
 straight line motion of the stars

options.label: D
options.text:
uniform motion in a circle and
 circular orbits of the stars

correct_option_texts:
uniform motion in a straight line and
 circular orbits of the stars
questions.explanation:
A double star or visual double is a pair of stars that appear close to each other in the sky as seen from Earth when viewed through an optical telescope.
In absence of external force Centre of mass of double star moves like a free particle. In Centre of mass frame both stars moving in a circle about the Centre of mass which is at rest and both star are diametrically opposite to each other.
Thus in our frame of reference, the trajectories of the stars are a combination of
uniform motion in a straight line of the Centre of mass and 
circular orbits of the stars about the Centre of mass. 
Trajectories of two stars, S_1 (dotted line) and S_2 (solid line) forming a binary system with their centre of mass C in uniform motion.
The same binary system, with the centre of mass C at rest.

questions.question: The vector product of two vectors a and b is a vector c such that the magnitude of c is given by:
questions.question_raw: The vector product of two vectors a and b is a vector c such that the magnitude of c is given by:
questions.explanation:
As per the definition of vector product:-
$\vec c = \vec a \times \vec b = \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \theta \hat n$
$\left| {\vec c} \right| = \left| {\vec a} \right|\left| {\vec b} \right|\sin \theta $

questions.question: The vector product of two vectors a and b is a vector c such that c is perpendicular to the plane containing a and b and the direction is given by:
questions.question_raw: The vector product of two vectors a and b is a vector c such that c is perpendicular to the plane containing a and b and the direction is given by:
questions.explanation:
If vectors A and B lie in the plane of this page, the vector C will be perpendicular to this plane. 
The sense (upward or downward) of the direction of the vector product is given by the direction of the advance of the tip of a right-handed screw when rotated from A to B through angle $\theta$ between them, the screw being placed with its axis perpendicular to the plane containing the two vectors.

questions.question: If $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}=a_x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+a_y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+a_z \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}=b_x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+b_y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+b_z \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ then the cross product $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}$ is given by:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}=a_x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+a_y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+a_z \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}  and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}=b_x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+b_y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+b_z \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} then the cross product {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}{/tex} is given by:
questions.explanation:
${\bf{\vec a}} \times {\bf{\vec b}}$ = $ \left( {{a_y}{b_z} - {a_z}{b_y}} \right){\bf{\hat i}} $ - $\left( {{a_x}{b_z} - {a_z}{b_x}} \right){\bf{\hat j}}$ + $ \left( {{a_x}{b_y} - {a_y}{b_x}} \right){\bf{\hat k}}$ 
As the cross product is the determinant of a 3x3 matrix.
$\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\mathbf{\hat i}}}&{{\mathbf{\hat j}}}&{{\mathbf{\hat k}}} \\ {{{\text{a}}_{\text{x}}}}&{{a_y}}&{{a_z}} \\ {{b_x}}&{{b_y}}&{{b_z}} \end{array}} \right|$

questions.question: If a body is rotating about z-axis with a speed $\omega $ and a point is at a distance of r in the x-y plane then the velocity of the point is:
questions.question_raw: If a body is rotating about z-axis with a speed {tex}\omega {/tex} and a point is at a distance of r in the x-y plane then the velocity of the point is:
questions.explanation:
the angular displacement of a particle about the z-axis
$d\theta = {{ds} \over r}$ 
differentiate with respect to time
${{d\theta } \over {dt}} = {1 \over r}{{ds} \over {dt}}$
${{d\theta } \over {dt}} = \omega$
${{ds} \over {dt}} $ = v
$\omega = {v \over r}$
v = $ r\omega$

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a body about any axis is
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a body about any axis is

options.label: A
options.text: equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about any axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

options.label: B
options.text: equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

options.label: C
options.text: equal to the difference of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

options.label: D
options.text: equal to the average of the moments of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

correct_option_texts: equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.
questions.explanation:
according to parallel axis theorem the moment of inertia of a body about any axis is equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its Centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.
$I = {I_{cm}} + M{a^2}$

questions.question: The angular acceleration vector is defined as:
questions.question_raw: The angular acceleration vector is defined as:
questions.explanation: As per the definition of angular acceleration that " it is the time rate of change of angular velocity" , therefore $\alpha=\frac{d \omega}{d t}$

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > System of Particles and Rotational Motion

questions.question: Torque $\tau $ is defined in terms of the position vector $r$ and the force acting F as:
questions.question_raw: Torque {tex}\tau {/tex} is defined in terms of the position vector {tex}r{/tex} and the force acting F as:
questions.explanation:
Torque = Force $\times$ Perpendicular distance of the line of action of the force from the axis of rotation. It is also called the moment of force.
Torque is defined as the cross product of the vector by which the force's application point is offset relative to the fixed suspension point (distance vector) and the force vector, which tends to produce rotational motion.
$\vec \tau = \vec r \times \vec F$

questions.question: The angular momentum L of the particle having a position vector $r$ and linear momentum $p$, with respect to the origin O is defined to be
questions.question_raw: The angular momentum L of the particle having a position vector {tex}r{/tex} and linear momentum {tex}p{/tex}, with respect to the origin O is defined to be
questions.explanation:
Angular momentum is also referred to as a moment of linear momentum
The angular momentum L of a particle of mass m with respect to a chosen origin is given by
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p$ 
The direction of angular momentum is according to the right-hand rule.

questions.question: Torque $\tau $ and angular momentum L are related by:
questions.question_raw: Torque {tex}\tau {/tex} and angular momentum L are related by:
questions.explanation:
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p$ 
on differentiation with respect to time 
$\frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}(\vec{r} \times \vec{p})$ 
Applying the product rule for differentiation to the right-hand side,
$\frac{d}{d t}(\vec{r} \times \vec{p})=\left(\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t} \times \vec{p}\right)+\left(\vec{r} \times \frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}\right)$
$\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t}=\vec{v}, \vec{p}=m \vec{v}$
$\left(\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t} \times \vec{p}\right)=\vec{v} \times m \vec{v}=0$
$\frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}=\vec{F}$
$\left(\vec{r} \times \frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}\right)=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}=\vec{\tau}$
Hence $\vec{\tau}=\frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}$

questions.question: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if:
questions.question_raw: A rigid body is in mechanical equilibrium if:
questions.explanation: For the mechanical equilibrium of a rigid body, it must be in translational as well as in rotational equilibrium. Linear acceleration and angular acceleration must be zero. Hence net force and net torque will be zero that is $\sum F = 0$ and $\sum \tau = 0$

questions.question: Centre of gravity can be defined:
questions.question_raw: Centre of gravity can be defined:

options.label: A
options.text: as that point where the total gravitational torque on the body is zero

options.label: B
options.text: as that point where the total gravitational torque on the body is greater than zero

options.label: D
options.text: as that point where the total gravitational force on the body is zero

correct_option_texts: as that point where the total gravitational torque on the body is zero
questions.explanation: The point at which the entire weight of a body may be thought of as centered so that if supported at this point the body would balance perfectly, so it can also be defined as that point where the total gravitational torque on the body is zero.

questions.question: Analogue of mass in rotational motion is:
questions.question_raw: Analogue of mass in rotational motion is:
questions.explanation: The moment of inertia is a physical quantity that tells how easily a body can be rotated about a given axis. It is a rotational analog of mass. It plays the same role in rotational motion as 'inertia' does in translational motion. A large moment of inertia also helps in keeping the rotation motion uniform.

questions.question: The radius of gyration of a body about an axis may be defined as the
questions.question_raw: The radius of gyration of a body about an axis may be defined as the

options.label: A
options.text: distance from the center of gravity to the axis of rotation

options.label: B
options.text: distance from the center of gravity to the center of mass

options.label: C
options.text: distance from the center of mass to the axis of rotation

options.label: D
options.text: distance from the axis of a mass point whose mass is equal to the mass of the whole body and whose moment of inertia is equal to the moment of inertia of the body about the axis

correct_option_texts: distance from the axis of a mass point whose mass is equal to the mass of the whole body and whose moment of inertia is equal to the moment of inertia of the body about the axis
questions.explanation:
It is the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation to a point mass of mass m that gives an equivalent inertia to the original object (of mass, m)
if radius of gyration is k then 
$\eqalign{ & I = m{k^2} \cr & k = \sqrt {{I \over m}} \cr} $

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a planar body (lamina) about an axis perpendicular to its plane is.
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a planar body (lamina) about an axis perpendicular to its plane is.

options.label: A
options.text: equal to the its moments of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the axis of rotation axis and lying in the plane of the body

options.label: B
options.text: equal to the difference of its moments of inertia about two perpendicular axes concurrent with perpendicular axis and lying in the plane of the body

options.label: C
options.text: equal to the average of its moments of inertia about three perpendicular axes concurrent with perpendicular axis and lying in the plane of the body

options.label: D
options.text: equal to the sum of its moments of inertia about two perpendicular axes concurrent with perpendicular axis and lying in the plane of the body

correct_option_texts: equal to the sum of its moments of inertia about two perpendicular axes concurrent with perpendicular axis and lying in the plane of the body
questions.explanation:
According to perpendicular axis theorem "For a planar object, the moment of inertia about an axis perpendicular to the plane is the sum of the moments of inertia of two perpendicular axes through the same point in the plane of the object".
${I_z} = {I_x} + {I_y}$

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a body about any axis is
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a body about any axis is

options.label: A
options.text: equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about any axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

options.label: B
options.text: equal to the average of the moments of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

options.label: C
options.text: equal to the difference of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

options.label: D
options.text: equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

correct_option_texts: equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.
questions.explanation:
according to parallel axis theorem the moment of inertia of a body about any axis is equal to the sum of the moment of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its Centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.
$I = {I_{cm}} + M{a^2}$

questions.question: The angular acceleration of a rigid body ${\bf{\alpha }}$ rotating about a fixed axis is given by (I is the moment of inertia and ${\bf{\tau }}$ is the torque):
questions.question_raw: The angular acceleration of a rigid body {tex}{\bf{\alpha }}{/tex} rotating about a fixed axis is given by (I is the moment of inertia and {tex}{\bf{\tau }}{/tex} is the torque):
questions.explanation:
$L = I\omega $ 
differentiate with respect to time
$\frac{d L}{d t}=I \frac{d \omega}{d t}$
$\frac{d L}{d t}=\tau$ 
$\frac{d \omega}{d t}=\alpha$
$\tau $ = $ I\alpha$

questions.question: For rolling motion without slipping the kinetic energy of the body is:
questions.question_raw: For rolling motion without slipping the kinetic energy of the body is:

options.label: A
options.text: equal to the difference of kinetic energies of translation and rotation

options.label: B
options.text: equal to the sum of kinetic energies of translation and rotation

options.label: C
options.text: equal to the kinetic energy of translation

options.label: D
options.text: equal to the kinetic energy of rotation

correct_option_texts: equal to the sum of kinetic energies of translation and rotation
questions.explanation:
Rolling motion without slipping involves both translation motion and rotation motion. Hence kinetic energy of the body in rolling motion is the sum of both translation kinetic energy and rotational kinetic energy.
K = K_trans + K_rot
 K = $ {1 \over 2}I{\omega ^2} + {1 \over 2}m{v^2} $

questions.question: A solid sphere of mass M and radius R spins about an axis passing through its centre making 600 rpm. Its kinetic energy of rotation is:
questions.question_raw: A solid sphere of mass M and radius R spins about an axis passing through its centre making 600 rpm. Its kinetic energy of rotation is:
questions.explanation:
The kinetic energy of rotation
K =$\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ 
$n = \frac{600} {60} = 10$ revolution per second
angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n $ = 2$\pi$ $\times$ 10 = 20 rad/sec
moment of inertia about an axis passing through centre of a solid sphere
$I =\frac {2}{ 5}M{R^2}$ 
The kinetic energy of rotation
$K=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5} M R^2 \times(20 \pi)^2$ = 80$\pi$^2MR^2

questions.question: A child swinging on a swing in a sitting position stands up. Then, the time period of the swing will:
questions.question_raw: A child swinging on a swing in a sitting position stands up. Then, the time period of the swing will:

options.label: B
options.text: Increase if the child is tall and decreases if the child is short

questions.explanation:
As the child stand up then Centre of mass is shifted in upwards direction from the sitting position of the child, thus decreasing the effective length of the swing, so as the time period is proportional to the square root of length, thus time period decreases.
time period 
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {{l \over g}} $
$T \propto \sqrt l $
The time period of swing decreases as effective length decreases.

questions.question: Angular momentum is:
questions.question_raw: Angular momentum is:
questions.explanation:
The axial vector represents the rotational effect and is always along the axis of rotation. The direction of angular momentum always along the axis of rotation in accordance with the right-hand screw rule. Hence angular momentum is an axial vector.
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p$

questions.question: Angular momentum of a body is defined as the product of:
questions.question_raw: Angular momentum of a body is defined as the product of:

options.label: B
options.text: Moment of inertia and angular velocity

correct_option_texts: Moment of inertia and angular velocity
questions.explanation:
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p$ 
if r is the perpendicular to P (in case of circular motion)
L = mvr
L = $ m\left( {r\omega } \right)r = m{r^2}\omega$
 L = $ I\omega$
