questions.question: The radius of gyration of a circular loop of radius r and mass m rotating about its diameter as the axis is:
questions.question_raw: The radius of gyration of a circular loop of radius r and mass m rotating about its diameter as the axis is:
questions.explanation:
Moment of inertia of ring or circular loop about an axis passing through centre of mass and perpendicular to the plane is MR^2,
Applying perpendicular axis theorem 
I_z = I_x + I_y
MR^2 = I + I
I = $\frac {1}{2}M{R^2}$
Here I = Moment of inertia about an axis passing through diameter
A moment of inertia in terms of the radius of gyration (k) is Mk^2
I = Mk^2
$\frac{1}{2}$MR^2 = Mk^2
k = $\frac{R}{\sqrt2}$

questions.question: In an orbital motion, the angular momentum vector is:
questions.question_raw: In an orbital motion, the angular momentum vector is:
questions.explanation:
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p = m\left( {\vec r \times \vec v} \right)$ 
Hence the direction of angular momentum $\vec L$ is perpendicular to both $\vec r$ and $\vec v$ that is perpendicular to the orbital plane.

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a circular disc of mass M and radius R rotating about an axis passing through its edge and perpendicular to the plane of the disc is:
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a circular disc of mass M and radius R rotating about an axis passing through its edge and perpendicular to the plane of the disc is:
questions.explanation:
Moment of inertia of a disc about an axis passing through Centre of mass and perpendicular to the plane
${I_{cm}} = \frac{1}{2}M{R^2}$}
if I = Moment of inertia of a disc about an axis passing through its edge and perpendicular to the plane
applying parallel axis theorem
I = I_cm + MR^2
I = $\frac{1}{2}$MR^2 + MR^2
I = $\frac{3}{2}$MR^2

questions.question: A constant torque acting on a uniform circular wheel changes its angular momentum from A_0 to 4A_0 in 4 seconds. The magnitude of the torque is:
questions.question_raw: A constant torque acting on a uniform circular wheel changes its angular momentum from A0 to 4A0 in 4 seconds. The magnitude of the torque is:
questions.explanation:
$\tau=\frac{\Delta L}{\Delta t}=\frac{L_f-L_i}{\Delta t}$
L_f = 4A_0
L_i = A_0
$\Delta t $ = 4 sec
$\tau=\frac{4 A_0-A_0}{4}=\frac{3 A_0}{4}$

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a circular ring about an axis passing through its centre and normal to its plane is 200 gm cm^2. Then its moment of inertia about diameter is:
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a circular ring about an axis passing through its centre and normal to its plane is 200 gm cm2. Then its moment of inertia about diameter is:
questions.explanation:
applying perpendicular axis theorem
I_z = I_x + Iy
I_z = 200
200 = I + I
2I = 200
I = 100
I = moment of inertia about an axis passing through diameter
I = 100 gm cm^2

questions.question: A dancer on ice spins faster when she folds her hands. This is due to:
questions.question_raw: A dancer on ice spins faster when she folds her hands. This is due to:

options.label: A
options.text: constant angular momentum and an increase in kinetic energy

options.label: B
options.text: increase in energy and decrease in angular momentum

options.label: C
options.text: increase in energy and an increase in angular momentum

options.label: D
options.text: the decrease in friction at the skates

correct_option_texts: constant angular momentum and an increase in kinetic energy
questions.explanation:
L =$ I\omega $ 
if L = const.
then $I\omega $ = const.
When a dancer is spinning, she has a certain angular momentum. When the dancer folds her arms, the distance of all the points of her body decreases with respect to axis of rotation so that her moment of inertia decreases. Thus, in order to conserve angular momentum, the speed of rotation has to increase and hence the dancer spins faster.
$K = {{{L^2}} \over {2I}}$
$
 K \propto {1 \over I} $ 
Hence if the moment of inertia decrease then kinetic energy will increase.

questions.question: A uniform rod of mass 2 kg is 1 meter long. Its radius of gyration about an axis through its one end and perpendicular to its length in SI units is:
questions.question_raw: A uniform rod of mass 2 kg is 1 meter long. Its radius of gyration about an axis through its one end and perpendicular to its length in SI units is:
questions.explanation:
Moment of inertia of the rod about an axis through its one end and perpendicular to its length
$I = \frac{M{L^2}}{3}$ 
M = 2 Kg
L = 1 m
Moment of inertia of rod in terms of the radius of gyration
I = Mk^2
$\frac{M{L^2}}{3} = M{k^2}$
$k=\sqrt{\frac{L^2}{3}}=\frac{L}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

questions.question: Two discs of the same mass and same thickness t are made from two different materials of densities d_1 and d_2 respectively. The ratio of the moments of inertia of the two about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the lamina is:
questions.question_raw: Two discs of the same mass and same thickness t are made from two different materials of densities d1 and d2 respectively. The ratio of the moments of inertia of the two about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the lamina is:
questions.explanation:
if V = volume of disc (At), A = area of disc = $\pi$r^2, t = thickness of disc, M = mass of disc, d = density of material of disc
$V = \frac{M}{d}$
At = $\frac{M}{d}$
$\pi r^{2} t=\frac{M}{d}$
$r^{2}=\frac{M}{\pi d_{1} t}$
r^2 $ \propto \frac{1}{d}$
$\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}$
$\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} M r_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} M r_{2}^{2}}=\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}=\frac{d_{2}}{d_{1}}$
I_1:I_2 = d_2:d_1

questions.question: Four spheres each of mass M and radius R are placed with their centers on the four corners of a square of side 'b'. The moment of inertia of the system about any side of the square is:
questions.question_raw: Four spheres each of mass M and radius R are placed with their centers on the four corners of a square of side 'b'. The moment of inertia of the system about any side of the square is:
questions.explanation:
The moment of inertia of the system about any side of the square is
I = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_4
I = $\frac {2}{5}M{R^2} + \frac{2}{5}M{R^2}$ + $\left(\frac{2}{5} M R^2+M b^2\right)$ + $\left(\frac{2}{5} M R^2+M b^2\right)$
I = $\frac{8}{5}$MR^2 + 2Mb^2

questions.question: When a mass is rotating in a plane about a fixed point, its angular momentum is directed along:
questions.question_raw: When a mass is rotating in a plane about a fixed point, its angular momentum is directed along:

options.label: C
options.text: a line at an angle of 45^o to the plane of rotation

questions.explanation:
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p = m\left( {\vec r \times \vec v} \right)$ 
Hence the direction of the angular vector $\vec L$ is perpendicular to both $\vec r$ and $\vec v$ that is along the axis of rotation according to the right-hand screw rule.

questions.question: A mass is revolving in a circle, which is in the plane of the paper. The direction of angular acceleration if any, is:
questions.question_raw: A mass is revolving in a circle, which is in the plane of the paper. The direction of angular acceleration if any, is:

options.label: C
options.text: Upward from the plane of the paper

options.label: D
options.text: At right angles to the plane of the paper

correct_option_texts: At right angles to the plane of the paper
questions.explanation: Angular acceleration is an axial vector. It is always directed along the axis of rotation according to the right-hand screw rule. Hence the direction of the angular acceleration vector is perpendicular to the plane in which the rotation takes place.

questions.question: Which of the following has the largest moment of inertia?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following has the largest moment of inertia?

options.label: A
options.text: Disc of mass M and radius R about an axis perpendicular to its plane

options.label: B
options.text: Ring of mass M and radius R about an axis perpendicular to its plane

options.label: C
options.text: A solid sphere of mass M and radius R about an axis passing through its centre of mass

options.label: D
options.text: Bar magnet of mass M and length R about an axis passing through its centre of mass

correct_option_texts: Ring of mass M and radius R about an axis perpendicular to its plane
questions.explanation:
I_Ring = MR^2
I_disc = $1\over 2$MR^2
I_sphere = $2\over 5$MR^2
I_rod=$MR^2\over 12$
Hence the ring has the largest moment of inertia.

questions.question: A spherical solid ball of 1 kg. mass and radius 3 cm is rotating about an axis passing through its centre with an angular velocity of 50 radians/s. The kinetic energy of rotation is
questions.question_raw: A spherical solid ball of 1 kg. mass and radius 3 cm is rotating about an axis passing through its centre with an angular velocity of 50 radians/s. The kinetic energy of rotation is
questions.explanation:
M = 1Kg
R = 3cm = 3 $\times$ 10^-2m
$\omega$ = 50rad/s
K = $\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5} M R^{2} \times \omega^{2}$
K = $\frac12 \times\frac25$ $\times$1 $\times$(3 $\times$10^−2)^2 $\times$50 $\times$50 = $9 \over {20}$J

questions.question: A jet airplane travelling at the speed of 500 kmh^–1 ejects its products of combustion at the speed of 1500 km h^–1 relative to the jet plane. What is the speed of the combustion with respect to an observer on ground?
questions.question_raw: A jet airplane travelling at the speed of 500 kmh–1 ejects its products of combustion at the speed of 1500 km h–1 relative to the jet plane. What is the speed of the combustion with respect to an observer on ground?
questions.explanation:
Speed of the jet airplane, v_jet = 500 km/h
Relative speed of its products of combustion with respect to the plane, v_smoke = – 1500 km/h
Speed of its products of combustion with respect to the ground = v′_smoke
Relative speed of its products of combustion with respect to the airplane, v_smoke = v′_smoke – v_jet
$\Rightarrow$ – 1500 = v′_smoke – 500
$\Rightarrow$ v′_smoke = – 1000 km/h
The negative sign indicates that the direction of its products of combustion is opposite to the direction of motion of the jet airplane.

questions.question: If a gymnast sitting on a rotating stool with his arms outstretched, suddenly lowers his hands:
questions.question_raw: If a gymnast sitting on a rotating stool with his arms outstretched, suddenly lowers his hands:
questions.explanation: When gymnast lowers his hand the distance of the mass from rotational axis decrease. Hence his moment of inertia decreases and angular velocity increases to conserve angular momentum.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > System of Particles and Rotational Motion

questions.question: A planet is revolving around the sun in an elliptical orbit. The maximum and the minimum distances of the planet from the sun are 3 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{12}}}}$ m and 2 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{10}}}}$ m respectively. The speed of the planet when it is nearest to sun is 2 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{7}}}}$ m/sec. What is the speed of the planet when it is farthest from the sun?
questions.question_raw: A planet is revolving around the sun in an elliptical orbit. The maximum and the minimum distances of the planet from the sun are 3 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{12}}}}{/tex} m and 2 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{10}}}}{/tex} m respectively. The speed of the planet when it is nearest to sun is 2 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{7}}}}{/tex} m/sec. What is the speed of the planet when it is farthest from the sun?
questions.explanation:
Perihelion is the nearest distance of planet from focus.
aphelion is the farthest distance of planet from focus.
v_p = 2 $\times$ 10^7m/s
v_a = ?
r_p = 2 $\times$ 10^10m
r_a= 3 $\times$ 10^12m
$\frac{v_{p}}{v_{a}}=\frac{r_{a}}{r_{p}}$
$\frac{2 \times 10^{7}}{v_{a}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^{10}}$
v_a = 1.33 $\times$ 10^5m/s

questions.question: The angular velocity of the body changes from ${\omega _{\rm{1}}}$ to ${\omega _{\rm{2}}}$ without applying torque but by changing moment of inertia. The ratio of the initial radius of gyration to the final radius of gyration is:
questions.question_raw: The angular velocity of the body changes from {tex}{\omega _{\rm{1}}}{/tex} to {tex}{\omega _{\rm{2}}}{/tex} without applying torque but by changing moment of inertia. The ratio of the initial radius of gyration to the final radius of gyration is:

questions.question: Two bodies with moments of inertia I_1 and I_2 (I_1$ > $ I_2) have equal angular momenta. If K.E. of rotation is E_1 and E_2 then:
questions.question_raw: Two bodies with moments of inertia I1 and I2 (I1{tex} > {/tex} I2) have equal angular momenta. If K.E. of rotation is E1 and E2 then:
questions.explanation:
K.E. of rotation 
E = ${{{L^2}} \over {2I}}$
E $\propto$ $ {1 \over I}$
I_1 > I_2
E_1 < E_2

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform ring of mass 1 Kg about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring is 0.25 Kg m^2. Then the diameter of the ring is:
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform ring of mass 1 Kg about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring is 0.25 Kg m2. Then the diameter of the ring is:
questions.explanation:
I = MR^2
I = 0.25Kgm^2
M = 1Kg
$R=\sqrt{\frac{I}{M}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.25}{1}}$ = 0.5 m
Diameter d = 2R
d = 2 $\times$ 0.5 = 1 m

questions.question: We have two spheres, one is a hollow shell and the other a solid. They have identical masses and moments of inertia about their respective diameters. The ratio of their radii is given by
questions.question_raw: We have two spheres, one is a hollow shell and the other a solid. They have identical masses and moments of inertia about their respective diameters. The ratio of their radii is given by

${I_{1}} = {2 \over 3}M{R_1}^2$ 
Moment of inertia of hollow shell about an axis passing through its diameter

questions.question: A particle performs uniform circular motion with an angular momentum L. If the frequency of particle's motion is doubled and its K.E. is halved, the angular momentum becomes:
questions.question_raw: A particle performs uniform circular motion with an angular momentum L. If the frequency of particle's motion is doubled and its K.E. is halved, the angular momentum becomes:
questions.explanation:
$K=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times I \omega \times \omega$
$K=\frac{1}{2} L \omega$
$\frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\frac{L_{1} \omega_{1}}{L_{2} \omega_{2}}$ 
K_1 = K, K_2 = $K\over2$ 
n_1 = n, ${\omega _1} = 2\pi n = \omega $ 
n_2 = 2n, ${\omega _2} = 2\pi \times 2n = 2\omega $ 
L_1 = L, L_2 = ?
${{2K} \over K} = {{L\omega } \over {{L_2} \times 2\omega }}$
$L_{2}=\frac{L}{4}$

questions.question: A flywheel at rest is to reach an angular velocity of 36 rad/sec, in 6 sec, with a constant angular acceleration. The total angle turned during this interval is:
questions.question_raw: A flywheel at rest is to reach an angular velocity of 36 rad/sec, in 6 sec, with a constant angular acceleration. The total angle turned during this interval is:
questions.explanation:
$\omega = {\omega _o} + \alpha t$
36 = 0 + 6$\alpha$ 
$\alpha = {{36} \over 6} = 6rad/{s^2}$
$\theta = {\omega _o}t + {1 \over 2}\alpha {t^2}$
$\theta$ = 0 + ${1 \over 2}$ $\times$ 6 $\times$ 6 $\times$ 6 
$\theta$ = 108rad

$I = \frac{M{l^2}} {{3}}$ 
Moment of inertia of rod in terms of the radius of gyration

M = 100 gm
l = 100 cm
Mk^2 = $\frac{Ml^2}{3}$
$k=\sqrt{\frac{l^{2}}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{100 \times 100}{3}}$

questions.question: The M.I. of a body about a given axis is 1.2 kg m^2. Initially, the body is at rest. In order to produce rotational kinetic energy of 1500 joule, the angular acceleration of 25 rad/sec^2 must be applied about that axis for the duration of
questions.question_raw: The M.I. of a body about a given axis is 1.2 kg m2. Initially, the body is at rest. In order to produce rotational kinetic energy of 1500 joule, the angular acceleration of 25 rad/sec2 must be applied about that axis for the duration of
questions.explanation:
K = 1500 J
I = 1.2 Kgm^2
$\alpha $ = 25rad/s^2
$\Delta$t = ?
$K=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{I}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1500}{1.2}}$ = 50rad/s
$\alpha = {{\Delta \omega } \over {\Delta t}} = {{\omega - {\omega _o}} \over {\Delta t}} = {{50 - 0} \over {\Delta t}}$
25 = $ {{50 - 0} \over {\Delta t}}$ 
$ \Delta t = {{50} \over {25}} $ = 2 sec

questions.question: A thin circular ring of mass M and radius R is rotating about its central axis with angular velocity. Four-point objects each of mass m are attached gently to the opposite ends of two perpendicular diameters, the angular velocity of the ring is given by:
questions.question_raw: A thin circular ring of mass M and radius R is rotating about its central axis with angular velocity. Four-point objects each of mass m are attached gently to the opposite ends of two perpendicular diameters, the angular velocity of the ring is given by:
questions.explanation:
Let $\omega$ be the angular velocity of the Ring of Mass M, thus the moment of inertia about a given axis is I_1 = $MR^2$ and the four-point objects are gently placed at perpendicular diameters at the opposite end, so, the distance of each object from the axis of rotation is R, so total moment of inertia of the ring and four objects is I_2 = $MR^2 + 4mR^2$. 
According to the law of conservation of angular momentum I_1 $\omega$ = I_2$\omega$_2 , So on solving $\omega_2=\left(\frac{M R^2}{M R^2+4 m R^2}\right) \omega=\frac{M}{M+4 m} \cdot \omega$

questions.question: What is the moment of inertia of a thin rod of length L and mass M about an axis passing through one end and perpendicular to its length?
questions.question_raw: What is the moment of inertia of a thin rod of length L and mass M about an axis passing through one end and perpendicular to its length?
questions.explanation: Using theorem of the parallel axis, the axis is shifted by $\frac{L}{2}$ distance from the centre of Mass where the Moment of Inertia is $\frac{M L^2}{12}$, thus the moment of inertia of a thin rod about an axis passing through one end and perpendicular to its length is $\frac{M L^2}{12}+\frac{M L^2}{4}=\frac{1}{3} M L^2$

And the radius of gyration MK^2 = $\frac{1}{4}$MR^2 
K = $\frac{R}{2}$

As Mass = Density $\times $ Volume of Sphere

questions.question: A thin uniform rod of length 2l and mass M is acted upon a constant torque. The angular velocity changes from zero to $\omega $ in time t. The value of torque is:
questions.question_raw: A thin uniform rod of length 2l and mass M is acted upon a constant torque. The angular velocity changes from zero to {tex}\omega {/tex} in time t. The value of torque is:

As Torque($\tau $) is equal to the product of Moment of Inertia (I) and Angular acceleration $\left( \alpha \right)$
$\tau=I \alpha$
 $\tau=I \frac{\Delta \omega}{\Delta t}$

questions.question: All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun situated at one of the foci of the ellipse. This law was given by
questions.question_raw: All planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun situated at one of the foci of the ellipse. This law was given by
questions.explanation: In the 17th century, Johannes Kepler discovered that the orbits along which the planets travel around the Sun are ellipses with the Sun at one focus, and described this in his first law of planetary motion.

questions.question: According to Kepler’s Law of areas:
questions.question_raw: According to Kepler’s Law of areas:

options.label: A
options.text: Only the line that joins earth to the sun sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time

options.label: B
options.text: The line that joins any planet to the sun sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time

options.label: C
options.text: Only the line that joins mars to the earth sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time

options.label: D
options.text: The line that joins any planet to the earth sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time

correct_option_texts: The line that joins any planet to the sun sweeps equal areas in equal intervals of time
questions.explanation: Kepler law of areas describes the speed of a planet travelling in an elliptical orbit around the sun. A planet’s orbital speed changes, depending on how far it is from the Sun. The closer a planet is to the Sun, the stronger the Sun’s gravitational pull on it, and the faster the planet moves. The farther it is from the Sun, the weaker the Sun’s gravitational pull, and the slower it moves in its orbit. So, the statement of Kepler's law of area is "The line joining the sun to the planet sweeps out equal areas in equal interval of time". i.e. areal velocity is constant.

questions.question: According to Kepler’s Law of periods, the ________ of the time period of revolution of a planet is proportional to the cube of the _________ of the ellipse traced out by the planet.
questions.question_raw: According to Kepler’s Law of periods, the ________ of the time period of revolution of a planet is proportional to the cube of the _________ of the ellipse traced out by the planet.
questions.explanation: Kepler's 3^rd Law: T^2 = a^3. Kepler's 3rd law is a mathematical formula. It means that if you know the period of a planet's orbit (T = how long it takes the planet to go around the Sun), then you can determine that planet's distance from the Sun (a is the length of the semimajor axis of the planet's orbit)

questions.question: The area $\Delta $A swept out by a planet of mass m in time interval $\Delta $t is related to the angular momentum by:
questions.question_raw: The area {tex}\Delta {/tex}A swept out by a planet of mass m in time interval {tex}\Delta {/tex}t is related to the angular momentum by:
questions.explanation:
The law of areas can be understood as the consequence of conservation of angular momentum which is valid for any central force such that the force on the planet is along the vector joining the sun and the planet. Let the sun be at the origin and let the position and momentum of the planet be denoted by r and p respectively. Then the area swept out by the planet of mass m in the time interval Δt is given by ΔA = $\frac{1}{2}$ (r $\times$ vΔt).
Hence $\frac{\Delta{A}}{\Delta{t}}$ = $\frac{1}{2}$(r $\times$ p)/m = $\frac{L}{2m}$
where v is the velocity, L is the angular momentum equal to (r $\times$ p).

questions.question: Newton’s law of universal gravitation states that the gravitational force of attraction between any two particles of masses m_1 and m_2 separated by a distance r has the magnitude equal to:
questions.question_raw: Newton’s law of universal gravitation states that the gravitational force of attraction between any two particles of masses m1 and m2 separated by a distance r has the magnitude equal to:
questions.explanation: According to Newton's law of universal gravitation, the force of attraction between two objects is directly proportional to the product of two masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

questions.question: The direction of the universal gravitational force between particles of masses ${{\rm{m}}_{\rm{1}}}$ and ${{\rm{m}}_{\rm{2}}}$ is:
questions.question_raw: The direction of the universal gravitational force between particles of masses {tex}{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} and {tex}{{\rm{m}}_{\rm{2}}}{/tex} is:

options.label: C
options.text: towards the center of the earth

questions.explanation:
Since the gravitational force is attractive in nature, one Force will be on m_1 which will be directed towards m_ 2. i.e., ${\overrightarrow F _{12}} = G{{{m_1}{m_2}} \over {{r_{21}}^2}}$ and other force will be on m_2 which will be directed towards m_ 1 i.e., ${\overrightarrow F _{21}} = -G{{{m_1}{m_2}} \over {{r_{12}}^2}}$
Clearly, It can be seen that ${\overrightarrow F _{12}} = - {\overrightarrow F _{21}}$.

questions.question: A central force is always directed:
questions.question_raw: A central force is always directed:

options.label: A
options.text: at a varying angle to the position vector of the point of application of the force with respect to the fixed point

options.label: B
options.text: along the position vector of the point of application of the force with respect to the fixed point

options.label: C
options.text: at a fixed angle to the position vector of the point of application of the force with respect to the fixed point

options.label: D
options.text: perpendicular to the position vector of the point of application of the force with respect to the fixed point

correct_option_texts: along the position vector of the point of application of the force with respect to the fixed point
questions.explanation:
The motion of a particle under a central force F always remains in the plane defined by its initial position and velocity. This may be seen by symmetry. Since the position r, velocity v, and force F all lie in the same plane, there is never an acceleration perpendicular to that plane, because that would break the symmetry between "above" the plane and "below" the plane.
To demonstrate this mathematically, it suffices to show that the angular momentum of the particle is constant. This angular momentum L is defined by the equation
L = r $\times$ p = r $\times$ mv
as angular momentum is conserved, the torque vanishes as F is zero or along r
$dL\over dt$= $r \times F$ = 0
central forces always satisfy this condition. A central force is always directed towards the center.

questions.question: The force of attraction between a hollow spherical shell of uniform density and a point mass situated outside is just as if the entire mass of the shell is
questions.question_raw: The force of attraction between a hollow spherical shell of uniform density and a point mass situated outside is just as if the entire mass of the shell is

questions.explanation: According to Shell's theorem, If a particle of mass m is located outside a spherical shell of mass M at, for instance, point P, the shell attracts the particle as though the mass of the shell were concentrated at its centre. Thus, as far as the gravitational force acting on a particle outside the shell is concerned, a spherical shell acts no differently from the solid spherical distributions of mass.

questions.question: The force of attraction due to a hollow spherical shell of uniform density, on a point mass situated inside it, is
questions.question_raw: The force of attraction due to a hollow spherical shell of uniform density, on a point mass situated inside it, is
questions.explanation: The gravitational force acting by a spherically symmetric shell upon a point mass inside it, is the vector sum of gravitational forces acted by each part of the shell, and this vector sum is equal to zero. That is, a mass m within a spherically symmetric shell of mass M will feel no net force

questions.question: In Cavendish’s experiment:
questions.question_raw: In Cavendish’s experiment:

options.label: A
options.text: net torque on bar AB having two small lead spheres due to gravitational forces is positive

options.label: B
options.text: torque on bar AB having two small lead spheres due to gravitational forces is balanced by the restoring torque of the wire

options.label: C
options.text: the net force on bar AB having two small lead spheres due to gravitational forces is negative

options.label: D
options.text: the net force on bar AB having two small lead spheres due to gravitational forces is positive

correct_option_texts: torque on bar AB having two small lead spheres due to gravitational forces is balanced by the restoring torque of the wire
questions.explanation: Cavendish's apparatus for experimentally determining the value of G involved a light, rigid rod about 2-feet long. Two small lead spheres were attached to the ends of the rod and the rod was suspended by a thin wire. When the rod becomes twisted, the torsion of the wire begins to exert a torsional force that is proportional to the angle of rotation of the rod. The more twist of the wire, the more the system pushes backward to restore itself towards the original position.

questions.question: The value of the gravitational constant G is
questions.question_raw: The value of the gravitational constant G is
questions.explanation:
The value of G was experimentally determined by Lord Henry Cavendish using a torsion balance. Cavendish brought two large lead spheres near the smaller spheres attached to the rod. Since all masses attract, the large spheres exerted a gravitational force upon the smaller spheres and twisted the rod a measurable amount. Once the torsional force balanced the gravitational force, the rod and spheres came to rest and Cavendish was able to determine the gravitational force of attraction between the masses. Cavendish expressed his result in terms of the density of the Earth.
$G = g{{R_{earth}^2} \over {{M_{earth}}}} = {{3g} \over {4\pi {R_{earth}}{\rho _{earth}}}}$
After converting to SI units, Cavendish's value for the Earth's density, 5.448 g cm^−3, gives
G = 6.74×10^−11 m^3 kg^–1 s^−2
Today, the currently accepted value is 6.67259 x 10^-11 N m^2/kg^2.

questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity at a height h in terms of mass of earth M_E, radius of the earth R_E and gravitational constant G is
questions.question_raw: The acceleration due to gravity at a height h in terms of mass of earth ME, radius of the earth RE and gravitational constant G is
questions.explanation:
We know by Newton's law of gravitation, the force on the body of mass m, situated at height h to the surface of the earth of mass M_E is given by:
$F=G \frac{M_e m}{(r+h)^2}$ ...(i)
We also know F = mass $\times$ acceleration(here acceleration is the acceleration due to gravity)
$\Rightarrow$ F = mg ...(ii)
Equating (i) & (ii), we get
$g=G \frac{M_e}{(r+h)^2}$

questions.question: The acceleration due to gravity at a depth d in terms of g(acceleration due to gravity) at radius of the earth R_E is
questions.question_raw: The acceleration due to gravity at a depth d in terms of g(acceleration due to gravity) at radius of the earth RE is
questions.explanation:
Acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the earth of radius R_E
$g = G\frac{M}{{{R_E^2}}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi \rho GR_E$ ...(1)
Acceleration due to gravity at depth d from the surface of the earth 
$g(d) = \frac{4}{ 3}\pi \rho G(R - d)$ ...(2)
From (1) & (2), we get
$g(d) = g(1 - \frac{d}{R_E})$

questions.question: The formula for gravitational potential energy associated with two particles of masses m_1 and m_2 separated by distance r is given by
questions.question_raw: The formula for gravitational potential energy associated with two particles of masses m1 and m2 separated by distance r is given by
questions.explanation:
Change in gravitational potential energy of a system is defined as the -ve of the work done by the gravitational force as the configuration of the system is changed.
${U_f}\; - {\rm{ }}{U_i}\; = {\rm{ }}{W_{gr}}=V(Potential Energy)$ 
Change in gravitational potential energy of two point masses m_1 and m_2 as their separation is changed from r_1 to r_2 is given by
$U\left(r_2\right)-U\left(r_1\right)=G m_1 m_2\left[\frac{1}{r_1}-\frac{1}{r_2}\right]$ 
If, at infinite separation, gravitational potential energy is assumed to be zero, then the gravitational potential energy of the above two point mass system at separation r,
$U(r) = - G\frac{{m_1}{m_2}} {r}$ 
$\therefore V = - G\frac{{m_1}{m_2}} { r}$

questions.question: The minimum velocity with which a body should be projected from the surface of the earth so as it goes out of the gravitational field of earth and never return to earth is known as
questions.question_raw: The minimum velocity with which a body should be projected from the surface of the earth so as it goes out of the gravitational field of earth and never return to earth is known as
questions.explanation:
Escape velocity is the minimum velocity with which a body should be projected from the surface of earth so as it goes out of gravitational field of earth and never return to earth.
${V_{escape}} = \sqrt {{{2GM} \over R}} $
V_escape=11184 m/sec approximate to 11.2 km/sec

questions.question: For the earth the escape speed in terms of g, the acceleration due to gravity and R_E, the radius of the earth is
questions.question_raw: For the earth the escape speed in terms of g, the acceleration due to gravity and RE, the radius of the earth is
questions.explanation:
The work done to displace a body from the surface of earth (r = R_e) to infinity ( $r = \infty $ ) is
$W = G{{Mm} \over {R_e{\rm{ }}}}$
If v_e is the required escape velocity then kinetic energy which should be given to the body is ${1 \over 2}{\rm{ }}m{v_e}^2$
$\therefore {1 \over 2}{\rm{ }}m{v_e}^2 = G{{Mm} \over R_e}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_e} = \sqrt {2g{R_e}} $

questions.question: For earth satellites, the centripetal force is provided by
questions.question_raw: For earth satellites, the centripetal force is provided by

options.label: C
options.text: forces exerted by firing rockets on satellite

questions.explanation:
Gravity supplies the necessary centripetal force to hold a satellite in orbit about the earth. Setting the gravity force from the universal law of gravity equal to the required centripetal force yields the equation of motion of satellites in the circular orbit.
i.e., ${F_{gravity}} = {F_{centripetal}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{orbital}} = \sqrt {{{GM} \over r}} $

questions.question: Time period of an earth satellite very close to the surface of earth is given by
questions.question_raw: Time period of an earth satellite very close to the surface of earth is given by
questions.explanation:
It is the time taken by satellite to go once around the earth
$\therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{\text { Circumference of the orbit }}{\text { orbitalvelocity }}$
$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\nu } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{r}{{GM}}} $ ${\text{[As }}\nu {\text{ = }}\sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{r}{\text{ }}} ]$ 
Since Satellite is very close to the surface of earth, so here, we can take r = R (Radius of earth)
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{R^3}}}{{GM}}} $
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{R^3}}}{{g{R^2}}}}$ $[GM = g{R^2}]$
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{R}}}{{g}}}$

questions.question: The total energy of a circularly orbiting satellite is
questions.question_raw: The total energy of a circularly orbiting satellite is
questions.explanation:
When a satellite revolves around a planet in its orbit, it possesses both potential energy (due to its position against the gravitational pull of earth) and kinetic energy (due to orbital motion).
$ \begin{gathered} {\text{Total Energy = Potential Energy(U) + Kinetic Energy(K)}} \hfill \\ {\text{ = }}\frac{{ - GMm}}{r} + \frac{{GMm}}{{2r}} \hfill \\ {\text{ = }}\frac{{ - GMm}}{{2r}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $
Hence we can say that total energy of circularly orbiting satellite is negative.

questions.question: For a circularly orbiting satellite the potential energy:
questions.question_raw: For a circularly orbiting satellite the potential energy:

options.label: A
options.text: is negative, and has a magnitude thrice that of the positive kinetic energy.

options.label: B
options.text: is positive, and having a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.

options.label: C
options.text: is negative, and has a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.

options.label: D
options.text: is negative, and having a magnitude twice that of the negative kinetic energy.

correct_option_texts: is negative, and has a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.
questions.explanation:
In the case of circularly orbiting satellite,
Potential Energy = ${\text{ }}\frac{{ - GMm}}{r} = \frac{{ - {L^2}}}{{m{r^2}}}$
Kinetic Energy = $\frac{G M m}{2 r}=\frac{L^{2}}{2 m r^{2}}$ 
Comparing both, we can say that the potential energy is negative and has a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.

questions.question: For Geostationary Satellites:
questions.question_raw: For Geostationary Satellites:

options.label: A
options.text: a circular orbit is in the equatorial plane of the earth and period of rotation is 24 hrs

options.label: B
options.text: circular orbit is in the equatorial plane of the earth and period of rotation is 34 hrs

options.label: C
options.text: a circular orbit is in the longitudinal plane of the earth and period of rotation is 20 hrs

options.label: D
options.text: circular orbit is in the equatorial plane of the earth and period of rotation is 14 hrs

correct_option_texts: a circular orbit is in the equatorial plane of the earth and period of rotation is 24 hrs
questions.explanation:
If there is a satellite in the equatorial plane rotating in the direction of earth’s rotation from west to east, then for an observer, on the earth, the angular velocity of the satellite will be $({\omega _s} - {\omega _E})$ 
The time interval between the two consecutive appearances overhead will be,
$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{{{\omega _s} - {\omega _E}}} = \frac{{{T_s}{T_E}}}{{{T_E} - {T_s}}}$ 
If ${\omega _s} = {\omega _E}$, T = $\infty$, i.e. satellite will appear stationary relative to earth. Such satellites are called geostationary satellites and will have a period of rotation as the period of rotation of earth i.e 24 Hrs.

questions.question: Geostationary Satellites:
questions.question_raw: Geostationary Satellites:

options.label: A
options.text: are not useful for communication applications

options.label: B
options.text: needless powerful rockets to put them into larger distance orbits

options.label: C
options.text: need more powerful rockets to put them into smaller distance orbits

options.label: D
options.text: need more powerful rockets to put them into larger distance orbits

correct_option_texts: need more powerful rockets to put them into larger distance orbits
questions.explanation: The orbital velocity of the satellite depends on its altitude above Earth. The nearer Earth, the faster the required orbital velocity. At an altitude of 200 kilometers, the required orbital velocity is just over 27,400 kph. To maintain an orbit that is 35,786 km above Earth, the satellite must orbit at a speed of about 11,300 kmph. That orbital speed and distance permit the satellite to make one revolution in 24 hours. To achieve his speed, Judging from the mass of fuel needed, it stands to reason that you'd use a more powerful launch vehicle with a bi-propellant upper stage to get the satellite to GEO.

questions.question: Polar satellites:
questions.question_raw: Polar satellites:

options.label: A
options.text: are low altitude satellites, and go around the poles of the earth in a north-south direction

options.label: B
options.text: are high altitude satellites, and go around the equator of the earth in a north-east direction

options.label: C
options.text: are low altitude satellites, and go around the equator of the earth in a north-east direction

options.label: D
options.text: are high altitude satellites, and go around the equator of the earth in a west-east direction

correct_option_texts: are low altitude satellites, and go around the poles of the earth in a north-south direction
questions.explanation: A polar-orbiting satellite closely parallels the earth's meridian lines. It passes over the north and south poles each revolution. As the earth rotates to the east beneath the satellite, each pass monitors an area to the west of the previous pass at intervals of roughly 90 to 100 minutes. These strips can be pieced together to produce a picture of a larger area. They are used in monitoring the weather, observing the Earth’s surface, military uses including spying.

questions.question: Which of the following statements correct?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following statements correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Acceleration due to gravity is independent of mass of the earth.

options.label: B
options.text: The formula -G Mm$(\frac {1}{r_2}- \frac {1}{r_1})$ is less accurate than the formula mg(r_2 - r_1) for the difference of potential energy.

options.label: C
options.text: Acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing altitude.

options.label: D
options.text: Acceleration due to gravity increases with increasing depth .

correct_option_texts: Acceleration due to gravity decreases with increasing altitude.
questions.explanation:
The acceleration due to gravity due to a body at point P on the surface of earth is
g = $ G{M \over {{R^2}}}$ ...(i)
Let the body is placed to point Q at a height h abovr the surface of earth, then acceleration due to gravity:
g_h = $ G{M \over {{{(R + h)}^2}}}$ ...(ii)
Dividing (i) & (ii), we get
${{{g_h}} \over g} = {{{R^2}} \over {{{(R + h)}^2}}}$
By simplifying and expanding through binomial theorem, we get
g_h = $ g(1 - {{2h} \over R})$
From above expression we can clearly say that with increase in altitiude h, the value of acceleration due to gravity decreases.

questions.question: Suppose there existed a planet that went around the sun twice as fast as the earth. What would be its orbital size as compared to that of the earth?
questions.question_raw: Suppose there existed a planet that went around the sun twice as fast as the earth. What would be its orbital size as compared to that of the earth?
questions.explanation:
The time period of revolution of the earth around sun T_e = 1 Year
The time period of revolution of the planet around the sun, T_p = 0.5 Year
Orbital size of earth, r_e = 1 A.U
Orbital size of the planet,r_p =?
Applying Kepler's third law we get: 
${{{r_p}^3} \over {{r_e}^3}} = {{{T_p}^2} \over {{T_e}^2}}$ $ \Rightarrow {{{r_p}^3} \over {{r_e}^3}} = {\left( {{{{1 \over 2}} \over 1}} \right)^2} = {1 \over 4}$ 
$ \Rightarrow {{{r_p}} \over {{r_e}}} = {\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)^{1 \over3}}$
$\Rightarrow {r_p} = {\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)^{1\over 3}}{r_e}$
As r_e= 1 A.U (Given)
$ \Rightarrow {r_p} = {\left( {{1 \over 4}} \right)^{1\over 3}}$
$\Rightarrow$ r_p = 0.63 A.U.

questions.question: A typical adult human has a mass of about 70 kg. What force does a full moon exert on such a human when it is directly overhead with its centre 378,000 km away?
questions.question_raw: A typical adult human has a mass of about 70 kg. What force does a full moon exert on such a human when it is directly overhead with its centre 378,000 km away?
questions.explanation:
The force exerted by the full moon on the human
$F = G\frac{{Mm}}{{{r^2}}}$ 
Here G is the gravitational constant, m is the mass of the human, M is the mass of the moon. r is the separation distance between moon and human.
Substitute Value Of G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11N.m^2/Kg^2, m = 70 kg, M = 7.35 $\times$ 10^22, r = 3.78 $\times$ 10^8
$\Rightarrow $ F = $\frac{\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11} N . m^{2} / K g^{2}\right) \times(70 K g) \times\left(7.35 \times 10^{22} K g\right)}{\left(3.78 \times 10^{8} m\right)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow $$F=\frac{3.43 \times 10^{3} \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{22}}{14.29 \times 10^{16}} N$
$\Rightarrow$ F = 2.4 $\times$ 10^−3N

questions.question: A particle of mass 3m is located 1.00 m from a particle of mass m. Where should you put a third mass M so that the net gravitational force on M due to the two masses is exactly zero?
questions.question_raw: A particle of mass 3m is located 1.00 m from a particle of mass m. Where should you put a third mass M so that the net gravitational force on M due to the two masses is exactly zero?
questions.explanation:
Let x be the distance of the third particle from the mass 3m. then (1 - x) is the distance will be the distance from mass m.
If we require that net force on another mass M be zero, then we must have the following:
$\frac{{GM(3m)}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{GM(m)}}{{{{\left( {1 - x} \right)}^2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{3}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {1 - x} \right)}^2}}}$
$\Rightarrow {x^2} = 3 \times {\left( {1 - x} \right)^2}$
$\Rightarrow x = \sqrt {3 \times {{\left( {1 - x} \right)}^2}} = \sqrt 3 (1 - x) $
$\Rightarrow x = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{{1 + \sqrt 3 }} = 0.63$
$\therefore$ The particle is 0.63 m away from 3m.

questions.question: At what distance above the surface of the earth is the acceleration due to the earth’s gravity 0.980 m/s^2 if the acceleration due to gravity at the surface has magnitude 9.8 m/s^2.
questions.question_raw: At what distance above the surface of the earth is the acceleration due to the earth’s gravity 0.980 m/s2 if the acceleration due to gravity at the surface has magnitude 9.8 m/s2.
questions.explanation:
We know:
$g^{\prime}=g\left(\frac{R^{2}}{(R+h)^{2}}\right)$
or 
g' = $g\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$
where R = Radius of earth = 6371 km = 6371$\times$ 10^3 m
r = R + h
g = Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth
g′ = Acceleration due to gravity at height h
Putting above values in the relation, we get:
$r^{2}=\frac{9.8 \times\left(6371 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2}}{0.98}$
$\Rightarrow r=\sqrt{10} \times 6371 \times 10^{3}$
$\Rightarrow$ r = 20146.87 $\times$ 10^3
We know r = R + h
$\Rightarrow$ h = r - R
$\Rightarrow h=20132.36 \times 10^{3}-6371 \times 10^{3}$
$\Rightarrow$ h = 10^3(20132.36 - 6371)
$\Rightarrow$ h = 13761.36 $\times$ 10^3 = 1.37 $\times$ 10^7m

questions.question: Titania, the largest moon of the planet Uranus, has $\frac 1 8$ the radius of the earth and $\frac {1} {1700}$ the mass of the earth. What is the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of Titania? Data: G = 6.67$ \times $ 10^-11 N m$^{\rm{2}}$/kg$^{\rm{2}}$, R_E = 6.38 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{6}}}$m, m_E = 5.97 $ \times $ 10^24 kg.
questions.question_raw: Titania, the largest moon of the planet Uranus, has {tex}\frac 1 8{/tex} the radius of the earth and {tex}\frac {1} {1700}{/tex} the mass of the earth. What is the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of Titania? Data: G = 6.67{tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 N m{tex}^{\rm{2}}{/tex}/kg{tex}^{\rm{2}}{/tex}, RE = 6.38 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{6}}}{/tex}m, mE = 5.97 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg.
questions.explanation:
$g_{t}=G \frac{M_{t}}{R_{t}^{2}}, g_{E}=G \frac{M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}=9.8 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow g_{t}=G \frac{\left(\frac{M_{E}}{1700}\right)}{\left(\frac{R_{E}}{8}\right)^{2}} $
$=\frac{64}{1700} G \frac{M_{E}}{R_{E}^{2}}=\frac{64}{1700} \times g_{E}$
= 0.37m/s^2

questions.question: Titania, the largest moon of the planet Uranus, has $\frac 1 8$ the radius of the earth and $\frac {1} {1700}$ the mass of the earth. What is the average density of Titania? Data: G = 6.67$ \times $ 10^-11 Nm$^{\rm{2}}$/kg$^{\rm{2}}$, R_E = 6.38 $ \times $ 10^6 m, ${{\rm{M}}_{\rm{E}}}$ = 5.97 $ \times $ 10^24kg.
questions.question_raw: Titania, the largest moon of the planet Uranus, has {tex}\frac 1 8{/tex} the radius of the earth and {tex}\frac {1} {1700}{/tex} the mass of the earth. What is the average density of Titania? Data: G = 6.67{tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 Nm{tex}^{\rm{2}}{/tex}/kg{tex}^{\rm{2}}{/tex}, RE = 6.38 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 m, {tex}{{\rm{M}}_{\rm{E}}}{/tex} = 5.97 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024kg.
questions.explanation:
The density of Titania = $\frac{{{\text{Mass Of Titania}}}}{{{\text{Volume Of Titania}}}} = \frac{{\frac{{{{\text{M}}_E}}}{{1700}}}}{{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\left( {\frac{{{R_E}}}{8}} \right)}^3}}}$
Here, mass of earth (M_E = 5.97 $\times$ 10^24kg) and radius of earth(R_E = 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m) 
$= \frac{{\frac{{{\text{5}}{\text{.97}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{24}}}}{{1700}}kg}}{{\frac{4}{3}\pi {{\left( {\frac{{6.38 \times {{10}^6}}}{8}m} \right)}^3}}} $
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{3}}{\text{.5}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{21}}kg}}{{\frac{4}{3} \times 3.14 \times {{\left( {0.79 \times {{10}^6}m} \right)}^3}}}$
$= \frac{{{\text{3}}{\text{.5}} \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{21}}kg}}{{2.064 \times {{10}^{18}}{m^3}}}$
= 1.7 $\times$ 10^3kg/m^3 = 1700 kg/m^3

questions.question: A rocket is fired from the earth towards the sun. At what distance from the earth’s centre is the gravitational force on the rocket zero? Mass of the sun = 2 $ \times $ 10^30 kg, mass of the earth = 6 $ \times $ 10^24 kg. Neglect the effect of other planets etc. (orbital radius = 1.5 $ \times $ 10^11 m).
questions.question_raw: A rocket is fired from the earth towards the sun. At what distance from the earth’s centre is the gravitational force on the rocket zero? Mass of the sun = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1030 kg, mass of the earth = 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg. Neglect the effect of other planets etc. (orbital radius = 1.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 1011 m).
questions.explanation:
Mass of the Sun,M_s = 2 $\times$ 10^30 kg
Mass of the Earth, M_e = 6 $\times$ 10^24 kg
Orbital radius, r = 1.5 $\times$ 10^11 m
Mass Of rocket = m
Let x be the distance from the centre of the earth where the gravitational forces acting on satellite P under the influence of Sun & the Earth as:
$G \frac{M_{s} m}{(r-x)^{2}}=G \frac{M_{e} m}{x^{2}}$
$[{(r-x) \over x}]^{2}=\frac{M_{s}}{M_{e}}$
$\frac{(r-x)}{x}=\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{30}}{6 \times 10^{24}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}$ = 577.35
$\Rightarrow$ 1.5 $\times$ 10^11 − x = 577.35x
$\Rightarrow$ 577.35x = 1.5 $\times$ 10^11
$\Rightarrow x=\frac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{578.35}$ = 2.6 $\times$ 10^8m

questions.question: How will you ‘weigh the sun’, that is estimate its mass? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is 1.5 $ \times $ 10^8 km.
questions.question_raw: How will you ‘weigh the sun’, that is estimate its mass? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is 1.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 km.
questions.explanation:
R = Radius Of Orbit Of Earth = 1.5 $\times$ 10^8 km = 1.5 $\times$ 10^11m
T = Time Period Of Earth Around The Sun = 365 Days 
= 365 $\times$ 24 $\times$ 60 $\times$ 60 sec
G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^11 Nm^2kg^-2 From the relation,
$\frac{R}{T^{2}}=\frac{G M_{s}}{4 \pi^{2} R^{2}}$ where M_s = Mass of sun = ?
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{s}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} \mathrm{R}^{3}}{\mathrm{T}^{2} G}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{M}_{s}=\frac{4 \times 9.87 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{11}\right)^{3}}{(365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60)^{2} \times 6.67 \times 10^{-11}} $ kg
$\Rightarrow$ M_s = 2.0 $\times$ 10^30 kg
$\therefore
$ Mass of sun = 2.0 $\times$ 10^30 kg

questions.question: A Saturn year is 29.5 times the earth year. How far is the Saturn from the sun if the earth is 1.50 $ \times $ 10^8 km away from the sun?
questions.question_raw: A Saturn year is 29.5 times the earth year. How far is the Saturn from the sun if the earth is 1.50 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 km away from the sun?
questions.explanation:
The distance of the earth from the sun, re = 1.5 $\times$ 10^8 km = 1.5 $\times$ 10^11m
The time period of the earth = T_e
The time period of the Saturn = T_s = 29.5 T_e
The distance of Saturn from the sun = r_s
From Kepler's third law of planetary motion, we have
$\mathrm{T}=\left(\frac{4 \pi r^{3}}{G M}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$
For Saturn & Sun, we can write
$\frac{r_{s}^{3}}{r_{e}^{3}}=\frac{T_{s}^{2}}{T_{e}^{2}}$
r_s = $r_{e}\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_{s}}{\mathrm{T}_{e}}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}$
$r_{s}=1.5 \times 10^{11}\left(\frac{29.5 \mathrm{T}_{e}}{\mathrm{T}_{e}}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}}$
= 1.5 $\times$ 10^11$(29.5)^{2\over3}$^_ = 14.32 $\times$ 10^11m
Hence, the distance between Saturn and the sun is 1.43 $\times$ 10^12m

questions.question: A body weighs 63 N on the surface of the earth. What is the gravitational force on it due to the earth at a height equal to half the radius of the earth?
questions.question_raw: A body weighs 63 N on the surface of the earth. What is the gravitational force on it due to the earth at a height equal to half the radius of the earth?
questions.explanation:
${g_h} = g{\left( {1 + \frac{h}{R}} \right)^{ - 2}}$
Given, h = $\frac{R}{2}$
$ \therefore {g_h} = g{\left( {1 + \frac{{\frac{{\text{R}}}{2}}}{R}} \right)^{ - 2}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{{{g_h}}}{g} = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {1 + \frac{1}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{1}{{{{\left( {\frac{3}{2}} \right)}^2}}} = {\left( {\frac{2}{3}} \right)^2} = \frac{4}{9}$
Let m = mass of the body
If W and W_h be its weight at earth's surface and at a height h above the earth's surface, then
W = mg
${\text{and }}{{\text{W}}_h} = m{g_h} = m \times \frac{4}{9}g = \frac{4}{9}mg$
$ \Rightarrow {{\text{W}}_h} = m{g_h} = \frac{4}{9} \times 63 $ = 28N

questions.question: A body weighed 250 N on the surface. Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would it weigh halfway down to the centre of earth?
questions.question_raw: A body weighed 250 N on the surface. Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would it weigh halfway down to the centre of earth?
questions.explanation:
Weight of a body of mass m at the surface of earth's surface, W = mg = 250 N.
Body of mass m is located at depth,
d = $\frac{1}{2}$Re
where Re = Radius of earth
Acceleration due to gravity at depth g(d)is given by the relation:
g(d) = $\left(1-\frac{d}{R_{e}}\right) g$
$=\left(1-\frac{R_{e}}{2 \times R_{e}}\right) g=\frac{1}{2} g$
Weight of the body at depth d,
W' = mg(d)
$=\mathrm{m} \times \frac{1}{2} g=\frac{1}{2} m g=\frac{1}{2} W$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 250$ = 125N

questions.question: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of 5 km sec^-1 ^ ^ from the earth’s surface. How far from the earth does the rocket go before returning to the earth? Mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; mean radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^-11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of 5 km sec-1   from the earth’s surface. How far from the earth does the rocket go before returning to the earth? Mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; mean radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106  m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Work done in bringing a mass m against a mass M at a distance 'x' through a distance dx 
= Gravitational force $\times$ Distance = $\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{x^{2}} d x$
Work done in bringing it from $\infty$ to r = GMm$\left[\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{R}\right]$
$=-\mathrm{GMm}\left[\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]$
Therefore Gravitational Potential Energy, $W =\frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{R}$
Initial Total Energy = Kinetic Energy + Potential Energy
$=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{R}$
Final Total Energy = 0 - $\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(R+h)}$
Applying law of conservation of energy:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{R}=0-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(R+h)}$
$\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(R+h)}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{R}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}$
$\frac{G M}{(R+h)}=\frac{1}{R}\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)$
$\frac{(R+h)}{R}=\frac{G M}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)}$
$(R+h)=\frac{G M R}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)}$
h = $\frac{G M R}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Rv}^{2}\right)}-R$
$h=\frac{\frac{1}{2} R^{2} v^{2}}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)}$
$h=\frac{\left[\frac{1}{2} \times\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2} \times\left(5 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2}\right]}{\left[\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{24}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 6.4 \times 10^{6} \times\left(5 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2}\right]\right.} $m
$=\frac{\left(512 \times 10^{18}\right)}{40 \times 10^{13}-80 \times 10^{12}}$
=1.6 × 10^6m
Height achieved by the rocket with respect to the centre of the Earth = Re + h
= 6.4 $\times$ 10^6 + 1.6 $\times$ 10^6 = 8 $\times$ 10^6 m.

questions.question: The escape speed of a projectile on the earth’s surface is 11.2 km sec^-1. A body is projected out with thrice this speed. What is the speed of the body far away from the earth? Ignore the presence of the sun and other planets.
questions.question_raw: The escape speed of a projectile on the earth’s surface is 11.2 km sec-1. A body is projected out with thrice this speed. What is the speed of the body far away from the earth? Ignore the presence of the sun and other planets.
questions.explanation:
The escape velocity of a projectile from the Earth, V_esc = 11.2 km/s
Projection velocity of the projectile, V_p = 3V_esc
Mass of the projectile = m
The velocity of the projectile far away from the Earth = V_f
The total energy of the projectile on the earth$= \frac{1}{2}m{v_p}^2 - \frac{1}{2}m{v_{esc}}^2$ 
The gravitational potential energy of the projectile far away from the Earth is zero.
The total energy of the projectile far away from the Earth = $\frac{1}{2}m{v_f}^2$ 
From the law of conservation of energy, we have
$= \frac{1}{2}m{v_p}^2 - \frac{1}{2}m{v_{esc}}^2 = \frac{1}{2}m{v_f}^2$ 
${v_f} = \sqrt {{v_p}^2 - {v_{esc}}^2}$ 
$ = \sqrt {{{\left( {3{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2}}$ 
$= \sqrt {{{\left( {3{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2}}$
$=\sqrt 8 {v_{esc}}$
$=\sqrt 8 \times 11.2 $ = 31.68 km/sec

questions.question: A satellite orbits the earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. How much energy must be expended to rocket the satellite out of the earth’s gravitational influence? Mass of the satellite = 200 kg; mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^-11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A satellite orbits the earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. How much energy must be expended to rocket the satellite out of the earth’s gravitational influence? Mass of the satellite = 200 kg; mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Mass of the Earth, M = 6.0 $\times$ 10^24kg
Mass of the satellite, m = 200 kg
Radius of the Earth, Re = 6.4 $\times$ 10^6m Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11N m^2 kg^−2
Height of the satellite, h = 400 km = 4 $\times$ 10^5m = 0.4 $\times$ 106m
Total energy of the satellite at height = $1\over2$mv^2 + $\left(\frac{-G M_{e} m}{R_{e}+h}\right)$
Orbital velocity of the satellite, $v=\sqrt{\frac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}+h}}$
Total energy at height, $h=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}+h}\right)-\frac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}+h}=-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-G M_{e} m}{R_{e}+h}\right)$
The negative sign indicates that the satellite is bound to the Earth. 
This is called the bound energy of the satellite. The energy required to send the satellite out of its orbit = ... (Bound energy)
$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_{e} m}{\left(R_{e}+h\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.0 \times 10^{24} \times 200}{\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}+0.4 \times 10^{6}\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6.67 \times 6 \times 2 \times 10^{15}}{6.8 \times 10^{6}}$ = 5.9 $\times$ 10^9J

questions.question: For a satellite to be in a circular orbit 780 km above the surface of the earth, what orbital speed must it be given?
questions.question_raw: For a satellite to be in a circular orbit 780 km above the surface of the earth, what orbital speed must it be given?
questions.explanation:
Mass of the Earth, M_e = $6.0 \times {10^{24}}$kg
The radius of the Earth, Re = $6.4 \times {10^6}$m
Universal gravitational constant, G = $6.67 \times {10^{ - 11}}\;N{\text{ }}{m^2}{\text{ }}k{g^{ - 2}}$
Height of the satellite, h = 780 km = 780 $\times$ 10^3m = 0.78 $\times$ 10^6m
Orbital velocity of the satellite, v = $\sqrt {\frac{{G{M_e}}}{{{R_e} + h}}}$
$= \sqrt {\frac{{6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 6.0 \times {{10}^{24}}}}{{6.4 \times {{10}^6} + 0.78 \times {{10}^6}}}}$
$= \sqrt {\frac{{40 \times {{10}^{13}}}}{{7.18 \times {{10}^6}}}}$
$= \sqrt {5.57 \times {{10}^7}} $
$= {10^3} \times \sqrt {5.57 \times 10}$
$= 7.46 \times {10^3}$
= 7460 m/sec

questions.question: For a satellite to be in a circular orbit 780 km above the surface of the earth, what is the period of the orbit (in hours)?
questions.question_raw: For a satellite to be in a circular orbit 780 km above the surface of the earth, what is the period of the orbit (in hours)?
questions.explanation:
Mass of the Earth, M_e = 6.0 $\times$ 10^24 kg
The radius of the Earth, Re =$ \;6.4 \times {10^6}m$
Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11Nm^2 kg^-2
Height of the satellite, h = 780 km = 780 $\times$ 10^3m = 0.78 $\times$ 10^6 m
Time Period of the satellite, T = $2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{{({R_e} + h)}^3}}}{{G{M_e}}}}$
$= 2 \times \frac{{22}}{7} \times \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {6.4 \times {{10}^6} + 0.78 \times {{10}^6}} \right)}^3}}}{{6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 6.0 \times {{10}^{24}}}}}$
$= 2 \times \frac{{22}}{7} \times \sqrt {\frac{{{{\left( {7.18 \times {{10}^6}} \right)}^3}}}{{40 \times {{10}^{13}}}}}$
$= 2 \times \frac{{22}}{7} \times \sqrt {9 \times {{10}^5}}$
$= 2 \times \frac{{22}}{7} \times 948$
= 2 $\times$ 22 $\times$ 135.42
= 5958.85 sec = 1.65 hr

questions.question: Two satellites are in circular orbits around a planet that has radius 9.00 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{6}}}$ m. One satellite has mass 68.0 kg, orbital radius 5.00 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{7}}}$ m, and orbital speed 4800 m/s. The second satellite has a mass 84.0 kg and an orbital radius of 3.00 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{7}}}$ m. What is the orbital speed of this second satellite?
questions.question_raw: Two satellites are in circular orbits around a planet that has radius 9.00 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{6}}}{/tex} m. One satellite has mass 68.0 kg, orbital radius 5.00 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{7}}}{/tex} m, and orbital speed 4800 m/s. The second satellite has a mass 84.0 kg and an orbital radius of 3.00 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{\rm{7}}}{/tex} m. What is the orbital speed of this second satellite?
questions.explanation:
We know $T=\frac{2 \pi R}{v}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\left(\frac{2 \pi R_{1}}{v_{1}}\right)^{2}}{R_{1}^{3}}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 \pi R_{2}}{v_{2}}\right)^{2}}{R_{2}^{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{4 \pi^{2}}{v_{1}^{2} R_{1}}=\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{v_{2}^{2} R_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow v_{1}^{2} R_{1}=v_{2}^{2} R_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{v_{1}^{2} R_1}{R_{2}}=v_{2}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{v_{1}^{2} R_1}{R_{2}}}=v_{2}$
Here v_1 = 4800 m/sec; R_1 = 5 $\times$ 10^7m, R_2 = 3 $\times$ 10^7m, v^2 = ?
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{\frac{(4800)^{2} \times 5 \times 10^{7}}{3 \times 10^{7}}}=v_{2}$
$\Rightarrow$ 4800 $\times$ 1.29 = 6192 m/sec.

questions.question: Deimos, a moon of Mars, is about 12 km in diameter with mass 2.0 $ \times $ 10^15 kg. Suppose you are stranded alone on Deimos and want to play a one-person game of baseball. You would be the pitcher, and you would be the batter! With what speed would you have to throw a baseball so that it would go into a circular orbit just above the surface and return to you so you could hit it?
questions.question_raw: Deimos, a moon of Mars, is about 12 km in diameter with mass 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1015 kg. Suppose you are stranded alone on Deimos and want to play a one-person game of baseball. You would be the pitcher, and you would be the batter! With what speed would you have to throw a baseball so that it would go into a circular orbit just above the surface and return to you so you could hit it?
questions.explanation:
$v=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{r}}$
Here, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11N m^2 kg^−2
M = 2 $\times$ 10^15 kg
$r=\frac{12000}{2}$ = 6000
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2 \times 10^{15}}{6000}}$
$\Rightarrow$ v = 4.7 m/sec

questions.question: Deimos, a moon of Mars, is about 12 km in diameter with mass 2.0 $ \times $ 10^15 kg. Suppose you are stranded alone on Deimos and want to play a one-person game of baseball. You would be the pitcher, and you would be the batter! You have to throw a baseball so that it would go into a circular orbit just above the surface and return to you so you could hit it. How long (in hours) after throwing the ball should you be ready to hit it?
questions.question_raw: Deimos, a moon of Mars, is about 12 km in diameter with mass 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1015 kg. Suppose you are stranded alone on Deimos and want to play a one-person game of baseball. You would be the pitcher, and you would be the batter! You have to throw a baseball so that it would go into a circular orbit just above the surface and return to you so you could hit it. How long (in hours) after throwing the ball should you be ready to hit it?
questions.explanation:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{r^{3}}{G M}}$
Here,G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11Nm^2kg^−2
M = 2 $\times$ 10^15 kg
$r=\frac{12000}{2}$ = 6000m
$\Rightarrow T=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \sqrt{\frac{(6000)^{3}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2 \times 10^{15}}}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \sqrt{\frac{216 \times 10^{9}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2 \times 10^{15}}}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \sqrt{\frac{216 \times 10^{9}}{13.34 \times 10^{4}}}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \sqrt{\frac{216 \times 10^{9}}{13.34 \times 10^{4}}}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \sqrt{16.2 \times 10^{5}}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{6}}$
$\Rightarrow$ T = 2 $\times$ $\frac{{22}}{7}$ $\times$ 1.26 $\times$10^3
$\Rightarrow$ T = 7920 sec 
$\Rightarrow$ T = 2.2 hr

questions.question: Two stars each of one solar mass (= 2 $ \times $ 10^30 kg) are approaching each other for a head-on collision. When they are a distance 10^9 km, their speeds are negligible. What is the speed with which they collide? The radius of each star is 10^4 km. assume the stars to remain undistorted until they collide. (Use the known value of G).
questions.question_raw: Two stars each of one solar mass (= 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1030 kg) are approaching each other for a head-on collision. When they are a distance 109 km, their speeds are negligible. What is the speed with which they collide? The radius of each star is 104 km. assume the stars to remain undistorted until they collide. (Use the known value of G).
questions.explanation:
Let $\vec v_1$ and $\vec v_2$ be the velocities of two stars when they collide. 
According to the law of conservation of momentum,
$M{{\vec v}_1} + M{{\vec v}_2} = 0$
$\Rightarrow {{\vec v}_1} = - {{\vec v}_2}$
$\Rightarrow {v_1} = {v_2} = v$
According to law of conservation of energy,
${\text{2}}\left[ {\frac{1}{2}M{v^2}} \right] = G{M^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{r_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_1}}}} \right]$
We Have
Mass of the star,M = 2 $\times$ 10^30 kg
Distance between the stars, r_1 = 10^9 km = 10^12 m
Radius of star, r = 10^4km = 10^7m
The distance between two stars when they collide is,
r_2 = 2r = 2 $\times$ 10^7m
$\therefore$ The speed with which the stars collide is given by,
$v = \sqrt {GM\left[ {\frac{1}{{{r_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_1}}}} \right]}$
$v = \sqrt {6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 2 \times {{10}^{30}} \times \left[ {\frac{1}{{2 \times {{10}^7}}} - \frac{1}{{{{10}^{12}}}}} \right]}$
v = 2.6 $\times$ 10^6m/sec

questions.question: Two heavy spheres each of mass 100 kg and radius 0.10 m are placed 1.0 m apart on a horizontal table. What is the gravitational force and potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centers of the spheres? Is an object placed at that point in equilibrium? If so, is the equilibrium stable or unstable?
questions.question_raw: Two heavy spheres each of mass 100 kg and radius 0.10 m are placed 1.0 m apart on a horizontal table. What is the gravitational force and potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centers of the spheres? Is an object placed at that point in equilibrium? If so, is the equilibrium stable or unstable?
questions.explanation:
Here, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11N m^2 kg^−2
M = 100 kg
R = 0.1 m
Distance between the two spheres d = 1.0 m
Suppose that the distance of either sphere from the midpoint of the line joining their centre is r. Then
r = $d\over2$ = 0.5 m
The gravitational field at the midpoint due to two spheres will be equal and opposite.
Hence, the resultant gravitational field at the midpoint = 0
The gravitational potential at the midpoint.
$=\left(\frac{-G M}{r}\right) \times 2$
$=-\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 100 \times 2}{0.5}$ 
= −2.7 $\times$ 10^−8 Jkg^-1
As the effective force on the body placed at the midpoint is zero, so the body is in equilibrium, if the body is displaced a little towards either mass body from its equilibrium position, it will not return to its initial position of equilibrium. Hence, the body is in unstable equilibrium.

questions.question: A geostationary satellite orbits the earth at a height of nearly 36,000 km from the surface of the earth. What is the potential due to the earth’s gravity at the site of this satellite? (Take the potential energy at infinity to be zero).Mass of the earth = 6.0$ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{24}}}}$kg, radius = 6400 km.
questions.question_raw: A geostationary satellite orbits the earth at a height of nearly 36,000 km from the surface of the earth. What is the potential due to the earth’s gravity at the site of this satellite? (Take the potential energy at infinity to be zero).Mass of the earth = 6.0{tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{24}}}}{/tex}kg, radius = 6400 km.
questions.explanation:
Mass of the earth, M = 6.0 $\times$ 10^24kg.
Radius of the earth, R = 6400 km = 6.4 $\times$ 10^6m.
Height of a geostationary satellite from the surface of the earth,
h = 36000 km = 3.6 × 10^7 m
Gravitational potential energy due to earth's gravity at height h,
${\text{ = }} - \frac{{GM}}{{(R + h)}}$
$= - \frac{{6.67 \times {\text{1}}{{\text{0}}^{ - 11}} \times 6 \times {{10}^{24}}}}{{3.6 \times {{10}^7} + 0.64 \times {{10}^7}}}$
$= - \frac{{6.67 \times 6 \times {{10}^6}}}{{4.24}} = - 9.4 \times {10^6}{\text{ J/kg}}$

questions.question: A spaceship is stationed on Mars. How much energy must be expended on the spaceship to launch it out of the solar system? Mass of the space ship = 1000 kg; the mass of the sun = 2 $ \times $ 10^30 kg; mass of mars = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^23 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; radius of the orbit of mars = 2.28 $ \times $ 10^8 km; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A spaceship is stationed on Mars. How much energy must be expended on the spaceship to launch it out of the solar system? Mass of the space ship = 1000 kg; the mass of the sun = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1030 kg; mass of mars = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 1023 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; radius of the orbit of mars = 2.28 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 km; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 1011 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Mass of the spaceship is given by, m_s = 1000 kg
Mass of the Sun is given by, M = 2 $\times$ 10^30kg
Mass of Mars is given by, M_m = 6.4 $\times$ 10^23kg
Orbital radius of Mars, r = 3395 km = 3.395 $\times$ 10^6m
Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11 Nm^2kg^-2
Potential energy of the spaceship due to gravitational attraction of the Sun = - $\frac{GM{m_s}}{R}$
Potential energy of the spaceship due to gravitational attraction of the Mars = - $\frac{{G{M_m}{m_s}}}{r}$
Since the spaceship is stationed on Mars, its velocity and hence, its kinetic energy will be zero.
Total energy of the spaceship = $- \frac{{GM{m_s}}}{R}{\text{ - }}\frac{{G{M_m}{m_s}}}{r}$
$= - G{m_s}\left[ {\frac{M}{R} + \left( {\frac{{{M_m}}}{r}} \right)} \right]$
The negative sign indicates that the system is in bound state.
Energy required for launching the spaceship out of the solar system
= - Total energy of the spaceship = $G{m_s}\left[ {\frac{M}{R} + \left( {\frac{{{M_m}}}{r}} \right)} \right] $$= 6.67 \times {10^{ - 11}} \times {10^3} \times \left( {\frac{{2 \times {{10}^{30}}}}{{2.28 \times {{10}^{11}}}} + \frac{{6.4 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{{3.395 \times {{10}^6}}}} \right) $
= 6.67 $\times$ 10^-8 (87.72 $\times$ 10^17 + 1.88 $\times$ 10^17) 
= 6.67 $\times$ 10^-8 $\times$ 89.50 $\times$ 10^17
= 596.96 $\times$ 10^9
= 5.96 $\times$ 10^11 J

questions.question: A rocket is fired ‘vertically’ from the surface of mars with a speed of 2 km s^−1. If 20 percent of its initial energy is lost due to Martian atmospheric resistance, how far will the rocket go from the surface of Mars before returning to it? Mass of mars = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^23 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^−11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A rocket is fired ‘vertically’ from the surface of mars with a speed of 2 km s−1. If 20 percent of its initial energy is lost due to Martian atmospheric resistance, how far will the rocket go from the surface of Mars before returning to it? Mass of mars = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 1023 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity of the rocket, v = 2 km/s = 2 $\times$ 10^3 m/s
Mass of Mars, M = 6.4 $\times$ 10^23 kg
Radius of Mars, R = 3395 km = 3.395 $\times$ 10^6 m
Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11 N m^2 kg^−2
Mass of the rocket = m , Initial kinetic energy of the rocket = $1\over2$mv^2
Initial potential energy of the rocket = $-GMm\over r$
Total initial energy = $1\over 2$mv^2 - $GMm\over r$
If 20% of initial kinetic energy is lost due to Martian atmospheric resistance, then only 80 % of its kinetic energy helps in reaching a height.
Total initial energy available = $\frac{80}{100} \times \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}=0.4 m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}$
The maximum height reached by rocket = h
At this height, the velocity and hence, the kinetic energy of the rocket will become zero. The total energy of the rocket at height h = $-G M m \over {R + h }$
Applying the law of conservation of energy for the rocket, we can write: 
$0.4 m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}=\frac{-G M m}{(R+h)}$
$0.4 m v^{2}=\frac{G M m}{R}-\frac{G M m}{(R+h)}$
$0.4 v^2=\operatorname{GM}\left(\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{(R+h)}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{R+h}{h}=\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}$
$\frac{R}{h}+1=\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}$
$\frac{R}{h}=\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}-1$
$h=\frac{R}{\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}-1}$
$h=\frac{0.4 v^{2} R^{2}}{G M-0.4 v^{2} R}$
$=\frac{0.4 \times (2 \times 10^3)^2\times \left(3.395 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2} }{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.4 \times 10^{23}-0.4 \times (2\times 10^3)^2 \times \left(3.395 \times 10^{6}\right) }$
$=\frac{18.442 \times 10^{18}}{42.688 \times 10^{12}-5.432 \times 10^{12}}=\frac{18.442}{37.256} \times 10^{6}$
= 495 $\times$ 10^3m = 495 km

questions.question: The star Cancri is 57 light-years from the earth and has a mass of 0.85 times that of our sun. A planet has been detected in a circular orbit around Cancri with an orbital radius equal to 0.11 times the radius of the earth’s orbit around the sun. What is the orbital speed? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is 1.5 $ \times $ 10^8 km.
questions.question_raw: The star Cancri is 57 light-years from the earth and has a mass of 0.85 times that of our sun. A planet has been detected in a circular orbit around Cancri with an orbital radius equal to 0.11 times the radius of the earth’s orbit around the sun. What is the orbital speed? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is 1.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 km.
questions.explanation:
$v=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{r}}$
G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11Nm^2kg^−2
Mass of sun = Ms = 1.99 $\times$ 10^30kg
Mass of planet which revolve around star Cancri,
M = 0.85 $\times$ 1.99 $\times$ 10^30 kg
r = 0.11 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 10^8km = 0.11 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$ 10^11m
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.85 \times 1.99 \times 10^{30}}{0.11 \times 1.5 \times 10^{11}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ v = 8.269 $\times$ 10^4 m/sec = 82700 m/sec

questions.question_raw: The star Cancri is 57 light-years from the earth and has a mass of 0.85 times that of our sun. A planet has been detected in a circular orbit around Cancri with an orbital radius equal to 0.11 times the radius of the earth’s orbit around the sun. What is the orbital period of the planet of Cancri? The mean orbital radius of the earth around the sun is 1.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 km.
questions.explanation:
$v=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{r}}$
G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11Nm^2kg^−2
Mass of sun = M_s = 1.99 $\times$ 10^30kg
Mass of planet which revolve around star Cancri,
M = 0.85 $\times$ 1.99 $\times$10^30kg ,r = 0.11 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$10^8km = 0.11 $\times$ 1.5 $\times$10^11mṁ
