depression at midpoint $x ={ l ({Mg\over {YA}})}^{1/3}$
$x = 0.5 ({0.1 \times 10 \over (2 \times 10 ^11)(0.50 \times 10 ^{-6})})^{1/3}$
x = 1.074 $\times$ 10^-2 m

questions.question: Two strips of metal are riveted together at their ends by four rivets, each of diameter 6.0 mm. What is the maximum tension that can be exerted by the riveted strip if the shearing stress on the rivet is not to exceed 6.9 $ \times $ 10^7 Pa? Assume that each rivet is to carry one-quarter of the load:
questions.question_raw: Two strips of metal are riveted together at their ends by four rivets, each of diameter 6.0 mm. What is the maximum tension that can be exerted by the riveted strip if the shearing stress on the rivet is not to exceed 6.9 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 Pa? Assume that each rivet is to carry one-quarter of the load:
questions.explanation:
if the riveted strip is subjected to a stretching load W, the tensile force i.e. tension in each strip(equal to W)
let A be the area of each rivet on which the shearing force acts.
shearing stress on each rivet = $\frac{\text {shearing force}}{\text {area}}$= $\frac{W}{4 A}$
let W_max be the maximum permissible tension exerted by the riveted strip then $\frac{W_{\max }}{4 A}$ = $6.9 \times 10^{7} \Rightarrow W_{\max }$ = 4 A $\times$ 6.9 $\times$ 10^7 $\Rightarrow$ (1)
Given that diameter of each rivet d = 6.0 mm
area A = $\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{4}=\frac{3.14 \times\left(6 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4}$
A = 28.26 $\times$ 10^−6m^2
substitute in equation 1
W_max = 4 $\times$ 28.26 $\times$ 10^−6 $\times$ 6.9 $\times$ 10^7
W_max = 7.8 $\times$ 10^3 N

questions.question: The Marina trench is located in the Pacific Ocean, and in one place it is nearly eleven km beneath the surface of the water. The water pressure at the bottom of the trench is about 1.1 $ \times $ 10^8 Pa. A steel ball of initial volume 0.32 m^3 is dropped into the ocean and falls to the bottom of the trench. What is the change in the volume of the ball when it reaches the bottom? The bulk modulus of steel = 160 GPa.
questions.question_raw: The Marina trench is located in the Pacific Ocean, and in one place it is nearly eleven km beneath the surface of the water. The water pressure at the bottom of the trench is about 1.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 Pa. A steel ball of initial volume 0.32 m3 is dropped into the ocean and falls to the bottom of the trench. What is the change in the volume of the ball when it reaches the bottom? The bulk modulus of steel = 160 GPa.
questions.explanation:
bulk modulus is given by $B=\frac{\Delta P}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}} \Rightarrow \Delta V=\frac{\Delta P V}{B}$ $\Rightarrow$ (1)
volume of ball = 0.32 m^3
given water pressure at bottom = 1.1 $\times $ 10^8 pa
depth of marina trench = 11000 km bulk modulus of ball B = 160 Gpa = 160 $\times$ 10^9 pa
pressure at top of surface will be atmospheic pressure p_0 = P_atm
pressure at bottom will be p_2 = P_atm + P_water = P_atm + (1.1 $\times$ 10^8)
$\Delta P=p_{2}-p_{1}$ = 1.1 $\times$ 10^8pa
substitute values in equation 1
$\Delta V=\frac{1.1 \times 10^{8} \times 0.32}{160 \times 10^{9}}$
$\Delta V=2.2 \times 10^{-4} $m^3

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Mechanical Properties of Solids

questions.question: What diameter should a 10-m-long steel wire have if we do not want it to stretch more than 0.5 cm under a tension of 940 N? Take Young's modulus of steel as 20 $ \times $ 10^10 Pa.
questions.question_raw: What diameter should a 10-m-long steel wire have if we do not want it to stretch more than 0.5 cm under a tension of 940 N? Take Young's modulus of steel as 20 {tex} \times {/tex} 1010 Pa.
questions.explanation:
Given length of wire L = 10 m
elongation in wire $\Delta L$ = 0.5 cm = 0.5 $\times$ 10^-2 m
restoring force in wire i.e. tension T = 940 N
young modulus of wire Y = 20 $\times$ 10^10N/m^2
using relation for young modulus $Y = \frac{s t r e s s}{s t r a i n} = \frac{T \times L}{A \times \Delta L}$
$A = \frac{T \times L}{Y \times \Delta L} = \frac{940 \times 10}{20 \times 10^{10} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} = 940 \times 10^{-8}$
R = 17.30 $\times$ 10^-4
Diameter d = 2 R $\Rightarrow$ 2 $\times$ 17.30 $\times$ 10^-4
d = 34.60 $\times$ 10^-4 m $\approx$ 3.4 mm

questions.question: A solid brass sphere is initially surrounded by air, and the air pressure exerted on it is 1.0 $ \times $ 10^5N/m^2 (normal atmospheric pressure). The sphere is lowered into the ocean to a depth at which the pressure is 2.0 $ \times $ 10^7 N/m^2. The volume of the sphere in the air is 0.50 m^3. By how much does this volume change once the sphere is submerged? ( take the modulus of brass as 61 GPa.)
questions.question_raw: A solid brass sphere is initially surrounded by air, and the air pressure exerted on it is 1.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 105N/m2 (normal atmospheric pressure). The sphere is lowered into the ocean to a depth at which the pressure is 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 107 N/m2. The volume of the sphere in the air is 0.50 m3. By how much does this volume change once the sphere is submerged? ( take the modulus of brass as 61 GPa.)
questions.explanation:
Given initial pressure p_1 = 1.0 $\times$ 10^5 N/m^2
final pressure p_2 = 2.0 $\times$ 10^7 N/m^2
volume of sphere V = 0.50 m^3
bulk modulus B of brass = 61 Gpa = $61 \times 10^{9}$ pa
bulk modulus is given by $B=-\frac{\Delta p}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$
$\Delta V=-\frac{\Delta p \times V}{B}=-\frac{\left(p_{2}-p_{1}\right) \times V}{B}$ = $-\frac{\left(2.0 \times 10^{7}-1.0 \times 10^{5}\right) \times 0.50}{61 \times 10^{9}} $
$\Delta V=-1.6 \times 10^{-4} m^3$

questions.question: A 200-kg load is hung on a wire with a length of 4.00 m, a cross-sectional area of 0.200 $ \times $ 10^−4 m^2, and Young’s modulus of 8.00 $ \times $ 10^10 N/m^2. What is its increase in length?
questions.question_raw: A 200-kg load is hung on a wire with a length of 4.00 m, a cross-sectional area of 0.200 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−4 m2, and Young’s modulus of 8.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 1010 N/m2. What is its increase in length?
questions.explanation:
Given load hung to a wire = 200 kg thus force applied to wire = weight W = 200 $\times$ g
W = 200 $\times$ 9.8 = 1960 N
at equilibrium restoring force = applied force
thus, tension T = 1960 N
length of wire L = 4.0 m
cross sectional area of wire = 0.200 $\times$ 10^-4 m^2
young modulus of wire Y = 8.00 $\times$ 10^10 N/m^2
also $Y = \frac{stress}{strain} = \frac{T \times L}{A \times \Delta L} $
$\Delta L$ = $\frac{T \times L}{A \times Y} = \frac{1960 \times 4.0}{0.200 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.00 \times 10^{10}} $
$\Delta L$ = 4.90 mm

questions.question: A child slides across a floor in a pair of rubber-soled shoes. The frictional force acting on each foot is 20.0 N. The footprint area of each shoe’s sole is 14.0 cm^2, and the thickness of each sole is 5.00 mm. Find the horizontal distance by which the upper and lower surfaces of each sole are offset. The shear modulus of the rubber is 3.00 $ \times $ 10^6 N/m^2.
questions.question_raw: A child slides across a floor in a pair of rubber-soled shoes. The frictional force acting on each foot is 20.0 N. The footprint area of each shoe’s sole is 14.0 cm2, and the thickness of each sole is 5.00 mm. Find the horizontal distance by which the upper and lower surfaces of each sole are offset. The shear modulus of the rubber is 3.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 N/m2.
questions.explanation:
let restoring force be T
at equilibrium frictional force = restoring force
thus T = 20 N
area A of each sole = 14.0 cm^2 = 14.0 $\times$ 10^-4 m^2
thickness of each sole H = 5.00 mm = 5.00 $\times$ 10^-3 m
shear modulus of rubber $\eta$ = 3.00 $\times$ 10^6 N/m^2
let distance by which upper and lower surface are offset is x
shear modulus $\eta$ = $\frac{\text {shear stress}}{\text {shear strain}} $ = $ \frac{T \times H}{A \times x}$
x = $\frac{T \times H}{A \times \eta}$ = $\frac{20 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{14 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^{6}}$= 2.38 mm

questions.question: If the elastic limit of copper is 1.50 $ \times $ 10^8 N/m^2, determine the minimum diameter a copper wire can have under a load of 10.0 kg if its elastic limit is not to be exceeded?
questions.question_raw: If the elastic limit of copper is 1.50 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 N/m2, determine the minimum diameter a copper wire can have under a load of 10.0 kg if its elastic limit is not to be exceeded?
questions.explanation:
Given mass to be hung m = 10.0 kg
elastic limit = 1.50 $\times$ 10^8 N/m^2
elastic limit i.e. pessure P = $\frac{\text {force}}{\text {area}} $ $\rightarrow$ (1)
force F = m g and area = $\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$ (r $\rightarrow$ radius of wire)
using 1
1.50 $\times$ 10^8= $\frac{10.0 \times 9.8}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}$
r^2 = 20.80 $\times$ 10^-8
r = 4.56 $\times$ 10^-4 m
d = 2r
d = 0.912 mm

questions.question: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 $ \times $ 10^8 N/m^2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to shear a steel bolt 1.00 cm in diameter?
questions.question_raw: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 N/m2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to shear a steel bolt 1.00 cm in diameter?
questions.explanation:
Given shear stress S = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 N/m^2
diameter d = 1.00 cm
radius r = $d\over 2 $= 0.5cm
area (A) = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.5 $\times$ 10^−2)^2 = 0.785 $\times$ 10^−4m^2
stress S = $force \over area$ $\Rightarrow$ force = S $\times$ area
force F = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 $\times$ 0.785 $\times$ 10^−4 F = 3.14 $\times$ 10^4N

questions.question: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 $ \times $ 10^8 N/m^2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to punch a 1.00-cm-diameter hole in a steel plate 0.500 cm thick?
questions.question_raw: If the shear stress exceeds about 4.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 N/m2, steel ruptures. Determine the shearing force necessary to punch a 1.00-cm-diameter hole in a steel plate 0.500 cm thick?
questions.explanation:
Given shear stress S = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 N/m^2
diameter d = 1.00 cm
radius r = $d\over 2$ = 0.5cm
height h = 0.500 cm
force will be applied on area A = 2 $\pi$rh = 2 $\times $ 3.14 $\times $ (0.5 $\times$ 10^-2) $\times$ 0.5 $\times$ 10^-2 = 1.57 $\times$ 10^-4 m
area A = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.5 $\times$ 10^−2)^2 = 0.785 $\times$ 10^−4m^2
stress S = $force \over area$ $\Rightarrow$ force = S $\times$ area
force F = 4.00 $\times$ 10^8 $\times$ 1.57$\times$ 10^−4 
F = 6.28$\times$ 10^4N

questions.question: When water freezes, it expands by about 9.00%. What would be the pressure increase inside your automobile’s engine block if the water in it froze? (The bulk modulus of ice is 2.00 $ \times $ 10^9 N/m^2.)
questions.question_raw: When water freezes, it expands by about 9.00%. What would be the pressure increase inside your automobile’s engine block if the water in it froze? (The bulk modulus of ice is 2.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 109 N/m2.)
questions.explanation:
Given $\frac{\Delta V}{V}$ = 9% = $\frac{9}{100}$
the bulk modulus of water = 2.00 $\times$ 10 ^9 N/m^2
$B=\frac{\Delta P}{\frac{\Delta V}{V}}$ $\Rightarrow $ $\Delta P=B \times \frac{\Delta V}{V}$
$ΔP$ = 2.00 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ $9\over 100$
$ΔP$ = 1.8 × 10^8N/m^2

questions.question: In a materials testing laboratory, a metal wire made from a new alloy is found to break when a tensile force of 90.8 N is applied perpendicular to each end. If the diameter of the wire is 1.84 mm, what is the breaking stress of the alloy?
questions.question_raw: In a materials testing laboratory, a metal wire made from a new alloy is found to break when a tensile force of 90.8 N is applied perpendicular to each end. If the diameter of the wire is 1.84 mm, what is the breaking stress of the alloy?
questions.explanation:
given tensile force F = 90.8 N
diameter d = 1.84 mm
radius r = 1.84/2 = 0.92 mm = 0.92 $\times$10^−3m
stress = $\frac{\text {tensile force}}{\text {area}}=\frac{90.8}{\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}}=\frac{90.8}{3.14 \times\left(0.92 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}$
stress = 3.41 $\times$ 10^7 N/m^2

questions.question: A steel cable with cross-sectional area 3.00 cm^2 has an elastic limit of 2.40 $ \times $ 10^8 Pa. Find the maximum upward acceleration that can be given a 1200-kg elevator supported by the cable if the stress is not to exceed one-third of the elastic limit?
questions.question_raw: A steel cable with cross-sectional area 3.00 cm2 has an elastic limit of 2.40 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 Pa. Find the maximum upward acceleration that can be given a 1200-kg elevator supported by the cable if the stress is not to exceed one-third of the elastic limit?
questions.explanation:
Given area of cable A = 3cm^2
elastic limit = breaking stress = 2.40 $\times$ 10^8pa mass of elevator m = 1200kg
given condition stress should not exceed $\frac13$ of elastic limit i.e.
$\text {stress}<\frac{\text {elastic limit}}{3}$
$\frac{\text {force}}{\text {area}}<\frac{2.4 \times 10^{8}}{3}$
force < $\frac{2.4 \times 10^{8} \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}{3}$
force < 2.4 $\times$ 10^4
but force = mass $\times$ acceleration 
net acceleration a_net^ = $\frac{2.4 \times 10^{4}}{1200}$
a_net = 20m/s^2 
when elevator moves upwards net acceleration = a + g
here 'a' is acceleration given to elevator and'g' is acceleration due to gravity
a + g = 20
a = 20 - 9.8
a = 10.2 m/s^2

questions.question: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire were twice as long?
questions.question_raw: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire were twice as long?
questions.explanation:
Let length in the first case is L
extension $\Delta L$ = 0.18 mm
length in 2 nd case will be 2L
given that stress $\alpha$ strain $\Rightarrow $ stress = k $\times$ strain (k is proportionality constant)
as stress in both cases is same because same flood lamp is hung and
the diameter of wire remains same on increasing length to 2L thus force per unit area
i.e. stress will be the same. so
$\frac{\text {stress}_{1}}{\text {stress}_{2}}=\frac{k \times \text {strain}_{1}}{k \times \text {strain}_{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\operatorname{strain}_{1}}{\operatorname{strain}_{2}}$ = 1 (stress_1 = stress_2)
$\frac{\frac{\Delta L_{1}}{L_{1}}}{\frac{\Delta L_{2}}{L_{2}}}=1 \Rightarrow \Delta L_{2}=\frac{\Delta L_{1} \times L_{2}}{L_{1}}=\frac{0.18 \times 2 L}{L}$
$\Delta L_2$ = 0.36 mm

questions.question: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire had the same length but twice the diameter?
questions.question_raw: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched if the wire had the same length but twice the diameter?
questions.explanation:
let length in the first case is L
extension $\Delta L$ = 0.18 mm
length in 2nd case will be 2L
given that stress $\alpha$ strain ...(1)
as stress in both cases is same because the diameter of wire is changed so the area of wire will change thus force per unit area
i.e. stress will be the same. so
stress = force/area
from (1)st equation :-
$$${F\over A} \propto {\Delta{L\over l}}$
${1\over A}\propto \Delta l$ ( because l is same )
${1\over r^2} \propto \Delta l$
$({r_2\over r_1})^2 = {\Delta l_1\over \Delta l_2}$
thus, $({2r\over r})^2 = {0.18\over \Delta l_2}$
$\Delta L_2$ = 0.045 mm

questions.question: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched for a copper wire of the original length and diameter? (young modulus of steel=2.0 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2 and young modulus of copper=1.1 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2)
questions.question_raw: You hang a flood lamp from the end of vertical steel wire. The flood lamp stretches the wire 0.18 mm and the stress is proportional to the strain. How much would it have stretched for a copper wire of the original length and diameter? (young modulus of steel=2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2  and young modulus of copper=1.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2)
questions.explanation:
Let the length of wire in both cases be L
extension in case of steel $\Delta L_{\text {steel}}$ = 0.18 mm
young modulus y = $\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}}$
as stress in both case is same
$y_{\text {steel}}=\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}_{\text {steel}}} \Rightarrow$ (1) 
$y_{\text {copper}}=\frac{\text {stress}}{\text {strain}_{\text {copper}}} \Rightarrow$ (2)
equation 1 divided by equation 
$$$\frac{y_{\text {steel }}}{y_{\text {copper }}}=\frac{\text { strain }_{\text {copper }}}{\text { strain }_{\text {steel }}}$
$\frac{2.0 \times 10^{11}}{1.1 \times 10^{11}}=\frac{\frac{\Delta L_{\text {copper}}}{L}}{\frac{\Delta L_{\text {steel}}}{L}}$
$\Delta L_{\text {copper}}=\frac{2.0 \times 0.18}{1.1}$
$\Delta L_{\text {copper}}$ = 0.327 mm $\approx$ 0.33 mm

questions.question: A 12.0-kg mass, fastened to the end of aluminum wire with an unstretched length of 0.50 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with a constant angular speed of 120 rev/min. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm^2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of the path (young modulus of steel = 0.70 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2)?
questions.question_raw: A 12.0-kg mass, fastened to the end of aluminum wire with an unstretched length of 0.50 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with a constant angular speed of 120 rev/min. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of the path (young modulus of steel = 0.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2)?
questions.explanation:
Given mass m = 12.0 kg
length of aluminium wire L = 0.50 m
angular speed $\omega$ = 120 rev/min = 120 $\times$ $\frac{2 \pi}{60} = 4 \pi$ rad/s
area of wire A = 0.014 cm^2 = 0.014 $\times$ 10^-4m^2
when whirled in vertical circle at lowest point let tension in wire be T
applying net force = centripetal force
T - mg = $\mathrm{m} \omega^{2} \mathrm{r}$
T= (12 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$0.50) + 12 $\times$ 9.8
T = 1064.12N
young modulus y = $\frac{\mathrm{stress}}{\mathrm{strain}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{A}}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{y}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{1064.12 \times 0.50}{0.014 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.70 \times 10^{11}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}$ = 54291.91 $\times$ 10^-7 m = 0.54 cm

questions.question: 12.0-kg mass, fastened to the end of aluminum wire with an unstretched length of 0.50 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with a constant angular speed of 120 rev/min. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm^2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the highest point of its path. ( young modulus of aluminum 0.70 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2)
questions.question_raw: 12.0-kg mass, fastened to the end of aluminum wire with an unstretched length of 0.50 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with a constant angular speed of 120 rev/min. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.014 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the highest point of its path. ( young modulus of aluminum 0.70 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 N/m2)
questions.explanation:
given mass m = 12.0 kg
length of aluminum wire L = 0.50 m
angular speed $\omega$ = 120 rev/min = 120 $\times$ $\frac{2 \pi}{60} = 4 \pi$ rad/s
area of wire A = 0.014 cm^2 = 0.014 $\times$ 10^-4m^2
when whirled in vertical circle at lowest point let tension in wire be T
applying net force = centripetal force
T - mg = $\mathrm{m} \omega^{2} \mathrm{r}$
T=(12 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$0.50) + 12 $\times$ 9.8
T = 1064.1216 N
young modulus y = $\frac{\mathrm{stress}}{\mathrm{strain}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{A}}}{\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{y}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{1064.1216\times 0.50}{0.014 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.70 \times 10^{11}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}$ = 54291.92 $\times$ 10^-7 m = 0.54 cm

chapters.name: Mechanical Properties of Fluids
chapters.full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Mechanical Properties of Fluids

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Mechanical Properties of Fluids

questions.question: The basic property of a fluid that makes it different from solids is that:
questions.question_raw: The basic property of a fluid that makes it different from solids is that:

options.label: B
options.text: It does not expand on heating

questions.explanation: Fluids can flow due to unbalanced forces between the atoms of fluids.

questions.question: Density is defined as:
questions.question_raw: Density is defined as:

options.label: A
options.text: the volume of 10 kg of the material

options.label: B
options.text: the volume of 1 kg of the material

questions.explanation:
Density is defined as the compactness of a substance.
Mathematically,
Density(D) = $Mass (M) \over Volume (V)$

questions.question: The specific gravity of a material is:
questions.question_raw: The specific gravity of a material is:

options.label: A
options.text: the ratio of its mass to the mass of water

options.label: B
options.text: the ratio of its volume to the density of water

options.label: C
options.text: the ratio of its density to the density of water

options.label: D
options.text: the ratio of its mass to the density of water

correct_option_texts: the ratio of its density to the density of water
questions.explanation:
The specific gravity of an object is the ratio between the density of an object to a reference liquid. Usually, this reference liquid is water, which has a density of 1 g/mL or 1 g/cm^3.
Water has a specific gravity equal to 1. Materials with a specific gravity less than 1 are less dense than water, and will float on the pure liquid; substances with a specific gravity more than 1 are denser than water and will sink.

questions.question: Pressure p at any point in a fluid at rest is:
questions.question_raw: Pressure p at any point in a fluid at rest is:

options.label: A
options.text: the force parallel to the area at that point per unit area

options.label: B
options.text: the normal force at that point per unit volume

options.label: C
options.text: the normal force at that point per unit area

options.label: D
options.text: the normal force at that point per unit mass

correct_option_texts: the normal force at that point per unit area
questions.explanation: It is important to note that it is valid only for a fluid at rest. In the case of a moving fluid, pressures in different directions could be different depending upon fluid accelerations in different directions.

questions.question: According to Pascal’s Law:
questions.question_raw: According to Pascal’s Law:

options.label: A
options.text: pressure in a fluid increases with temperature

options.label: B
options.text: pressure in a fluid decreases with temperature

options.label: C
options.text: pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points if they are at the same height

options.label: D
options.text: pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points if they are at different heights

correct_option_texts: pressure in a fluid at rest is the same at all points if they are at the same height
questions.explanation:
According to Pascal's Law,
P-P_0 = hdg
from above
Change in pressure is directly proportional to a depth from the free surface.
At the same horizontal line, all points are at the same depth and have the same value of acceleration due to gravity and density of water as well.

questions.question: The pressure at a depth of h in a fluid of density $\rho $ at a place where the acceleration due to gravity is g and the pressure at h = 0 is ${{\rm{p}}_0}$ is given by:
questions.question_raw: The pressure at a depth of h in a fluid of density {tex}\rho {/tex} at a place where the acceleration due to gravity is g and the pressure at h = 0 is {tex}{{\rm{p}}_0}{/tex} is given by:
questions.explanation:
According to Pascal's Law,
Change in Pressure (P - P_0) = h$\rho $g
if h = 0 then P - P_0 = 0
and P = P_0

questions.question: The units of pressure in SI system is:
questions.question_raw: The units of pressure in SI system is:
questions.explanation: Pressure is defined as force per unit area. The SI unit for pressure is the pascal (Pa), equal to one newton per square meter (N/m^2, or kg m^-1 s^−2). This name for the unit was added in 1971; before that, pressure in SI was expressed simply in newtons per square meter.

questions.question: Gauge pressure at a point is:
questions.question_raw: Gauge pressure at a point is:

options.label: A
options.text: the pressure at that point - atmospheric pressure

options.label: B
options.text: the pressure at that point + atmospheric pressure

correct_option_texts: the pressure at that point - atmospheric pressure
questions.explanation:
Gauge pressure is zero-referenced against ambient air pressure, so it is equal to absolute pressure minus atmospheric pressure. Negative signs are usually omitted. 
The difference between absolute pressure and atmospheric pressure is what we call gauge pressure (P_gauge). It can be calculated if we know the absolute and atmospheric pressures using this formula: 
P_gauge = P_abs − P_atm

questions.question: According to Pascal’s law for transmission of fluid pressure, external pressure applied on any part of a fluid contained in a vessel is ________ in all directions.
questions.question_raw: According to Pascal’s law for transmission of fluid pressure, external pressure applied on any part of a fluid contained in a vessel is ________  in all directions.
questions.explanation:
Pascal's principle is defined as a change in pressure at any point in an enclosed fluid at rest is transmitted undiminished to all points in the fluid.
This principle is stated mathematically as
$\Delta P = \rho g(\Delta h)$ 
$\Delta P$ is the hydrostatic pressure (given in pascals in the SI system), or the difference in pressure at two points within a fluid column, due to the weight of the fluid.

questions.question: In a hydraulic lift, the force applied on the smaller cylinder of area A_1 is F_1. If the area of the larger cylinder is A_2 the maximum weight that can be lifted is:
questions.question_raw: In a hydraulic lift, the force applied on the smaller cylinder of area A1 is F1. If the area of the larger cylinder is A2 the maximum weight that can be lifted is:
questions.explanation:
According to Pascal's Law,
Pressure applied to any point inside the liquid is transmitted equally in all direction so,
Pressure applied to the smaller cylinder is equal to the pressure on the other cylinder, which is given by
$\frac{F_1}{A_1}=\frac{F_2}{A_2}$ 
So,
Maximum force on the other side is,
F_2 = $\frac{A_2}{A_1} \times F_1$

questions.question: The streamline in a flow is a curve:
questions.question_raw: The streamline in a flow is a curve:

options.label: A
options.text: whose perpendicular at any point is in the direction of the fluid velocity at that point.

options.label: B
options.text: whose tangent at any point is in the direction of the fluid velocity at that point.

options.label: C
options.text: which shows all points of equal pressure.

options.label: D
options.text: which shows all points of equal velocity.

correct_option_texts: whose tangent at any point is in the direction of the fluid velocity at that point.
questions.explanation:
A streamline is one that drawn is tangential to the velocity vector at every point in the flow at a given instant and forms a powerful tool in understanding flows. This definition leads to the equation for streamlines.${du\over u}={dv\over v}={dw\over w}$ 
where u, v, and w are the velocity components in x, y, and z directions respectively as sketched.

questions.question: Flow is said to be steady if:
questions.question_raw: Flow is said to be steady if:

options.label: A
options.text: the flow rate decreases 10$\% $ every second

options.label: B
options.text: the flow rate does not change with time

options.label: C
options.text: the flow rate decreases 20% every second

options.label: D
options.text: the flow rate increases with time

correct_option_texts: the flow rate does not change with time
questions.explanation:
The flow velocity u of a fluid is a vector field u = u(x, t)
which gives the velocity of an element of fluid at a position x and time t.
The flow of a fluid is said to be steady if u do not vary with time. That is if ${\delta u \over \delta t}$ = 0.

questions.question: If a pipe carrying incompressible liquid has an area A_1 and velocity V_1 at one point A_2 nd V_2 at another point, the equation of continuity gives the relation:
questions.question_raw: If a pipe carrying incompressible liquid has an area A1 and velocity V1 at one point A2 nd V2 at another point, the equation of continuity gives the relation:
questions.explanation:
we apply the principle of mass conservation. Since there is no flow through the sidewalls of the duct, what mass comes in overA_1 goes out of A_2, Over a short time interval $\Delta t$ 
Volume flow in over A_1 = A_1V_1$\Delta$t
Volume flow out over A_2 = A_2V_2$\Delta$t
Therefore
mass in over A = $\rho$A_1V_1$\Delta$t
mass out over A = $\rho$A_2V_2$\Delta$t
So:$\rho$ A_1V_1 = $\rho$A_2V_2$\Delta$t
As volume is the same so this equation can be written as|
A_1V_1 = A_2V_2
This equation is called the continuity equation for steady one-dimensional flow.

questions.question: in laminar flow:
questions.question_raw: in laminar flow:

options.label: A
options.text: adjacent layers of fluid slide smoothly past each other and the flow is steady

options.label: B
options.text: adjacent layers of fluid move in circle crossing each other and the flow is steady

options.label: C
options.text: adjacent layers of fluid do not slide smoothly past each other and the flow is unsteady

options.label: D
options.text: adjacent layers of fluid slide smoothly past each other and the flow is unsteady

correct_option_texts: adjacent layers of fluid slide smoothly past each other and the flow is steady
questions.explanation: laminar flow (or streamline flow) occurs when a fluid flows in parallel layers, with no disruption between the layers. At low velocities, the fluid tends to flow without lateral mixing, and adjacent layers slide past one another like playing cards.

questions.question: At large flow velocities, the flow of fluid becomes:
questions.question_raw: At large flow velocities, the flow of fluid becomes:
questions.explanation: When any liquid is flowing more than the velocity of its critical velocity then flow becomes turbulent.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Mechanical Properties of Fluids

questions.question: According to Bernoulli’s equation for a varying cross-section pipe carrying incompressible liquid, pressure p, density $\rho $ velocity v and height h at a place where the acceleration due to gravity is g.
questions.question_raw: According to Bernoulli’s equation for a varying cross-section pipe carrying incompressible liquid, pressure p, density {tex}\rho {/tex} velocity v and height h at a place where the acceleration due to gravity is g.
questions.explanation:
According to the conservation of energy,
The total energy of any system is always conserved.
And Bernoulli's Theorem says that at any point in the pipe in which the liquid is flowing the sum of all the energies (Pressure Energy, Kinetic Energy, Potential Energy) is always constant.

questions.question: If a ball is spinning in an airflow it develops pressure difference between the lower and upper faces due to differences in the:
questions.question_raw: If a ball is spinning in an airflow it develops pressure difference between the lower and upper faces due to differences in the:

options.label: A
options.text: the viscosity of air giving rise to lift

options.label: B
options.text: the density of air giving rise to lift

options.label: C
options.text: velocities of air giving rise to lift

options.label: D
options.text: height of air giving rise to lift

correct_option_texts: velocities of air giving rise to lift
questions.explanation:
The Magnus effect is an observable phenomenon that is commonly associated with a spinning object that drags air faster around one side, creating a difference in pressure that moves it in the direction of the lower-pressure side.
The Magnus effect often occurs when a spinning sphere (or cylinder) curves away from its principal flight path.
This phenomenon is important in the study of the physics of many ball sports. It is also an important factor in the study of the effects of spinning on guided missiles—and has some engineering uses, for instance in the design of rotor ships and Flettner aeroplanes.

questions.question: The lift on an aircraft wing arises because of:
questions.question_raw: The lift on an aircraft wing arises because of:

options.label: A
options.text: velocity increase and pressure increase on the lower side of the wing

options.label: B
options.text: velocity decrease and pressure increase on the lower side of the wing

options.label: C
options.text: velocity increase and pressure decrease on the lower side of the wing

options.label: D
options.text: velocity decrease and pressure decrease on the lower side of the wing

correct_option_texts: velocity decrease and pressure increase on the lower side of the wing
questions.explanation:
This is the application of Bernoulli's Theorem.
If air speeds up the pressure are lowered. Thus, a wing generates lift because the air goes faster over the top creating a region of low pressure, and thus lift.

questions.question: The coefficient of viscosity for a fluid is defined as the ratio of:
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of viscosity for a fluid is defined as the ratio of:

options.label: A
options.text: compressive stress to the strain rate

options.label: B
options.text: tensile stress to the strain rate

options.label: C
options.text: shearing stress to the flow rate

options.label: D
options.text: shearing stress to the strain rate

correct_option_texts: shearing stress to the strain rate
questions.explanation: The degree to which a fluid resists flows under an applied force expressed as the ratio of the shearing stress to the velocity gradient. The coefficient of viscosity of liquids decreases as temperature increases because the bonds between molecules are weakened.

questions.question: According to Stokes’ law if $\eta $ is the coefficient of viscosity, 'R' the radius of the sphere and it is moving with a velocity v the viscous drag force F is given by:
questions.question_raw: According to Stokes’ law if {tex}\eta {/tex} is the coefficient of viscosity, 'R' the radius of the sphere and it is moving with a velocity v the viscous drag force F is given by:
questions.explanation:
The force of viscosity on a small sphere moving through a viscous fluid is given by:
F_d = $ 6\pi \eta R v$
Where:
 F_d is the frictional force - known as Stokes' drag – acting on the interface between the fluid and the particle
 η is the dynamic viscosity 
 R is the radius of the spherical object
 v is the flow velocity relative to the object.
In SI units, F_d is given in Newtons, η in Pa·s, R in meters, and v in m/s.

questions.question: If $\rho $ is the density of the fluid flowing with a speed v, D stands for the dimension of the pipe, and $\eta $ is the viscosity of the fluid Reynolds number R_e is given by:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\rho {/tex} is the density of the fluid flowing with a speed v, D stands for the dimension of the pipe, and {tex}\eta {/tex} is the viscosity of the fluid Reynolds number Re is given by:
questions.explanation:
It is a dimensionless number comprised of the physical characteristics of the flow. An increasing Reynolds number indicates increasing turbulence of flow.
It is defined as: $R_e =$$ \rho vD\over\eta$ 
where;
v is the flow velocity
D is a characteristic linear dimension
ρ fluid density(kg/m^3)
$\eta$ is dynamic viscosity (Pa.s)
ν kinematic viscosity (m^2/s), v = $\eta\over\rho$

questions.question: The molecules on the surface, are:
questions.question_raw: The molecules on the surface, are:

options.label: A
options.text: The balanced attractions cause the surface to resist being stretched

options.label: B
options.text: The unbalanced attractions cause the surface to stretch less

options.label: C
options.text: The unbalanced attractions cause the surface to stretch more

options.label: D
options.text: The unbalanced attractions cause the surface to resist being stretched

correct_option_texts: The unbalanced attractions cause the surface to resist being stretched
questions.explanation: The surface tension of liquid results from an imbalance of intermolecular attractive forces, the cohesive forces between molecules: A molecule in the bulk liquid experiences cohesive forces with other molecules in all directions. A molecule at the surface of a liquid experiences only net inward cohesive forces. The unbalanced attraction of molecules at the surface of a liquid tends to pull the molecules back into the bulk liquid leaving the minimum number of molecules on the surface. It required energy to increase the surface area of a liquid because a larger surface area contains more molecules in the unbalanced situation and this may result as the surface being stretched.

questions.question: Water drops are spherical because of:
questions.question_raw: Water drops are spherical because of:
questions.explanation:
In fluids, there are primarily 2 forces cohesive force and adhesive force. Cohesive force is the one that acts between the same molecules and adhesive force is the one that acts between different molecules. 
The cohesive force gives rise to a phenomenon called surface tension. In this phenomenon a fluid surface tends to pull itself inwards, causing a reduction of the exposed surface.
For a water droplet that is left alone, cohesive forces undoubtedly dominate, leading to surface tension. Surface tension makes all the molecules of water to stick with each other closely, this results in its spherical shape.

questions.question: If a film of width L is stretched in the longitudinal direction a distance d by force F, surface tension is given by:
questions.question_raw: If a film of width L is stretched in the longitudinal direction a distance d by force F, surface tension is given by:
questions.explanation:
Let L be the width of the film. Since the film has two surfaces, the total length along which the surface force acts on the slider is 2L. The surface tension S in the film is defined as the ratio of the surface force F to the length d (perpendicular to the force) along which the force acts
$S=\frac{F}{d}$
Hence, in the case, d = 2L
$S=\frac{F}{2L}$

questions.question: Capillary Rise of water is explained by:
questions.question_raw: Capillary Rise of water is explained by:
questions.explanation: Capillary action(sometimes capillarity or wicking) is the ability of a liquid to flow in narrow spaces without the assistance of, or even in opposition to, external forces like gravity. The effect can be seen in the drawing up of liquids between the hairs of a paint-brush, in porous materials such as paper and plaster, in some non-porous materials such as sand and liquefied carbon fiber, or in a cell. It occurs because of intermolecular forces between the liquid and surrounding solid surfaces. If the diameter of the tube is sufficiently small, then the combination of surface tension (which is caused by cohesion within the liquid) and adhesive forces between the liquid and container wall act to propel the liquid.

questions.question: An iron needle floats on the surface of the water. This phenomenon is attributed to:
questions.question_raw: An iron needle floats on the surface of the water. This phenomenon is attributed to:
questions.explanation:
The answer lies in the surface tension of the water, As the cohesive forces between the molecules are responsible for the surface tension. When the molecules on the glass or container do not have a surface to stick to, they cohere more strongly to the molecules they are associated with. Since the molecules on top have no neighbours on the top to cohere to, they associate with their other neighbours more strongly. This creates a strong bond between the top layer of molecules. The molecules inward face no net force because they all cancel out each other. However, the surface molecules face a net inward force because there is no force for them on the surface to cancel it. This causes the molecules on the surface to 'contract' and resists being broken or stretched.
When a small body like a needle is placed onto the water carefully, the surface tension will not break due to the small mass of the object. 
As long as the surface tension is undisturbed, the needle will continue to float. Agitating the water will cause the surface tension to break and hence the needle will then sink
.

questions.question: The excess pressure across the surface of a soap bubble of radius R(with $\sigma$ as the surface tension of soap solution) is:
questions.question_raw: The excess pressure across the surface of a soap bubble of radius R(with {tex}\sigma{/tex} as the surface tension of soap solution) is:
questions.explanation:
Let the pressure of the air outside the bubble be P1, that within the soap solution be p' and that of the air inside the bubble be P_2. Looking at the outer surface the solution is on the concave side of the surface hence 
P′ - P_1 = $\frac{2\sigma}R$ 
Where R is the radius of the bubble. As the thickness of the bubble is small on a macroscopic scale, the difference in the radii of the two surfaces will be negligible. 
Similarly looking at the inner surface the air is on the concave side of the surface hence​
​​​​​​P_2 − P′ = $\frac{2\sigma}R$ 
Adding two equations 
P_2 − P_1 = $\frac{4\sigma}R$

questions.question: Two soap bubbles are blown simultaneously. In the first soap bubble, the excess pressure is 4 times of second soap bubble. The ratio of radii of first to second soap bubble is:
questions.question_raw: Two soap bubbles are blown simultaneously. In the first soap bubble, the excess pressure is 4 times of second soap bubble. The ratio of radii of first to second soap bubble is:
questions.explanation:
Using relation for excess pressure in the soap bubble
$P_2-P_1=\frac{4\sigma}R$ 
$\triangle P=\frac{4\sigma}R$
thus
$\triangle P \propto \frac{1}{R}$
given $$
$\triangle P_{1}=4 \triangle P_{2}$
$\frac{\triangle P_{1}}{\triangle P_{2}}=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}} \Rightarrow \frac{4 \triangle P_{2}}{\triangle P_{2}}=\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}$
$\frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}$ = 1:4

questions.question: A drop of oil is placed on the surface of the water. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_raw: A drop of oil is placed on the surface of the water. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It will remain on it as a sphere

options.label: B
options.text: It will partly change to spherical droplets and partly as a thin film

options.label: C
options.text: It will spread as a thin layer

options.label: D
options.text: It will float as a distorted drop on the water surface

correct_option_texts: It will spread as a thin layer
questions.explanation:
When any liquid is poured onto another liquid there are different phenomena that can take place depending on the properties of liquids, In case of crude oil and water:
The two liquids are non-miscible and the first is lighter than the second. The first liquid then spreads on the surface of the second liquid forming a thin layer. The thickness of this layer depends on the properties of the two liquids. This is the case for most hydrocarbons, including both refined products and crude oil of API gravity(API gravity is thus an inverse measure of a petroleum liquid's density relative to that of water (also known as specific gravity). greater than 10.

questions.question: Two small drops of mercury, each of radius R coalesce to form a single large drop. The ratio of the total surface energies before and after the change is
questions.question_raw: Two small drops of mercury, each of radius R coalesce to form a single large drop. The ratio of the total surface energies before and after the change is
questions.explanation:
$2 \times \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi r ^ { 3 } = \frac { 4 } { 3 } \pi R ^ { 3 }$ 
$2 ^ \frac{1}{3} r $ = R or $\frac { r } { R } = \frac { 1 } { 2 ^ \frac{1}{3 } }$
$\frac{\text { surface energy of } 2 \text { small drops }}{\text { surface energy of large drop }}$ $=\frac{2 \times 4 \pi \times r^2 \times T}{4 \pi \times R^2 \times T}$
$\frac{E_1}{E_2}=\frac{2 r^2}{R^2}=\frac{2}{2^{\frac{2}{3}}}=2^{1 / 3}: 1$

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Mechanical Properties of Fluids

questions.question: A wooden block is taken to the bottom of a deep, calm lake of water and then released. It rises up with a:
questions.question_raw: A wooden block is taken to the bottom of a deep, calm lake of water and then released. It rises up with a:
questions.explanation:
The wooden block rises up with acceleration,
a = $Upthrust-weight \ of \ block\over mass\ of\ block$ 
Upthrust and mass of block will be constant. So the acceleration will also be a constant throughout the motion.

questions.question: A man in a lift is holding a can filled with water but having a hole in its bottom. When will the drainage of water be maximum?
questions.question_raw: A man in a lift is holding a can filled with water but having a hole in its bottom. When will the drainage of water be maximum?

options.label: B
options.text: when the lift goes down with uniform velocity

options.label: C
options.text: when the lift is going down with acceleration

options.label: D
options.text: when the lift is going up with the acceleration

correct_option_texts: when the lift is going up with the acceleration
questions.explanation:
Force acting on the liquid contained in the cylinder in the vertical directions are
 $P_1\triangle S$ upward due to the liquid below it
 $P_2\triangle S$ downward due to the liquid above it
 Applying newton's second law
 ${P_1}\Delta S - {P_2}\Delta S - mg = m{a_0}$
 $({P_1} - {P_2})\Delta S = m(g + {a_0}) $ = $ \Delta Sz\rho (g + {a_0})$
 ${P_1} - {P_2} = \rho (g + {a_0})z$
 weight mg = $\triangle$S z $\rho$ g where $\rho$ is the density of liquid
 Net force = mass $\times$ acceleration
Thus pressure difference is greatest when lift moves upwards with acceleration a because for uniform motion $a_0$ will be zero and for lift moving downwards the term in the bracket would be (g - $a_0$). So due to the maximum pressure difference between top and bottom, drainage of water will be maximum in this case.

questions.question: In old age, arteries carrying blood in the human body, become narrow resulting in an increase in blood pressure. This follows from:
questions.question_raw: In old age, arteries carrying blood in the human body, become narrow resulting in an increase in blood pressure. This follows from:
questions.explanation: According to the Bernoulli principle for fluids: in ideal state pressure and density are inversely related or in other words, a slow-moving fluid exerts more pressure than a fast-moving fluid. Due to narrowness and other obstruction, the velocity of the flow of blood gets decreased in old age. This results in increased pressure inside the blood vessel.

questions.question: Soap helps in better cleansing of clothes because:
questions.question_raw: Soap helps in better cleansing of clothes because:

options.label: B
options.text: it increases the surface tension of the solution

options.label: C
options.text: it reduces the surface tension of the solution

correct_option_texts: it reduces the surface tension of the solution
questions.explanation:
Surfactants are substances that help lower the surface tension of water, so as to increase the wetting ability of water which helps in cleaning the clothes.
Soaps (which are a type of detergent) contain surfactants in significant amounts, which help water spread uniformly over clothes.

questions.question: If two soap bubbles of different radii are in communication with each other
questions.question_raw: If two soap bubbles of different radii are in communication with each other

options.label: A
options.text: Air flows from the larger bubble into the smaller one until the two bubbles are of equal size

options.label: B
options.text: The sizes of the bubbles remain unchanged

options.label: C
options.text: Air flows from the larger into the smaller bubble until the radius of the smaller one becomes half that of the larger one

options.label: D
options.text: Air flows from the smaller bubble into the larger one and large bubble grows at the expense of the smaller one

correct_option_texts: Air flows from the smaller bubble into the larger one and large bubble grows at the expense of the smaller one
questions.explanation:
Excess pressure in a soap bubble is given by $P _ { 2 } - P _ { 1 } = \frac { 4 S } { R }$ $\Delta P = \frac { 4 S } { R }$ $\Rightarrow \triangle P \propto \frac { 1 } { R }$ 
Thus pressure difference will be large in smaller bubble or we can say that pressure inside the smaller bubble will be greater than bigger bubble. As air always flow from a high pressure to low pressure end thus in this case air will flow from smaller bubble to larger bubble and larger bubble will grow at the expanse of smaller bubble.

questions.question: More liquid rises in a thin tube because of:
questions.question_raw: More liquid rises in a thin tube because of:

options.label: B
options.text: A larger value of surface tension

options.label: C
options.text: A larger value of the radius

options.label: D
options.text: A smaller value of the radius

correct_option_texts: A smaller value of the radius
questions.explanation: The rise of liquid in a thin tube is caused by forces of attraction between the molecules of water and the glass walls and among the molecules of liquid themselves. The narrower the bore of the capillary tube, the higher the water liquid.

questions.question: The surface tension of the soap solution is 25 $\times$ 10^-3 Nm^-1. The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of diameter 1 cm is:
questions.question_raw: The surface tension of the soap solution is 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 Nm-1. The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of diameter 1 cm is:
questions.explanation:
given surface tension S = 25 $\times$ 10^−3N/m
diameter = 1cm
thus radius r = $d\over 2$ = 0.5cm excess pressure inside soap bubble
$\triangle P=\frac{4 S}{r}=\frac{4 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 100}{0.5}$.
$\Delta P$ = 20pa

questions.question: When a soap bubble is charged, it:
questions.question_raw: When a soap bubble is charged, it:

options.label: B
options.text: Does not undergo any change in size

questions.explanation: A charge will be distributed uniformly on the surface of a bubble if the bubble is charged. The bubble will expand because the charged particles uniformly distributed on it cause them to repel each other due to the electrostatic force. This will happen to both positive and negatively charged bubbles because of the charge on it.

questions.question: A drop of oil is placed on the surface of the water. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_raw: A drop of oil is placed on the surface of the water. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It will remain on it as a sphere

options.label: B
options.text: It will spread as a thin layer

options.label: C
options.text: It will float as a distorted drop on the water surface

options.label: D
options.text: It will partly change to spherical droplets and partly as a thin film

correct_option_texts: It will spread as a thin layer
questions.explanation:
When any liquid is poured onto another liquid there are different phenomena that can take place depending on the properties of liquids, In case of crude oil and water:
The two liquids are non-miscible and the first is lighter than the second. The first liquid then spreads on the surface of the second liquid forming a thin layer. The thickness of this layer depends on the properties of the two liquids. This is the case for most hydrocarbons, including both refined products and crude oil of API gravity(API gravity is thus an inverse measure of a petroleum liquid's density relative to that of water (also known as specific gravity). greater than 10.

questions.question: Small droplets of liquid are usually more spherical in shape than larger drops of the same liquid because:
questions.question_raw: Small droplets of liquid are usually more spherical in shape than larger drops of the same liquid because:

options.label: A
options.text: the force of surface tension is equal and opposite to the force of gravity

options.label: B
options.text: the force of surface tension predominates the force of gravity

options.label: C
options.text: the force of gravity and force of surface tension act in the same direction and are equal

options.label: D
options.text: the force of gravity predominates the force of surface tension

correct_option_texts: the force of surface tension predominates the force of gravity
questions.explanation: In small drops effect of gravity can be ignored because all the points can be considered at the same height. Thus to acquire minimum energy they will acquire spherical shape while in larger drops due to finite radius of drop all the points cannot be assumed at same height thus drop will try to acquire spherical shape but due to effect of gravity, its shape will be a little distorted from the pure spherical shape.

questions.question: Water rises to a height of 16.3 cm in a capillary of height 18 cm above the water level. If the tube is suddenly cut at a height of 12 cm.
questions.question_raw: Water rises to a height of 16.3 cm in a capillary of height 18 cm above the water level. If the tube is suddenly cut at a height of 12 cm.

options.label: A

correct_option_texts: water will stay at a height of 12 cm in the capillary tube
questions.explanation: water will stay at a height of 12 cm in the capillary tube

questions.question: A spherical soap bubble has a radius ′r′. The surface tension of the soap film is T. The energy needed to double the diameter of the bubble at the same temperature is:
questions.question_raw: A spherical soap bubble has a radius ′r′. The surface tension of the soap film is T. The energy needed to double the diameter of the bubble at the same temperature is:
questions.explanation: $24\pi\;r^2\sigma$

questions.question: A liquid drop of radius R is broken up into N small droplets. The work done is proportional to( take $N^\frac13$ >> 1).
questions.question_raw: A liquid drop of radius R is broken up into N small droplets. The work done is proportional to( take {tex}N^\frac13{/tex} >> 1).
questions.explanation:
When a droplet of radius R is broken into N small droplets total volume will remain constant. Let the radius of small droplets be r. Then
$\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=N \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}$
$r=\frac{R}{{N} ^\frac{1}{3}}$
work done will be equal to the change in surface energy, thus
W = $S_{f}-S_{i}=N 4 \pi r^{2} T-4 \pi R^{2} T$
W = $N 4 \pi\left(\frac{R}{N^{\frac{1}{3}}}\right)^{2} T-4 \pi R^{2} T$
W = $4 \pi R^{2} T\left(N^{\frac{1}{3}}-1\right)$
if $N^\frac{1}{3}$ is very large thus it becomes
W = 4$\pi$R^2T$N^\frac{1}{3}$
thus
$W \propto N^{\frac{1}{3}}$

questions.question: An air bubble of radius r is formed inside a tank of water at depth h. The atmospheric pressure is equal to a water column of height H. the pressure inside the bubble is:
questions.question_raw: An air bubble of radius r is formed inside a tank of water at depth h. The atmospheric pressure is equal to a water column of height H. the pressure inside the bubble is:
questions.explanation:
Excess pressure in an air bubble is given by
P_2 − P_1 = $\frac{2 T}{r}$ $\longrightarrow$ surface tension r $\longrightarrow$ the radius of the bubble if the bubble is at a depth h inside then
$P_{1}=P_{a t m}+h \rho g$
given $P_{a t m}=H \rho g$
$P_{1}=\rho g(H+h)$
thus
$P_{2}=\rho g(h+H)+\frac{2 T}{r}$

questions.question: It is easier to spray water to which soap is added, because:
questions.question_raw: It is easier to spray water to which soap is added, because:

options.label: C
options.text: Addition of soap to water makes surface tension of water zero

options.label: D
options.text: Addition of soap to water decreases the surface tension of water

correct_option_texts: Addition of soap to water decreases the surface tension of water
questions.explanation: The addition of soap decreases the surface tension of water, as we know the energy of spraying is directly proportional to the surface tension.

questions.question: A capillary tube remains dipped in a water container so that loss in weight of the capillary tube is:
questions.question_raw: A capillary tube remains dipped in a water container so that loss in weight of the capillary tube is:

options.label: A

options.label: B

options.label: C

questions.explanation: This is Archimedes principle when an object submerged in a liquid its weight is equal to the Buoyant force.

questions.question: One thousand small water droplets of equal size combine to form a big drop. The ratio of the final surface energy to the total initial surface energy is:
questions.question_raw: One thousand small water droplets of equal size combine to form a big drop. The ratio of the final surface energy to the total initial surface energy is:
questions.explanation:
Let 1000 small droplets of radius r combine to form a bigger drop of radius R. As total volume remains constant 
$1000 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$
$\Rightarrow$ R = 10r
initial surface energy
$S_{i}$ = surface area $\times$ surface tension
$S_{i}=1000 \times 4 \pi r^{2} \times T$
final surface energy
$S_{f}=4 \pi R^{2} \times T$
$S_{f}=100 \times 4 \pi r^{2} \times T$
taking ratio
$\frac{S_{f}}{S_{i}}=\frac{100 \times 4 \pi r^{2} \times T}{1000 \times 4 \pi r^{2} \times T}$
$\Rightarrow$ 1:10

questions.question: The unit of surface tension in S.I units is given by:
questions.question_raw: The unit of surface tension in S.I units is given by:
questions.explanation: surface tension is measured in force per unit length. The S.I unit is a newton per meter but the CGS unit dyne per centimeter is also used.

questions.question: What will be the height of a liquid column in a capillary tube on the surface of the moon?
questions.question_raw: What will be the height of a liquid column in a capillary tube on the surface of the moon?

options.label: B
options.text: Six times that on the earth's surface

options.label: D
options.text: 1/6th of what was on earth's surface

correct_option_texts: Six times that on the earth's surface
questions.explanation:
The height to which the liquid rises in a capillary tube is inversion all proportional to the acceleration due to gravity.
$h=\frac{2 S \cos \theta}{r \rho g}$
thus if all other parameters are fixed 
$h \propto\frac{1}{g}$
On the surface of the moon, acceleration due to gravity is $1\over6$th as that on the surface of the earth. Therefore the water will rise to a height 6 times that on earth's surface.

questions.question: Two match sticks are laid side by side on the surface of the water. When a drop of light oil on the end of a third match stick is touched to the surface film between the two floating sticks, the two match sticks
questions.question_raw: Two match sticks are laid side by side on the surface of the water. When a drop of light oil on the end of a third match stick is touched to the surface film between the two floating sticks, the two match sticks
questions.explanation: They will move apart rapidly because the surface tension of oil is lesser than that of water.

questions.question: Soldering of two metals possible because of the property of:
questions.question_raw: Soldering of two metals possible because of the property of:
questions.explanation: Cohesion is the attractive force among the same kind of materials. Thus soldering of two metals is possible due to cohesion.

questions.question: When the adhesive force in the case of liquid and glass is greater than the cohesive forces between the liquid molecules, the shape of the meniscus of liquid in a capillary tube is?
questions.question_raw: When the adhesive force in the case of liquid and glass is greater than the cohesive forces between the liquid molecules, the shape of the meniscus of liquid in a capillary tube is?
questions.explanation:
The formation of meniscus depends on cohesive and adhesive forces in a liquid. For water, adhesive forces are stronger than the cohesive forces, therefore, water in a container stick to the wall of the container and owing to the capillary action rises a little bit and form a concave meniscus.
When liquid water is confined in a tube, its surface (meniscus) has a concave shape because water wets the surface and creeps up the side.

questions.question: If the work done in blowing a bubble of volume V is W, then the work done in blowing a soap bubble of volume 2V will be
questions.question_raw: If the work done in blowing a bubble of volume V is W, then the work done in blowing a soap bubble of volume 2V will be
questions.explanation:
Work done to blow a bubble will be equal to its surface energy 
W = S = surface area $\times$ surface tension S = A $\times$ T
Given that work done to blow a bubble of volume V is W. 
W = S = surface area $\times$ surface tension, S = A $\times$ T for volume V
S = 4$\pi$r^2 $\times$ T
S $\propto$ r^2 but radius r $\propto$ Volume$(v)^{1\over 3}$
thus S $\propto$ $(V)^{2\over 3}$
$\Rightarrow$ $W \propto V^{\frac{2}{3}}$
$\Rightarrow$ thus if the volume is doubled 
the W_2 becomes $(2)^{\frac{2}{3}} \Rightarrow(4)^{\frac{1}{3}} W_{1}$
thus $W_{2}=\sqrt[3]{4} W_{1}$

questions.question: Two tubes of the same material but of different radii are dipped in a liquid. The height to which a liquid rises in one tube is 2.2cm and in the other is 6.6 cm. The ratio of their radii is:
questions.question_raw: Two tubes of the same material but of different radii are dipped in a liquid. The height to which a liquid rises in one tube is 2.2cm and in the other is 6.6 cm. The ratio of their radii is:
questions.explanation:
Using the relation for the height of the liquid in a capillary tube 
$h=\frac{2 S \cos \theta}{r \rho g}$
thus if all other parameters are fixed
$h \propto \frac{1}{r}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{h_{1}}{h_{2}}=\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}$ given h_1 = 2.2 and h_2 = 6.6
$\frac{2.2}{6.6}=\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}$
$\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}$ = 3 : 1

questions.question: A raft of wood of density 600kg/m^3 and mass 120 kg floats in water. How much weight can be put on the raft to make it just sink?
questions.question_raw: A raft of wood of density 600kg/m3 and mass 120 kg floats in water. How much weight can be put on the raft to make it just sink?
questions.explanation:
Given mass of raft M = 120 k.g.
density of raft = 600 k.g./m^3
thus volume of raft V = 
$V = \frac{{Mass}}{{density}} = \frac{{120}}{{600}} = 0.2{m^3}$
when raft just sinks inside after placing extra mass m. thus weight of
(raft + extra mass m)
will be equal to the buoyant force. so
(M + m)g = $V\rho g$
$120 + m = 0.2 \times 1000$
m = 80 kg.

questions.question: If a block of iron of unknown mass ​​​of size 5 cm $ \times $ 5 cm $ \times $ 5 cm was completely submerged in water, what is the mass of the block. (density of water = 1 gm/cm^3)
questions.question_raw: If a block of iron of unknown mass ​​​of size 5 cm {tex} \times {/tex} 5 cm {tex} \times {/tex} 5 cm was completely submerged in water, what is the mass of the block. (density of water = 1 gm/cm3)
questions.explanation:
When it is submerged completely its weight will be balanced by the buoyant force which is equal to the mass of water displaced. Thus let the mass of block be m than at equilibrium
mg = Vρg
m = 5 $\times$ 5 $\times$5 $\times$ 1
m = 125gm

questions.question: A boy is carrying a bucket of water in one hand with a 0.05 kg cork piece in it. After some time he takes out the cork piece and holds it on the other hand. The boy will carry:

options.label: B
options.text: Either less or more load depending on the density of plastic
