questions.question: A beaker is partly filled with water, the beaker and the contents have a mass of 50 gm. A piece of wood having a volume of 5 cc. is floated in the beaker. The density of wood is 0.8 g/cc. the mass of the beaker and its contents is:
questions.question_raw: A beaker is partly filled with water, the beaker and the contents have a mass of 50 gm. A piece of wood having a volume of 5 cc. is floated in the beaker. The density of wood is 0.8 g/cc. the mass of the beaker and its contents is:
questions.explanation:
v = $m\over d$
m = v $\times$ d . . . . .(mass = density $\times$ volume)
total mass = 50 + 0.8 $\times$ 5 = 50 + 4 = 54 g

questions.question: A bird, resting on the floor of an airtight box, which is being carried by a boy, starts flying. The boy will feel that the box:
questions.question_raw: A bird, resting on the floor of an airtight box, which is being carried by a boy, starts flying. The boy will feel that the box:

options.label: C
options.text: Lighter in the beginning and heavier afterwards

questions.explanation: If the cage is completely closed, it doesn't make a difference if the bird is hovering inside it or if it sits on the ground. When flying, the bird pushes air to the ground which will exert a downward force on the cage exactly equal to the weight of the bird. This is a direct consequence of the conservation of momentum and Newton's second & third law. Since no additional external force is acting on the cage-bird-system when the bird is flying as compared to when it's not, the acceleration on the cage can be no different. The effect due to the flying of the bird only concerns the internal forces and since action = reaction, hence there is no change in the weight of the box.

questions.question: A cube of side 10 cm is floating in equilibrium in a tank of water. When a mass of 10 gm is placed on the cube, the depth of the cube inside the water increases by: (g = 10 ms^−2 density of water = 10^3 kg m^−3)
questions.question_raw: A cube of side 10 cm is floating in equilibrium in a tank of water. When a mass of 10 gm is placed on the cube, the depth of the cube inside the water increases by: (g = 10  ms−2 density of water = 103 kg m−3)
questions.explanation:
Consider initially x c.m. height of cube is inside the water. For floatation, its weight will be balanced by a buoyant force which is equal to the weight of water displaced. Thus let the mass of cube is m. Given side of cube = 10 c.m. thus at equilibrium
Weight of cube = buoyant force
mg = V$\rho$g
where $\rho$ is the density of water and volume of cube inside will be $\left(\frac{10}{100}\right)^{2}$metre^3
m = $x\left(\frac{10}{100}\right)^{2} 1000$
m = 10x $\rightarrow$ (1)
now if a block of 10 g is placed on it then let the height of cube inside is (x + y)
thus at equilibrium
$\left(m+\frac{10}{1000}\right) g=(x+y)\left(\frac{10}{100}\right)^{2} 1000 g$
using(1)
(10x + $\frac{1}{{100}}$) = (x + y)10 $\Rightarrow$ 1000x + 1 = 1000x + 1000y
y = $1\over 1000$metre = 1mm
Thus after placing 10gm mass height of cube in water will increase by 1mm.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Mechanical Properties of Fluids

questions.question: A wooden cube just floats inside the water when a 200gm mass is placed on it. When the mass is removed, the cube is 2 cm above the water level. The size of the cube is:
questions.question_raw: A wooden cube just floats inside the water when a 200gm mass is placed on it. When the mass is removed, the cube is 2 cm above the water level. The size of the cube is:
questions.explanation:
Let the side of the cube be x, the density of water be $\rho$, the mass of cube is m, acceleration due to gravity be g.
When the weight is on the block,
Using Archimedes’ Principle,
Weight of the block + Weight of the mass = Buoyant force (B_1)
Since Bouyant force is equal to the volume of liquid displaced by the body thus 
B_1 = $\rho g x^{3}$
thus (m + 200) g = $\rho$ gx^3
m = $\rho$x^3 - 200 $\rightarrow$ (1)
when mass is removed the wooden cube is 2 cm outside
thus volume of cube inside = volume of water displaced = (x - 2) x^2
again balance the buoyant force B_2 = $\rho$g(x - 2) x^2
$\rho$g(x - 2) x^2 = mg
$\Rightarrow$ $\rho$(x - 2) x^2 = m
substitute for m
$\rho$(x - 2) x^2 = $\rho$x^3 - 200
$\Rightarrow$ 2 $\rho$x^2 = 200
take $\rho$ = 1 gram/cm^3
$\Rightarrow$ x = 10 cm

questions.question: Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is:
questions.question_raw: Pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is:

options.label: A
options.text: increased in proportion to the mass of the fluid and then transmitted.

options.label: B
options.text: transmitted unchanged to every portion of the fluid and walls of the containing vessel.

options.label: C
options.text: diminished and transmitted to the wall of the container.

options.label: D
options.text: increased and applied to every part of the fluid.

correct_option_texts: transmitted unchanged to every portion of the fluid and walls of the containing vessel.
questions.explanation: According to Pascal's law (or the principle of transmission of fluid-pressure) is a principle in fluid mechanics that states that a pressure change occurring anywhere in a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted throughout the fluid such that the same change occurs everywhere.

questions.question: A parrot is in a wire case, which is hanging from a spring balance. Initially, the parrot sits in the cage and in the second instance the parrot flies inside the cage, the reading of the balance will be:
questions.question_raw: A parrot is in a wire case, which is hanging from a spring balance. Initially, the parrot sits in the cage and in the second instance the parrot flies inside the cage, the reading of the balance will be:

options.label: A
options.text: Normal when the parrot flies in the cage

options.label: C
options.text: Lesser when the parrot flies in the cage

options.label: D
options.text: Greater when the parrot flies in the cage

correct_option_texts: Lesser when the parrot flies in the cage
questions.explanation: For the bird to stay in the air, the wings must push down on the air under them. If the cage is airtight, the air exerts an equal force on the floor of the cage. The net force down on the scale will remain constant. If the cage is not airtight (i.e; the wire cage), the air will move down and horizontal. The force down will be less than the weight of the bird.

questions.question: If there would be smaller gravitational effects, which of the following forces do you think would alter in some respect?
questions.question_raw: If there would be smaller gravitational effects, which of the following forces do you think would alter in some respect?
questions.explanation: Buoyancy force on an object is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object, or the density of the fluid multiplied by the submerged volume times the gravitational acceleration.

questions.question: If you float on your back on the water, your weight is:
questions.question_raw: If you float on your back on the water, your weight is:

options.label: C
options.text: Greater than the weight of water you displace

questions.explanation: Due to the buoyant force of water, the buoyancy of water reduces your body weight by 90%. When you get into the pool, your body displaces a volume of water (the "hole" in the water that your body fits into). As long as your body displaces water weighs more than you do, you float. This is basically Archimedes' Law. You weigh less than the water you're in, because your lungs are full of air, like a balloon, and like a balloon, the air in your lungs lifts you to the surface naturally.

questions.question: The height of the mercury column in a simple barometer is h. As the tube is inclined to the vertical at an angle $\alpha$ the length of the mercury column along the length of the tube is l, then:
questions.question_raw: The height of the mercury column in a simple barometer is h. As the tube is inclined to the vertical at an angle {tex}\alpha{/tex} the length of the mercury column along the length of the tube is l, then:
questions.explanation:
This will form a right-angled triangle with base l and hypotenuse h and the angle between base and hypotenuse is 
$\alpha$ thus cos$\alpha$ = $\frac{l}{h}$ 
l = h cos $\alpha$

questions.question: A cylindrical vessel is filled with an equal mass of mercury and water. The overall height of the two layers is 43.8 cm. The relative density of mercury is 13.6. Then the pressure of the liquid on the bottom of the vessel is:
questions.question_raw: A cylindrical vessel is filled with an equal mass of mercury and water. The overall height of the two layers is 43.8 cm. The relative density of mercury is 13.6. Then the pressure of the liquid on the bottom of the vessel is:
questions.explanation:
Given total height H of vessel = 43.8 cm 
As mass of mercury and water is equal
$\rho_{h g} V_{h g}=\rho_{w} V_{w} $ given $ \rho_{h g}$ = 13.6 $\rho_{w} $)
13.6 $\times$ $\pi r^{2} h_{h g}$ = $\pi r^{2} h_{w}$
h_w = 13.6 $h_{h g}$ $\rightarrow$ (1)
given h_w + h_hg = 43.8 $\rightarrow$ (2)
from(1) and (2)
h_w = 40.8 cm and h_hg = 3 cm 
Thus pressure at bottom will be
P = P_atm + $\rho_{h g} g h_{h g} + \rho_{w} g h_{w} $
P = $P_{a t m} + \left(\rho_{h g} g \times 3\right) + \frac{\rho_{h g}}{13.6} g \times 40.8$ 
P = P_atm + 3cm of Hg + 3cm of Hg
P = P_atm + 6 cm of Hg

questions.question: Two bodies are in equilibrium when suspended in water from the arms of a balance. The mass of one body is 36 gm and its density is 9 gm/cc. If the mass of the other is 48 gm, its density in gm/cm^3 is:
questions.question_raw: Two bodies are in equilibrium when suspended in water from the arms of a balance. The mass of one body is 36 gm and its density is 9 gm/cc. If the mass of the other is 48 gm, its density in gm/cm3 is:
questions.explanation:
W_1 = W_2 
$m_1 g -v_1\rho_\omega g = m_2g - v_2\rho_\omega g$
by simplifying the equation, we get 
​​36−4 = 48−V_2
V_2 = 48−32 = 16m^3
the density of body is given by 
$\rho=\frac{m_2}{V_2}$
$ = \frac{48}{16}$ = 3g/cm^3

questions.question: Three liquids of densities d, 2d, and 3 d are mixed in equal volumes. Then the density of the mixture is:
questions.question_raw: Three liquids of densities d, 2d, and 3 d are mixed in equal volumes. Then the density of the mixture is:
questions.explanation:
The density of the mixture is given by
$\rho_{\operatorname{mix}}=\frac{\text {total mass}}{\text {total volume}}=\frac{m_{1}+m_{2}+m_{3}}{v_{1}+v_{2}+v_{3}}$
asv_1 = v_2 = v_3
$\rho_{\operatorname{mix}}=\frac{\rho_{1} v+\rho_{2} v+\rho_{3} v}{v+v+v}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{d v+2 d v+3 d v}{3 v}$
$\rho_{m ix}$ = 2d

questions.question: Three liquids of densities d, 2d and 3d are mixed in equal proportion of weights. If density of water is d, then the specific gravity of the mixture is
questions.question_raw: Three liquids of densities d, 2d and 3d are mixed in equal proportion of weights. If density of water is d, then the specific gravity of the mixture is
questions.explanation:
w_1 = w_2 = w_3
$\Rightarrow$ m_1 = m_2 = m_3 = m (say)
Then, v_1 = $\frac{m}{d}$, v_2 = $\frac{m}{2d}$, v_3 = $\frac{m}{3d}$
$\therefore$ d_max = $\frac{mass}{volume}$= $\frac{3m}{v_1+v_2+v_3}$ = $\frac{18}{11}d$
So, specific gravity of mixture = $\frac{d_{max}}{d_{water}}$ = $\frac{18}{11}$

questions.question: What fraction of the volume of a solid piece of metal of sp gr 7.2 floats above the surface of a container of mercury of sp. gr. 13.6?
questions.question_raw: What fraction of the volume of a solid piece of metal of sp gr 7.2 floats above the surface of a container of mercury of sp. gr. 13.6?
questions.explanation:
V = Volume of metal
V’ = Volume of mercury displaced 
Weight of body = Weight of mercury displaced 
= 7.2 $\times$ 9810 $\times$ V = 13.6 $\times$9810 $\times$V′
$\Rightarrow$ ${V'\over V }= 0.53$ 
Fraction of volume above mercury = 1 - 0.53 = 0.47

questions.question: Which one of the following statements is correct for fluid passing through the narrow part of the non-uniform pipe?
questions.question_raw: Which one of the following statements is correct for fluid passing through the narrow part of the non-uniform pipe?

options.label: A
options.text: its velocity increases but its pressure decreases

options.label: B
options.text: its velocity and pressure both decrease

options.label: C
options.text: its velocity decreases but its pressure increases

options.label: D
options.text: its velocity and pressure both increase

correct_option_texts: its velocity increases but its pressure decreases
questions.explanation:
Consider two points A and B in the pipe at the same horizontal level. At point A, let a_1 be the area of cross-section of the pipe, v_1 the velocity of fluid flow, and p_1 the fluid pressure. let a_2, v_2, p_2 be the corresponding quantities at point B. then from Bernoulli's theorem we have, 
$p_1 +{1\over 2}\rho v_1^2 = p_2 +{1\over 2}\rho v_2^2 $
$\Rightarrow (p_1-p_2) = {1\over 2} \rho (v_2^2-v_1^2) \ $....(1)
if a_2 < a_1, it follows from continuity equation a_1v_1= a_2v_2 that v_1 > v_2 from eq (1) it follows that p_2 < p_1, Hence at the narrow part of the pipe, the fluid velocity increase but its pressure decrease.

questions.question: A cylindrical jar of cross-sectional area 0.01 m^2 is filled with water to a height of 50 cm. It carries a tight-fitting piston of negligible mass. What is the pressure at the bottom of the jar when the mass of 1 kg is placed on the piston? Take g = 10ms^−2.
questions.question_raw: A cylindrical jar of cross-sectional area 0.01 m2 is filled with water to a height of 50 cm. It carries a tight-fitting piston of negligible mass. What is the pressure at the bottom of the jar when the mass of 1 kg is placed on the piston? Take g = 10ms−2.
questions.explanation:
The pressure at the bottom of the jar is due to the weight of a column of water of height h = 50 cm = 0.5 m and the weight of a load of m = 1 kg.
The total force acting on the base = $h\rho g A+ mg $ 
= 0.5 $\times $ 1000 $\times $ 10 $\times $ 0.01 + 1 $\times $10 = 60N
$\therefore$ Pressure =$ {force \over area} = {60\over 0.01 } = 6000 \ Nm^{-2} \ $ or 6000 Pa

questions.question: Two vessels A and B of different shapes have the same base area and are filled with water up to the same height h. The force exerted by water on the base is ${{\rm{F}}_{\rm{A}}}$ for vessel A and ${{\rm{F}}_{\rm{B}}}$ for vessel B. The respective weights of the vessels are ${{\rm{W}}_{\rm{A}}}$ and ${{\rm{W}}_{\rm{B}}}$. Then
questions.question_raw: Two vessels A and B of different shapes have the same base area and are filled with water up to the same height h. The force exerted by water on the base is {tex}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{A}}}{/tex} for vessel A and {tex}{{\rm{F}}_{\rm{B}}}{/tex} for vessel B. The respective weights of the vessels are {tex}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{A}}}{/tex} and {tex}{{\rm{W}}_{\rm{B}}}{/tex}. Then
questions.explanation:
Since h is, the same for both vessels, pressure = $h\rho g$ is the same. Now force = $pressure \times base \ area $. Since the base area is the same, the force exerted by water on the base is the same for both vessels. Thus $F_A= F_B$.
The liquid exerts pressure, and hence force, not only on the base but also on the sides of the vessel. This force is normal to the sides of the vessel. For vessel B, this force has no component in the downward direction. but for vessel A, this force ( which is normal to the sides) has a non-zero component vertically downwards. Hence vessel A gives higher reading than vessel B when they are weighed on a weighing scale. Thus $W_A > W_B$

questions.question: A tank with a square base of area 2.0 m^2 is divided into two compartments by a vertical partition in the middle. There is a small hinged door of face area 20 cm$^{\rm{2}}$ at the bottom of the partition. Water is filled in one compartment and acid, of relative density 1.7, in the other, both to a height of 4m. If g = 10 ms^-2 the force necessary to keep the door closed is:
questions.question_raw: A tank with a square base of area 2.0 m2 is divided into two compartments by a vertical partition in the middle. There is a small hinged door of face area 20 cm{tex}^{\rm{2}}{/tex} at the bottom of the partition. Water is filled in one compartment and acid, of relative density 1.7, in the other, both to a height of 4m. If g = 10 ms-2 the force necessary to keep the door closed is:
questions.explanation:
Water compartment
P = $ h\rho g $
= 4 $\times$ 1.0 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 9.8
= 39.2 $\times$ 10^3Pa
Acid Compartment,
P' = $h\rho'g$
= 4 $\times$ 1.7 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 9.8
= 66.64 $\times$ 10^3Pa
Now,
P′ − P = 66.64 $\times$ 10^3 − 39.2 $\times$ 10^3
P′ − P = 27.44 $\times$ 10^3 Pa
A = 20 cm^2 = 20 $\times$ 10^−4m^2
Force using pressure,
Pressure = $ {Force \over Area}$
Force = pressure $\times$ area F = 27.44 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 20 $\times$ 10^−4
F = 54.88 N

questions.question: expansion of water is called anomalous because:

options.label: A

options.label: C

options.label: D
options.text: it expands on cooling from 10^oC to 4^oC

correct_option_texts: it contracts on heating from 0^oC to 4^oC
questions.explanation: Liquids expand on heating and contract on cooling at moderate temperatures. But water exhibits exceptional behaviour. When it is heated from 0^oC, it actually contracts rather than expanding, up to 4^oC. The volume of water is minimum at 4^oC. Beyond 4^oC, water starts expanding. This behavior of water between 0^oC and 4^oC is called the anomalous expansion of water.

questions.question: For an ideal gas the coefficient of volume expansion at constant pressure is:
questions.question_raw: For an ideal gas the coefficient of volume expansion at constant pressure is:
questions.explanation:
$\gamma=\frac{\Delta V}{V \Delta T}$
PV = nRT
V = $\frac{n R T}{P}$
$P \Delta V=n R \Delta T$
$\Delta V=\frac{n R \Delta T}{P}$
$\gamma=\frac{\frac{n R \Delta T}{P}}{\frac{n R T}{P}} \times \frac{1}{\Delta T}$ = $\frac{n R \Delta T}{P} \times \frac{P}{n R T} \times \frac{1}{\Delta T}=\frac{1}{T}$

questions.question: The coefficient of volume expansion is n times coefficient of linear expansion where n equals:
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of volume expansion is n times coefficient of linear expansion where n equals:
questions.explanation:
$\alpha = \left( {{{\Delta l} \over l}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ and $\gamma = \left( {{{\Delta V} \over V}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ 
Volume V = L^3
expanded length = $L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ 
expanded Volume = ${\left[ {L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)} \right]^3} = {L^3}(1 + {\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2} + 2\alpha \Delta T)(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ 
very small term is neglected
expanded volume = $V(1 + 3\alpha \Delta T)$ 
$\frac{\Delta l}{l}=a \Delta T$
$\gamma = \left( {{{\Delta V} \over V}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}} = {{3\alpha V\Delta T} \over V}{1 \over {\Delta T}}$ 
$ \gamma = 3\alpha$

questions.question: The coefficient of area expansion is n times of coefficient of linear expansion. where n equals
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of area expansion is n times of coefficient of linear expansion. where n equals
questions.explanation:
$\alpha = \left( {{{\Delta l} \over l}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ and $\beta = \left( {{{\Delta A} \over A}} \right){1 \over {\Delta T}}$ 
area A = L^2
expanded length = $L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ 
expanded area = ${\left[ {L(1 + \alpha \Delta T)} \right]^2} $ = $ {L^2}(1 + {\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2} + 2\alpha \Delta T)$ 
${\alpha ^2}\Delta {T^2}$ is very small
expanded area = $A(1 + 2\alpha \Delta T)$ 
$\Delta A = 2\alpha A\Delta T$
$\beta=\left(\frac{\Delta A}{A}\right) \frac{1}{\Delta T}=\frac{2 \alpha A \Delta T}{A} \frac{1}{\Delta T}$
$\beta=2 \alpha$ 
So that n = 2

questions.explanation: The latent heat of vaporization is actually the total amount of enthalpy (a kind of energy/heat) necessary to accomplish a phase change for a liquid or gas at the boiling/condensation point. Phase changes are generally considered at constant pressure, rather than constant volume.

questions.question: The electrical resistance in ohms of a certain thermometer varies with temperature according to the approximate law: R = R$_{\rm{o}}$ [1 + $\alpha $ (T – T$_{\rm{o}}$ )] The resistance is 101.6 $\Omega $ at the triple-point of water 273.16 K, and 165.5 $\Omega $ at the normal melting point of lead (600.5 K). What is the temperature when the resistance is 123.4 $\Omega $?
questions.question_raw: The electrical resistance in ohms of a certain thermometer varies with temperature according to the approximate law: R = R{tex}_{\rm{o}}{/tex} [1 + {tex}\alpha {/tex} (T – T{tex}_{\rm{o}}{/tex} )] The resistance is 101.6 {tex}\Omega {/tex} at the triple-point of water 273.16 K, and 165.5 {tex}\Omega {/tex} at the normal melting point of lead (600.5 K). What is the temperature when the resistance is 123.4 {tex}\Omega {/tex}?
questions.explanation:
It is given that:
R = R_0 [1 + $\alpha $ (T - T_0)] …(i)
where,
R_0 and T_0 are the initial resistance and temperature respectively
R and T are the final resistance and temperature respectively 
$\alpha $ is a constant
At the triple point of water, T_0 = 273.15 K
Resistance of lead, R_0 = 101.6 $\Omega $
At normal melting point of lead, T = 600.5 K
The resistance of lead, R = 165.5 $\Omega $
Substituting these values in equation (i), we get:
R = R_o [1 + $\alpha $ (T - T_o)]
165.5 = 101.6 [ 1 + $\alpha $(600.5 - 273.15) ] 
1.629 = 1 + $\alpha $(327.35) 
∴ $\alpha $ = $0.629 \over 327.35$ = 1.92 $\times$ 10^-3 K^-1
For resistance, R_1 = 123.4 $\Omega $
R_1 = R_0 [1 + $\alpha$ (T - T_0)]
where,
T is the temperature when the resistance of lead is 123.4 $\Omega $
123.4 = 101.6 [ 1 + 1.92 $\times$ 10^-3( T - 273.15) ]
Solving for T, we get
T = 384.8 K.

questions.question: A steel tape 1m long is correctly calibrated for a temperature of 27.0^oC. The length of a steel rod measured by this tape is found to be 63.0 cm on a hot day when the temperature is 45.0 ^oC. What is the actual length of the steel rod on that day? Coefficient of linear expansion of steel = 1.20 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1.
questions.question_raw: A steel tape 1m long is correctly calibrated for a temperature of 27.0oC. The length of a steel rod measured by this tape is found to be 63.0 cm on a hot day when the temperature is 45.0 oC. What is the actual length of the steel rod on that day? Coefficient of linear expansion of steel = 1.20 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta l = \alpha l\Delta T $ = 1.20 $\times$ 10^−5 $\times$ 63 $\times$ 18 = 0.0136cm
final length = $l +\Delta l$ = 63.0136cm

questions.question: A large steel wheel is to be fitted on to a shaft of the same material. At 27 $^\circ $C, the outer diameter of the shaft is 8.70 cm and the diameter of the central hole in the wheel is 8.69 cm. The shaft is cooled using ‘dry ice’. At what temperature of the shaft does the wheel slip on the shaft? Assume the coefficient of linear expansion of the steel to be constant over the required temperature range: ${\alpha _{{\rm{steel}}}}$ = 1.20 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1.
questions.question_raw: A large steel wheel is to be fitted on to a shaft of the same material. At 27 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C, the outer diameter of the shaft is 8.70 cm and the diameter of the central hole in the wheel is 8.69 cm. The shaft is cooled using ‘dry ice’. At what temperature of the shaft does the wheel slip on the shaft? Assume the coefficient of linear expansion of the steel to be constant over the required temperature range: {tex}{\alpha _{{\rm{steel}}}}{/tex} = 1.20 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1.
questions.explanation:
$\alpha=\frac{\Delta l}{l \Delta T}$
$\Delta T=\frac{\Delta l}{l \alpha}=\frac{0.01}{8.70 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}=96 C^{\circ}$
final temp. = 27 − 96 = −69^oC

questions.question: A hole is drilled in a copper sheet. The diameter of the hole is 4.24 cm at 27.0^oC. What is the change in the diameter of the hole when the sheet is heated to 227^oC? Coefficient of linear expansion of copper = 1.70 $ \times $ 10^-5 K^–1.
questions.question_raw: A hole is drilled in a copper sheet. The diameter of the hole is 4.24 cm at 27.0oC. What is the change in the diameter of the hole when the sheet is heated to 227oC? Coefficient of linear expansion of copper = 1.70 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-5 K–1.
questions.explanation:
Change in diameter
$\Delta l = \alpha l\Delta T $
= 1.70 $\times$ 10^−5 $\times$ 4.24 $\times$ (227 − 27) = 1.44 $\times$10^−2 cm

questions.question: A brass wire 1.8 m long at 27 ^oC is held taut with little tension between two rigid supports. If the wire is cooled to a temperature of –39 $^\circ $C, what is the tension developed in the wire, if its diameter is 2.0 mm? Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1; Young’s modulus of brass = 0.91 $ \times $ 10^11 Pa.
questions.question_raw: A brass wire 1.8 m long at 27 oC is held taut with little tension between two rigid supports. If the wire is cooled to a temperature of –39 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C, what is the tension developed in the wire, if its diameter is 2.0 mm? Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1; Young’s modulus of brass = 0.91 {tex} \times {/tex} 1011 Pa.
questions.explanation:
Initial temperature, T_1 = 27^oC
Length of the brass wire at T_1, l = 1.8 m
Final temperature, T_2 = –39^oC
Diameter of the wire, d = 2.0 mm = 2 $\times$ 10^–3 m
Tension developed in the wire = F
Coefficient of linear expansion of brass, α = 2.0 $\times$ 10^–5 K^–1
Young’s modulus of brass, Y = 0.91 $\times$ 10^11 Pa
Young’s modulus is given by the relation:
γ = $Stress \over Strain$ = ${{F\over A} \over {\Delta L\over L}}$
$\Delta L = {{FL} \over {YA}}$ ....
Where,
F = Tension developed in the wire 
A = Area of a cross-section of the wire. 
$\Delta L$ = Change in the length, given by the relation:
$\Delta L = \alpha L({T_2} - {T_1})$ … (ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get:
$\alpha L\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)=\frac{F L}{Y A}$
$\alpha L\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)=\frac{F L}{Y \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}}$
$F=\alpha\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right) Y \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2}$
F = 2 $\times$ 10^-5 $\times$ (-39 - 27) $\times$ 3.14 $\times$ 0.91 $\times$ 10^11 $\times$ ($2 \times 10^{-3} \over 2$ )^2
= -3.8 $\times$ 10^2 N
(The negative sign indicates that the tension is directed inward.)
Hence, the tension developed in the wire is 3.8 $\times$10^2 N.

questions.question: A brass rod of length 50 cm and diameter 3.0 mm is joined to a steel rod of the same length and diameter. What is the change in length of the combined rod at 250 $^\circ $C, if the original lengths are at 40.0 $^\circ $C? The ends of the rod are free to expand (Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1, steel = 1.2 $ \times $ 10^−5 K^−1).
questions.question_raw: A brass rod of length 50 cm and diameter 3.0 mm is joined to a steel rod of the same length and diameter. What is the change in length of the combined rod at 250 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C, if the original lengths are at 40.0 {tex}^\circ {/tex}C? The ends of the rod are free to expand (Co-efficient of linear expansion of brass = 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1, steel = 1.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 K−1).
questions.explanation:
For the expansion in the brass rod
$\frac{\Delta l_{1}}{l_{1}}=\alpha_{1} \Delta T$
$\Delta l_{1}=l_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta T=50 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 210$ = 0.2205 cm
For the expansion in the steel rod
$\frac{\Delta l_{2}}{l_{2}}=\alpha_{2} \Delta T$
$\Delta l_{2}=l_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta T=50 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 210=0.126 \mathrm{cm}$ 
Total change in the lengths of brass and steel
$\Delta l = \Delta {l_1} + \Delta {l_2} $ = 0.2205 + 0.126 = 0.346cm
Total change in the length of the combined rod = 0.346 cm

questions.question: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerin is 49 $ \times $ 10^−5 /^ 0C. What is the fractional change in its density for a 30 ^oC rise in temperature?
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerin is 49 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−5 / 0C. What is the fractional change in its density for a 30 oC rise in temperature?
questions.explanation:
Coefficient of volume expansion of glycerine,
γ=49×10^-5/ ^0C
Rise in temperature, ΔT=30^ 0C
As, $\gamma = {1\over \Delta T}$$({\Delta V\over V})$
$({\Delta V\over V})$= $\gamma\times \Delta T$
or ΔV/V=(49×10^-5)×30=0.0147
Since mass remains constant,
Fractional change in density = fractional change in volume = 0.0147

questions.question: A 10 kW drilling machine is used to drill a bore in a small aluminum block of mass 8.0 kg. How much is the rise in temperature of the block in 2.5 minutes, assuming 50 percent of power is used up in heating the machine itself or lost to the surroundings. Specific heat of aluminum = 0.91 J g^−1K^−1.
questions.question_raw: A 10 kW drilling machine is used to drill a bore in a small aluminum block of mass 8.0 kg. How much is the rise in temperature of the block in 2.5 minutes, assuming 50 percent of power is used up in heating the machine itself or lost to the surroundings. Specific heat of aluminum = 0.91 J g−1K−1.
questions.explanation:
W = Pt = 10 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 2.5 $\times$ 60 = 150 $\times$ 10^4J
Energy used in heating = 50% = 75 $\times$ 10^4J
Q = ms$\Delta$T
$ \Delta T = {Q \over {ms}} $
$\Delta T = {{75 \times {{10}^4}} \over {8000 \times 0.91}}$
$\Delta T $ = 103^oC

questions.question: A copper block of mass 2.5 kg is heated in a furnace to a temperature of 500 ^oC and then placed on a large ice block. What is the maximum amount of ice that can melt? (Specific heat of copper = 0.39 J g^−1K^−1; the heat of fusion of water = 335 J g^−1).
questions.question_raw: A copper block of mass 2.5 kg is heated in a furnace to a temperature of 500 oC and then placed on a large ice block. What is the maximum amount of ice that can melt? (Specific heat of copper = 0.39 J g−1K−1; the heat of fusion of water = 335 J g−1).
questions.explanation:
Heat given by Cu = Heat Intake by ice
mC_us$\Delta$T = m_iceL
2500 $\times$ 0.39 $\times$ 500 = m_ice $\times$ 335
m_ice = 1.5Kg

questions.question: In an experiment on the specific heat of a metal, a 0.2 kg block of the metal at 150 ^oC is dropped in a copper calorimeter (of water equivalent 0.025 kg) containing 150 cm^3 of water at 27 ^oC. The final temperature is 40 ^oC, the specific heat of the metal will be
questions.question_raw: In an experiment on the specific heat of a metal, a 0.2 kg block of the metal at 150 oC is dropped in a copper calorimeter (of water equivalent 0.025 kg) containing 150 cm3 of water at 27 oC. The final temperature is 40 oC, the specific heat of the metal will be
questions.explanation:
Heat lost by the metal = Heat gained by the water and calorimeter system
$m_{1} c_{1} \Delta T_{1}=\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) c_{2} \Delta T_{2}$
200 $\times$ c_1 $\times$ 110 = (150 + 25) $\times$ 4.186 $\times$ 13
$c_{1}=\frac{175 \times 4.186 \times 13}{200 \times 110}$
c_1 = 0.43Jg^−1K^−1

questions.question: A child running a temperature of 101^oF is given an antipyrin (i.e. medicine that lowers fever) which causes an increase in the rate of evaporation of sweat from his body. If the fever is brought down to 98 ^oF in 20 min, what is the average rate of extra evaporation caused, by the drug? Assume the evaporation mechanism to be the only way by which heat is lost. The mass of the child is 30 kg. The specific heat of human body is approximately the same as that of water, and the latent heat of evaporation of water at that temperature is about 580 cal g^−1.
questions.question_raw: A child running a temperature of 101oF is given an antipyrin (i.e. medicine that lowers fever) which causes an increase in the rate of evaporation of sweat from his body. If the fever is brought down to 98 oF in 20 min, what is the average rate of extra evaporation caused, by the drug? Assume the evaporation mechanism to be the only way by which heat is lost. The mass of the child is 30 kg. The specific heat of human body is approximately the same as that of water, and the latent heat of evaporation of water at that temperature is about 580 cal g−1.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta T = 101 - 98 = 3^\circ F = \left( {3 \times {5 \over 9}} \right)^\circ C$ 
Specific heat of the human body = Specific heat of water = 1000 cal/kg/ ^oC
The heat lost by the child is Q = $ mc\Delta T = 30 \times 1000 \times \left( {3 \times {5 \over 9}} \right) $ = 50000cal
Let m_1 be the mass of the water evaporated from the child’s body in 20 min. 
Loss of heat through water is
Q = m_1L
${m_1} = {Q \over L} = {{50000} \over {580}} $ = 86.2gm
The average rate of extra evaporation caused by the drug = $m_1 \over t$
= $86.2 \over 20$
= 4.3 g/min.

questions.question: A ‘thermal’ icebox is a cheap and efficient method for storing small quantities of cooked food in summer in particular. A cubical icebox of side 30 cm has a thickness of 5.0 cm. If 4.0 kg of ice is put in the box, estimate the amount of ice remaining after 6 h. The outside temperature is 45 ^oC, and the coefficient of thermal conductivity of thermal is 0.01 J s^−1m^−1K^−1. [Heat of fusion of water = 335 $ \times $ 10^3Jkg^−1]
questions.question_raw: A ‘thermal’ icebox is a cheap and efficient method for storing small quantities of cooked food in summer in particular. A cubical icebox of side 30 cm has a thickness of 5.0 cm. If 4.0 kg of ice is put in the box, estimate the amount of ice remaining after 6 h. The outside temperature is 45 oC, and the coefficient of thermal conductivity of thermal is 0.01 J s−1m−1K−1. [Heat of fusion of water = 335 {tex} \times {/tex} 103Jkg−1]
questions.explanation:
$Q = {{KA\Delta T} \over l}t$ 
A = Surface area of the box = 6s^2 = 6 $\times$ (0.3)^2 = 0.54 m^3
Time gap, t = 6 h = 6 $\times$ 60 $\times$ 60 s
$Q = {{0.01 \times 0.54 \times 45 \times 6 \times 60 \times 60} \over {0.05}} $ = 104976J
But Q= m'L
$\therefore$ m' = $Q\over L$
= $104976\over(335 \times 10^3)$ = 0.313 kg
Mass of ice left = 4 – 0.3 = 3.7 kg
Hence, the amount of ice remaining after 6 h is 3.7 kg.

questions.question: A brass boiler has a base area of 0.15 m^2 and thickness 1.0 cm. It boils water at a rate of 6.0 kg/min when placed on a gas stove. Estimate the temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler. Thermal conductivity of brass = 109 J s^−1m^−1K^−1 ; Heat of vaporization of water = 2256 $ \times $ 10^3Jkg^−1.
questions.question_raw: A brass boiler has a base area of 0.15 m2 and thickness 1.0 cm. It boils water at a rate of 6.0 kg/min when placed on a gas stove. Estimate the temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler. Thermal conductivity of brass = 109 J s−1m−1K−1 ; Heat of vaporization of water = 2256 {tex} \times {/tex} 103Jkg−1.
questions.explanation:
The base area of the boiler, A = 0.15 m^2
Thickness of the boiler, l = 1.0 cm = 0.01 m
Boiling rate of water, R = 6.0 kg/min
Mass, m = 6 kg
Time, t = 1 min = 60 s
Thermal conductivity of brass, K = 109 J s ^–1 m^–1 K^–1
Heat of vaporisation, L = 2256 × 10^3 J kg^–1
The amount of heat flowing into the water through the brass base of the boiler is
$Q = {{KA({T_1} - {T_2})} \over l}t$ ......(1)
where,
T_1 = Temperature of the flame in contact with the boiler
T_2 = Boiling point of water = 100°C 
Heat required for boiling the water
Q = mL....…(ii)
Equating equations (i) and (ii), we get:
mL = $KA(T_1 - T_2) t \over l $
T_1 - T_2 = $mLl \over KAt$
= $6 \times 2256 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \over(109 \times 0.15 \times 60)$
= 137.98 ^oC
Therefore, the temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler is 237.98^oC.

questions.question: A body cools from 80 ^oC to 50 ^oC in 5 minutes. Calculate the time it takes to cool from 60 ^oC to 30 ^oC. The temperature of the surroundings is 20 ^oC.
questions.question_raw: A body cools from 80 oC to 50 oC in 5 minutes. Calculate the time it takes to cool from 60 oC to 30 oC. The temperature of the surroundings is 20 oC.
questions.explanation:
Initial temperature (T_i​) = 80 ^oC
Final temperature (T_f) = 50 ^oC
Temperature of the surrounding (T_0) = 20 ^oC
t = 5 min
According to Newton's law of cooling,
Rate of cooling $\frac {dt}{dT}$​ = $K({(T_i+T_F)\over 2}-T_0)$
$\frac {(T_f - T_i)}{t} = \frac {K[(80 + 50)]}{2-20}$
$\frac {80-50}{5}$​ = K[65 − 20]
6 = K $\times$ 45
K = ${6\over 45}={2\over 15}$
In second condition,
initial temperature =T_i = 60 ^oC
Final temperature T_f​ = 30 ^oC
Time taken for cooling is t
According to Newton's law of cooling 
$\frac {(60-30)}{t}$t ​ =${2\over 15}[{(60+30)\over 2}-20]$
$\frac {30}{t}=(\frac {2}{15})(25)$
$\therefore$ t = 9 min

questions.question: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0^oC. What is its length when the temperature is 40.0$^\circ $ C? $\alpha$ = 11 $\times$ 10^−6 (^oC)^-1.
questions.question_raw: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0oC. What is its length when the temperature is 40.0{tex}^\circ {/tex} C? {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 (oC)-1.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta l = \alpha l\Delta \theta $ =11 $\times$ 10^−6 $\times$ 30 $\times$ 40 = 0.013m
Total length after expansion = 30 + 0.013 = 30.013m

questions.question: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0^oC. Suppose that the ends of the rail are rigidly clamped at 0.0^oC so that expansion is prevented. What is the thermal stress set up in the rail if its temperature is raised to 40.0^oC? $\alpha $− steel = 11 $\times$ 10^−6 (^oC)^-1 Y-steel = 20GPa?
questions.question_raw: A steel railroad track has a length of 30.000 m when the temperature is 0.0oC. Suppose that the ends of the rail are rigidly clamped at 0.0oC so that expansion is prevented. What is the thermal stress set up in the rail if its temperature is raised to 40.0oC? {tex}\alpha {/tex}− steel = 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−6 (oC)-1 Y-steel = 20GPa?
questions.explanation: Thermal Stress $ = Y\alpha \Delta \theta $ = 20 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ 11 $\times$ 10^−6 $\times$ 40 = 8.8 $\times$ 10^6

questions.question: An ideal gas occupies a volume of 100 ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ at ${\rm{2}}0^\circ $ C and 100 Pa. Find the number of moles of gas in the container.
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas occupies a volume of 100 {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex} at {tex}{\rm{2}}0^\circ {/tex} C and 100 Pa. Find the number of moles of gas in the container.
questions.explanation:
number of moles according to gas equation
$n = {{PV} \over {RT}} = {{100 \times 100 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \over {8.31 \times 293}} = 4.10 \times {10^{ - 6}}$

When the temperature of the gas in the can reaches 195^oC, what is the pressure inside the can? Assume any change in the volume of the can is negligible.

questions.question: A student eats a dinner rated at 2000 Calories. He wishes to do an equivalent amount of work in the gymnasium by lifting a 50.0-kg barbell. How many times must he raise the barbell to expend this much energy? Assume that he raises the barbell 2.00 m each time he lifts it and that he regains no energy when he drops the barbell to the floor.
questions.question_raw: A student eats a dinner rated at 2000 Calories. He wishes to do an equivalent amount of work in the gymnasium by lifting a 50.0-kg barbell. How many times must he raise the barbell to expend this much energy? Assume that he raises the barbell 2.00 m each time he lifts it and that he regains no energy when he drops the barbell to the floor.
questions.explanation:
Total no. of times he lifts barbell = $Total \ energy \over energy \ in \ lifting \ barbell \ once$
$= {Q \over {mgh}}$
$= {{2000 \times 4.186 \times {{10}^3}} \over {50 \times 9.8 \times 2}} $ = 8.54 $\times$ 10^3 times
