questions.question: A cowboy fires a silver bullet with a mass of 2.00 g and with a muzzle speed of 200 m/s into the pine wall of a saloon. Assume that all the internal energy generated by the impact remains with the bullet. What is the temperature change of the bullet? Specific heat of silver is 234J/Kg^oC?
questions.question_raw: A cowboy fires a silver bullet with a mass of 2.00 g and with a muzzle speed of 200 m/s into the pine wall of a saloon. Assume that all the internal energy generated by the impact remains with the bullet. What is the temperature change of the bullet? Specific heat of silver is 234J/KgoC?
questions.explanation:
Q = K
$mc\Delta T = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
$ \Delta T = {{{v^2}} \over {2c}} = {{200 \times 200} \over {2 \times 234}} = 85.5^\circ C $

questions.question:
What mass of steam initially at 130$^\circ $C is needed to warm 200 g of water in a 100-g glass container from 20.0$^\circ $C to 50.0^oC? Specific heat of container is 837J/Kg^oC 
Specific heat of steam is 2110J/Kg^oC 
Specific heat of water is 4186J/Kg^oC 
Latent heat of vapourisation is 2.26 $\times$ 10^6J/Kg
questions.question_raw:
What mass of steam initially at 130{tex}^\circ {/tex}C is needed to warm 200 g of water in a 100-g glass container from 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C to 50.0oC? Specific heat of container is 837J/KgoC  
Specific heat of steam is 2110J/KgoC 
Specific heat of water is 4186J/KgoC 
Latent heat of vapourisation is 2.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 106J/Kg
questions.explanation:
Heat given by steam = heat taken by water + heat taken by the container
$m{s_s}\Delta {T_1} + mL + m{s_w}\Delta {T_2} = m{s_w}\Delta {T_3} + m{s_c}\Delta {T_3}$
$\left( {m \times 2110 \times 30} \right) + \left( {m \times 2.26 \times {{10}^6}} \right) + \left( {m \times 4186 \times 50} \right) $$= \left( {0.2 \times 4186 \times 30} \right) + \left( {0.1 \times 837 \times 30} \right) $
after solving
mass of steam m = 10.9gm

questions.question: Liquid helium has a very low boiling point, 4.2 K, and a very low latent heat of vaporization, 2.09 $\times$ 10^4 J/kg. If energy is transferred to a container of boiling liquid helium from an immersed electric heater at a rate of 10.0 W, how long does it take to boil away 1.00 kg of the liquid?
questions.question_raw: Liquid helium has a very low boiling point, 4.2 K, and a very low latent heat of vaporization, 2.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J/kg. If energy is transferred to a container of boiling liquid helium from an immersed electric heater at a rate of 10.0 W, how long does it take to boil away 1.00 kg of the liquid?
questions.explanation:
Q = mL= 1 $\times$ 2.09 $\times$ 10^4 = 2.09 $\times$10^4J
$\Delta t = {Q \over P} = {{2.09 \times {{10}^4}} \over {10}} $ = 2.09 $\times$ 10^3 sec = 35 min

questions.question: The temperature of a silver bar rises by 10.0$^\circ $C when it absorbs 1.23 kJ of energy by heat. The mass of the bar is 525 g. Determine the specific heat of silver?
questions.question_raw: The temperature of a silver bar rises by 10.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C when it absorbs 1.23 kJ of energy by heat. The mass of the bar is 525 g. Determine the specific heat of silver?
questions.explanation: $s = {Q \over {m\Delta T}} = {{1.23} \over {0.525 \times 10}} = 0.234KJ/Kg^\circ C$

questions.question: A 1.50-kg iron horseshoe initially at 600^oC is dropped into a bucket containing 20.0 kg of water at 25.0^oC. What is the final temperature? (Neglect the heat capacity of the container and assume that a negligible amount of water boils away.) Specific heat of iron = 448J/Kg^oC.
questions.question_raw: A 1.50-kg iron horseshoe initially at 600oC is dropped into a bucket containing 20.0 kg of water at 25.0oC. What is the final temperature? (Neglect the heat capacity of the container and assume that a negligible amount of water boils away.) Specific heat of iron = 448J/KgoC.
questions.explanation:
by the principle of calorimetry,
The heat has given by iron = Heat intake by water
$m_{F e} s_{F e} \Delta T_{1}=m_{w} s_{w} \Delta T_{2}$
1.5 $\times$ 448(600 - T) = 20 $\times$ 4186(T - 25)|
after solving
final temperature T = 29.6^oC

questions.question:
An aluminum calorimeter with a mass of 100 g contains 250 g of water. The calorimeter and water are in thermal equilibrium at 10.0$^\circ $C. Two metallic blocks are placed into the water. One is a 50.0-g piece of copper at 80.0$^\circ $C; the other block has a mass of 70.0 g and is originally at a temperature of 100$^\circ $C. The entire system stabilizes at a final temperature of 20.0$^\circ $C. Determine the specific heat of the unknown sample.
Specific heat of aluminium is 0.215cal/gm^oC 
Specific heat of copper is 0.0924cal/gm^oC
questions.question_raw:
An aluminum calorimeter with a mass of 100 g contains 250 g of water. The calorimeter and water are in thermal equilibrium at 10.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. Two metallic blocks are placed into the water. One is a 50.0-g piece of copper at 80.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C; the other block has a mass of 70.0 g and is originally at a temperature of 100{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. The entire system stabilizes at a final temperature of 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. Determine the specific heat of the unknown sample.
Specific heat of aluminium is  0.215cal/gmoC 
Specific heat of copper is 0.0924cal/gmoC
questions.explanation:
Heat given by Copper(1) + heat given by unknown metal(2) = heat gain by aluminum (3) + heat gain by water(4)
${m_1}{s_1}\Delta {T_1} + {m_2}{s_2}\Delta {T_2} $ = ${m_3}{s_3}\Delta {T_3} + {m_4}{s_4}\Delta {T_4} $ 
(50 $\times$ 0.0924 $\times$ 60) + (70 $\times$ s_2 $\times$ 80) = (100 $\times$ 0.215 $\times$10) + (250 $\times$ 1 $\times$10)
after solving 
specific heat of unknown metal s_2 = 0.435cal/gm^oC

questions.question: A 3.00-g copper penny at 25.0$^\circ $C drops from a height of 50.0 m to the ground. (a) If 60.0% of the change in potential energy goes into increasing the internal energy, what is its final temperature?
questions.question_raw: A 3.00-g copper penny at 25.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C drops from a height of 50.0 m to the ground. (a) If 60.0% of the change in potential energy goes into increasing the internal energy, what is its final temperature?
questions.explanation:
mgh = 3 $\times$ 10^−3$\times$10 $\times$ 50 = 1.5J
Heat used in increasing temp 
$Q=1.5 \times \frac{60}{100}=0.9 J$
Q = ms $\Delta$T_0.9 = 3 $\times$ 0.385 $\times$ (T−298)
T = 298.8K = 25.8^oC

questions.question: A water heater is operated by solar power. If the solar collector has an area of 6.00 m^2 and the power delivered by sunlight is 550 W/ m^2, how long does it take to increase the temperature of 1.00 m^3 of water from 20.0^oC to 60.0^oC?
questions.question_raw: A water heater is operated by solar power. If the solar collector has an area of 6.00 m2 and the power delivered by sunlight is 550 W/ m2, how long does it take to increase the temperature of 1.00 m3 of water from 20.0oC to 60.0oC?
questions.explanation:
$Q = ms\Delta T$
$ P = {W \over t} = {{ms\Delta T} \over t} $ 
$t = {{ms\Delta T} \over P} = {{1000 \times 4186 \times 40} \over {550 \times 6}} $ = 50700 sec = 50.7 ksec

questions.question: A 3.00-g lead bullet at 30.0^oC is fired at a speed of 240 m/s into a large block of ice at 0^oC, in which it becomes embedded. What quantity of ice melts? Latent heat of ice = 3.33 $\times$ 10^-5 J/Kg, the specific heat of lead = 128J/Kg^oC?
questions.question_raw: A 3.00-g lead bullet at 30.0oC is fired at a speed of 240 m/s into a large block of ice at 0oC, in which it becomes embedded. What quantity of ice melts? Latent heat of ice = 3.33 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 J/Kg, the specific heat of lead = 128J/KgoC?
questions.explanation:
${1 \over 2}{m_{Pb}}{v^2} + {m_{Pb}}{s_{Pb}}(30 - 0) = {m_{ice}}{L_{ice}}$
($1\over 2$ $\times$ 3 $\times$ 10^−3 $\times$ 240 $\times$ 240)+(3 $\times$ 10^−3 $\times$ 128 $\times$ 30) = m_ice $\times$ 3.33 $\times$ 10^5
m_ice = 2.94gm

questions.question: A 1.00-kg block of copper at 20.0$^\circ $C is dropped into a large vessel of liquid nitrogen at 77.3 K. How many kilograms of nitrogen boil away by the time the copper reaches 77.3 K? (The specific heat of copper is 0.092 0 cal/g. $^\circ $C. The latent heat of vaporization of nitrogen is 48.0 cal/g.)
questions.question_raw: A 1.00-kg block of copper at 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C is dropped into a large vessel of liquid nitrogen at 77.3 K. How many kilograms of nitrogen boil away by the time the copper reaches 77.3 K? (The specific heat of copper is 0.092 0 cal/g. {tex}^\circ {/tex}C. The latent heat of vaporization of nitrogen is 48.0 cal/g.)
questions.explanation:
Heat is given by copper = Heat gain by nitrogen
ms$\Delta$T = mN_2L
1000 $\times$ 0.092 $\times$ 215.7 = mN_2 × 48
mN_2 = 414gm = 0.414Kg

questions.question: In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0^oC is added to 600 g of water at 18.0^oC. What is the final temperature of the system?
questions.question_raw: In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0oC is added to 600 g of water at 18.0oC. What is the final temperature of the system?
questions.explanation:
maximum heat given by water is $Q = {m_{water}}s\Delta T $ =600 $\times$ 1 $\times$ (18 − 0) = 10800cal
Heat required to melt the complete ice = m_ice L = 250 $\times$ 80 = 20000cal
ice remains unmelt is given by = ${{20000 - 10800} \over {80}} = {{9200} \over {80}} $ = 115gm
Heat given by water is not sufficient to meet the complete ice. so that temp of the system is $0^\circ C$ with some ice remains unmelt

questions.question:
In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0$^\circ $C is added to 600 g of water at 18.0$^\circ $C. How much ice remains when the system reaches equilibrium?
given that latent heat of fusion of ice = 79.5 cal/gm, the specific heat of water = 1cal/gm^oC.
questions.question_raw:
In an insulated vessel, 250 g of ice at 0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C is added to 600 g of water at 18.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. How much ice remains when the system reaches equilibrium?
given that latent heat of fusion of ice = 79.5 cal/gm, the specific heat of water = 1cal/gmoC.
questions.explanation:
maximum heat given by water is
${Q_1} = {m_w}{s_w}\Delta T = 600 \times 1 \times (18 - 0) = 10800cal$
The heat required to melt the complete ice = Q_2 = m_ice L
Q_2 = 250 $\times$ 79.5 = 19875cal
Heat given by water is not sufficient to melt complete ice. Hence the temperature of the mixture will be $0^\circ C$ with some ice remains unmelt.
amount of ice melt = $m = {{{Q_1}} \over L} = {{10800} \over {79.5}} $ = 136gm
L = latent heat of fusion of ice = 79.5 cal/gm
Amount of ice which remains unmelt at equilibrium = 250 - 136 = 114 gm

questions.question:
If 90.0 g of molten lead at 327.3^oC is poured into a 300-g casting form made of iron and initially at 20.0^oC, what is the final temperature of the system? (Assume that no energy loss to the environment occurs.) Latent heat of fusion of lead = 2.45 $\times$ 10^4 J/kg, specific heat of lead = 128J/Kg^oC
specific heat of iron = 448J/Kg^oC.
questions.question_raw:
If 90.0 g of molten lead at 327.3oC is poured into a 300-g casting form made of iron and initially at 20.0oC, what is the final temperature of the system? (Assume that no energy loss to the environment occurs.) Latent heat of fusion of lead = 2.45  {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J/kg, specific heat of lead = 128J/KgoC
specific heat of iron = 448J/KgoC.
questions.explanation:
The melting point of lead is given by 327.3^o C.
Assume the final temperature of the system is T. Then the amount of energy released by the lead as it solidifies is given by 
ΔQ = m_leadL_lead = 0.09 kg*(2.45*10^4 J/kg) = 2205 J,
and the amount of energy released as it cools is given by :-
ΔQ = m_leadc_leadΔT = 0.09 kg*(128 J/(kg^oC))(327.3 ^oC - T) = (11.52 J/^oC)(327.3 ^oC - T).
This energy is absorbed by the iron. Thus,
2205 J + (11.52 J/^oC) (327.3^oC - T) = m_ironc_ironΔT = 0.3 kg*(448 J/(kg^oC))(T - 20 ^oC).
5975.5 J - (11.52 J/^oC)T = (134 J/^oC)T - 2688 J.
8663.5 J = (145.52 J/^oC)T.
T = 59.5^o C.

questions.question:
A surveyor uses a steel measuring tape that is exactly 50.000 m long at a temperature of 20$^\circ $C. The markings on the tape are calibrated for this temperature.
 What is the length of the tape when the temperature is 35^oC?
 When it is 35^oC, the surveyor uses the tape to measure a distance. The value that she reads off the tape is 35.794 m. What is the actual distance? $\alpha$ = 1.2 $\times$ 10^-5oC
questions.question_raw:
A surveyor uses a steel measuring tape that is exactly 50.000 m long at a temperature of 20{tex}^\circ {/tex}C. The markings on the tape are calibrated for this temperature.

  1. What is the length of the tape when the temperature is 35oC?
  2. When it is 35oC, the surveyor uses the tape to measure a distance. The value that she reads off the tape is 35.794 m. What is the actual distance? {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5oC
questions.explanation:
$\alpha = {{\Delta l} \over l}{1 \over {\Delta T}}$
$\Delta l = l\alpha \Delta T$ = 50 $\times$ 1.2 $\times$ 10^-5 $\times$ 15 = 0.009m
final length = 50 + 0.009m = 50.009m
if actual distance = x
then $\Delta x = x\alpha \Delta T$
x−35.794 = x $\times$ 1.2 $\times$ 10^−5 $\times$ 15
x = 35.800m

questions.question: A 200- ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ glass flask is filled to the brim with mercury at 20^oC. How much mercury overflows when the temperature of the system is raised to 100$^\circ $ C.The coefficient of linear expansion of the glass is 0.40 $\times$ 10^−5/^oC. Coefficient of volume expansion of mercury is 1.82 $\times$ 10^-4/^oC.
questions.question_raw: A 200- {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}{/tex} glass flask is filled to the brim with mercury at 20oC. How much mercury overflows when the temperature of the system is raised to 100{tex}^\circ {/tex} C.The coefficient of linear expansion of the glass is 0.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−5/oC. Coefficient of volume expansion of mercury is 1.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4/oC.
questions.explanation:
coefficient of linear expansion of the glass ${\gamma _S} $ = 0.40 $\times$ 10^−5/^oC
Coefficient of volume expansion of mercury ${a_L} $ = 1.82 $\times$ 10^-4/^oC
The number of mercury overflows
$\Delta V = ({\gamma _L} - {\gamma _S})V\Delta \theta$
${\gamma _S} $ = 3α = 3 $\times$ 0.4 $\times$ 10^−5 = 0.12 $\times$ 10^−4
ΔV = (1.82−0.12) $\times$ 10^−4 $\times$ 200 $\times$ 80 = 2.7cm^3
mercury overflows = 2.7cm^3

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Thermodynamics

questions.question: The picture of heat as a fluid was discarded in favor of the modern concept of heat as a form of energy because
questions.question_raw: The picture of heat as a fluid was discarded in favor of the modern concept of heat as a form of energy because

options.label: A
options.text: Count Rumford observed that boring of brass cannon generated a lot of heat

options.label: B
options.text: Count Rumford observed that boring of brass cannon generated a lot of smoke

options.label: C
options.text: Count Rumford observed that boring of brass cannon generated a lot of noise

options.label: D
options.text: Count Rumford observed that boring of brass cannon generated a lot of dust

correct_option_texts: Count Rumford observed that boring of brass cannon generated a lot of heat
questions.explanation: If heat were actually a substance, as many scientists of the time believed, it was difficult to see how so much of it could be produced during the boring of a metal cannon. Rumford showed that the amount of heat was not related to the quantity of filings produced

questions.question: Thermodynamics is the branch of physics that deals with:
questions.question_raw: Thermodynamics is the branch of physics that deals with:

options.label: A
options.text: the concepts of kinetic energy and temperature and the inter-conversion of mechanical and other forms of energy

options.label: B
options.text: the concepts of heat and temperature and the inter-conversion of heat and other forms of energy

options.label: C
options.text: the concepts of heat and noise and the inter-conversion of noise and other forms of energy

options.label: D
options.text: the concepts of heat and dust and the inter-conversion of heat and other forms of matter

correct_option_texts: the concepts of heat and temperature and the inter-conversion of heat and other forms of energy
questions.explanation: Thermodynamics is a branch of physics concerned with heat and temperature and their relation to energy and work. It deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy (such as mechanical, electrical, or chemical energy), and, by extension, of the relationships between all forms of energy.

questions.question: The thermodynamic state of a system is an equilibrium state:
questions.question_raw: The thermodynamic state of a system is an equilibrium state:

options.label: A
options.text: if the macroscopic variables that characterize the system do not change in time

options.label: B
options.text: if the macroscopic variables that characterize the system change randomly

options.label: C
options.text: if the macroscopic variables that characterize the system change linearly in time

options.label: D
options.text: if the macroscopic variables that characterize the system change decrease in time

correct_option_texts: if the macroscopic variables that characterize the system do not change in time
questions.explanation: In thermodynamic equilibrium, there are no net macroscopic flows of matter or of energy, either within a system or between systems.

questions.question: Adiabatic wall is:
questions.question_raw: Adiabatic wall is:

options.label: A
options.text: a thermally insulating wall that does not allow flow of heat from one body to another

options.label: B
options.text: a thermally conducting wall that does not allow flow of heat from one body to many

options.label: C
options.text: a thermally conducting wall that does not allow the flow of heat from one body to another

options.label: D
options.text: a thermally insulating wall that allows the flow of heat from one body to another

correct_option_texts: a thermally insulating wall that does not allow flow of heat from one body to another
questions.explanation: In thermodynamics, an adiabatic wall between two thermodynamic systems does not allow heat or matter to pass across it. In theoretical investigations, it is sometimes assumed that one of the two systems is the surroundings of the other.

questions.question: The diathermic wall is:
questions.question_raw: The diathermic wall is:

options.label: A
options.text: a thermally conducting wall that allows flow of heat from one body to another

options.label: B
options.text: a thermally conducting wall that does not allow flow of heat from one body to another

options.label: C
options.text: a thermally insulating wall that does not allow flow of heat from one body to another

options.label: D
options.text: a thermally insulating wall that allows flow of heat from one body to another

correct_option_texts: a thermally conducting wall that allows flow of heat from one body to another
questions.explanation: A wall which prevents thermal interaction is known as adiabatic wall and a system enclosed within an adiabatic wall is called thermally isolated. If exchange of heat takes place between the system and surroundings through the boundary wall, the boundary is called diathermic wall.

questions.question: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
questions.question_raw: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics

options.label: A
options.text: states that two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third system simultaneously are never in thermal equilibrium with each other

options.label: B
options.text: states that two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third system separately cannot be in thermal equilibrium with each other

options.label: C
options.text: states that a system in thermal equilibrium with a third system separately is in thermal equilibrium with any other

options.label: D
options.text: states that two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third system separately are in thermal equilibrium with each other

correct_option_texts: states that two systems in thermal equilibrium with a third system separately are in thermal equilibrium with each other
questions.explanation:
The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that if twothermodynamic systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, then they are in thermal equilibrium with each other.
More simply:
if $\eqalign{ & {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{B}}} \cr & {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{A}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{C}}} \cr & {\rm{Then}} \cr & {{\rm{T}}_{\rm{B}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{T}}_{\rm{C}}} \cr} $
It is called the zeroth (0^th) law of thermodynamics because it was discovered after the others, but people thought it was more important so they wanted to give it a number before the other laws.

questions.question: Internal energy of a system:
questions.question_raw: Internal energy of a system:

options.label: A
options.text: is the difference of the kinetic energies and potential energies of the molecules of the system

options.label: B
options.text: is the change in the sum of the kinetic energies and potential energies of the molecules of the system

options.label: C
options.text: is the sum of the kinetic energies and potential energies of the molecules of the system

options.label: D
options.text: is the change in the difference of the kinetic energies and potential energies of the molecules of the system

correct_option_texts: is the sum of the kinetic energies and potential energies of the molecules of the system
questions.explanation: In thermodynamics, the internal energy of a system is the energy contained within the system. It is the energy in a system arising from the relative positions and interactions of its parts.

questions.question: Internal energy of a system is:
questions.question_raw: Internal energy of a system is:
questions.explanation: A state function describes the equilibrium state of a system. For example, internal energy, enthalpy, and entropy are state quantities because they describe quantitatively an equilibrium state of a thermodynamic system, irrespective of how the system arrived in that state.

questions.question: First Law of Thermodynamics is based on the law of:
questions.question_raw: First Law of Thermodynamics is based on the  law of:
questions.explanation:
The first law of thermodynamics is the application of the conservation of energy principle to heat and thermodynamic processes
$\Delta$Q = $\Delta$U + $\Delta$W

questions.question: If $\Delta $Q is the energy supplied to the system $\Delta $U the change in internal energy, and $\Delta $W the work done on the environment, First Law of Thermodynamics states that:
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\Delta {/tex}Q is the energy supplied to the system {tex}\Delta {/tex}U the change in internal energy, and {tex}\Delta {/tex}W the work done on the environment, First Law of Thermodynamics states that:
questions.explanation: Heat given to a system is equal to the sum of an increase in its internal energy and the work done by the system against the surroundings.

questions.question: For solids near room temperature the molar specific heat (R is the universal gas constant):
questions.question_raw: For solids near room temperature the molar specific heat (R is the universal gas constant):
questions.explanation: The molar specific heats of solids also demonstrate a marked temperature dependence. Solids have molar specific heats that generally decrease in a nonlinear manner with decreasing temperature and approach zero as the temperature approaches absolute zero. At high temperatures (usually above 300 K), the molar specific heats approach the value of 3R ≈ 25 J/mol.K, a result known as the DuLong–Petit law.

questions.question: If C_p and C_v are molar specific heat capacities of an ideal gas at constant pressure and volume respectively and R is the universal gas constant, the relation between the three is:
questions.question_raw: If Cp and Cv are molar specific heat capacities of an ideal gas at constant pressure and volume respectively and R is the universal gas constant, the relation between the three is:
questions.explanation:
This is Meyer's Equation
At constant volume as there is no work done on the gas(ΔV = 0) all the heat supplied goes for the increase of internal energy. Hence the temperature of the body increases with less supply of heat. Whereas at constant pressure heat supplied accounts for both internal energy and work done on the gas.
if we increase temp. of 1 mole of gas by 1K or 1C then
PΔV = nR$\Delta$T
n = 1mole
$\Delta$T = 1^oC
P$\Delta$V = R
W = P$\Delta$V
C_P > C_V
C_P−C_V = W
C_P−C_V = R
This is also called Meyer's formula.

questions.question: In Thermodynamics Extensive variables depend:
questions.question_raw: In Thermodynamics Extensive variables depend:
questions.explanation:
The variables which depend on the size or amount of the substance are called extensive variables.
Example: Mass, volume etc.

questions.question: In the case of the isobaric process, the volume of an ideal gas changes the work done by the gas is given by:
questions.question_raw: In the case of the isobaric process, the volume of an ideal gas changes the work done by the gas is given by:
questions.explanation:
dW = F.dy = PA.dy = P.dV
$ W = \int\limits_{{V_1}}^{{V_2}} {P.dV} $ 
W = P(V_2 − V_1) = P$\Delta$V

questions.question: In a quasi-static process,
questions.question_raw: In a quasi-static process,

options.label: A
options.text: the temperature of the surrounding reservoir and the external pressure differ substantially from the temperature and pressure of the system

options.label: B
options.text: the temperature of the surrounding reservoir and the external pressure equal the temperature and pressure of the system

options.label: C
options.text: the temperature of the surrounding reservoir differ only infinitesimally from the temperature of the system

options.label: D
options.text: the temperature of the surrounding reservoir and the external pressure differ only infinitesimally from the temperature and pressure of the system

correct_option_texts: the temperature of the surrounding reservoir and the external pressure differ only infinitesimally from the temperature and pressure of the system
questions.explanation: A quasi-static process is a thermodynamic process that happens slowly enough for the system to remain in internal equilibrium.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Thermodynamics

questions.question: In which type of process is the volume constant:
questions.question_raw: In which type of process is the volume constant:
questions.explanation: An isochoric process is a thermodynamic process during which the volume of the closed system undergoing such a process remains constant.

questions.question: The heat engine takes
questions.question_raw: The heat engine takes

options.label: A
options.text: heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ from a cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$, releases heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ to a cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and delivers work W to the surroundings

options.label: B
options.text: heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ from a hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$ , releases heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ to a cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and delivers work W to the surroundings

options.label: C
options.text: heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ from a hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$, releases heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ to a hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and delivers work W to the surroundings

options.label: D
options.text: heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ from a cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$, releases heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ to a hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and delivers work W to the surroundings

correct_option_texts: heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ from a hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$ , releases heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ to a cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$ and delivers work W to the surroundings
questions.explanation: a heat engine converts heat energy to mechanical work by using the temperature difference between a hot "source" and a cold "sink". Heat is transferred from the source, through the "working body" of the engine, to the "sink", and in this process some of the heat changes into work by using the qualities of the gas or liquid inside the engine.

questions.question: The efficiency $(\eta)$ of a heat engine is defined by (W is the output and ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ the heat input):
questions.question_raw: The efficiency {tex}(\eta){/tex} of a heat engine is defined by (W is the output and {tex}{{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}{/tex} the heat input):
questions.explanation: $\eta=\frac{\text { WorkDone }}{\text { HeatInput }}=\frac{W}{Q_1}=\frac{Q_1-Q_2}{Q_1}$

questions.question: A refrigerator's working substance
questions.question_raw: A refrigerator's working substance

options.label: A
options.text: extracts heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ from the hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$, some external work W is done by it and heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ is released to the cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$

options.label: B
options.text: extracts heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ from the cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$, some external work W is done on it and heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ is released to the hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$

options.label: C
options.text: extracts heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ from the cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$, some external work W is done on it and heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ is released to the hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$

options.label: D
options.text: extracts heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ from the hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$, some external work W is done on it and heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ is released to the hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$

correct_option_texts: extracts heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{2}}}$ from the cold reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{2}}}$, some external work W is done on it and heat ${{\rm{Q}}_{\rm{1}}}$ is released to the hot reservoir at temperature ${{\rm{T}}_{\rm{1}}}$
questions.explanation: According to the second law of thermodynamics heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder location to a hotter area; work is required to achieve this.

questions.question: The coefficient of performance K of a refrigerator is given by:
questions.question_raw: The coefficient of performance K of a refrigerator is given by:
questions.explanation:
The performance of refrigerator is expressed by means of cofficient of performance
cofficient of performance = Heat extracted / work done = $\frac{Q_2}{W}$

questions.question: Kelvin-Planck statement of Second Law of Thermodynamics states that
questions.question_raw: Kelvin-Planck statement of Second Law of Thermodynamics states that

options.label: A
options.text: Processes are not possible whose sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and the partial conversion of the heat into work.

options.label: B
options.text: No process is possible whose sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and the partial conversion of the heat into work.

options.label: C
options.text: Processes are possible whose sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and the complete conversion of the heat into work.

options.label: D
options.text: No process is possible whose sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and the complete conversion of the heat into work.

correct_option_texts: No process is possible whose sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and the complete conversion of the heat into work.
questions.explanation: It is impossible to devise a cyclically operating device, the sole effect of which is to absorb energy in the form of heat from a single thermal reservoir and to deliver an equivalent amount of work. This implies that it is impossible to build a heat engine that has 100% thermal efficiency.

questions.question: Clausius statement of Second Law of Thermodynamics states that:
questions.question_raw: Clausius statement of Second Law of Thermodynamics states that:

options.label: A
options.text: Many processes are possible whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a colder object to a hotter object

options.label: B
options.text: No process is possible whose sole result is the transfer of work from a hotter object to a hotter object

options.label: C
options.text: No process is possible whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a colder object to a hotter object

options.label: D
options.text: No process is possible whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a hotter object to a colder object

correct_option_texts: No process is possible whose sole result is the transfer of heat from a colder object to a hotter object
questions.explanation: It is impossible for a self-acting machine unaided by any external agency to transfer heat from a cold to a hot reservoir. In other words, heat by itself cannot pass from a colder to a hotter body.

questions.question: The spontaneous processes of nature are:
questions.question_raw: The spontaneous processes of nature are:
questions.explanation: Spontaneous processes are irreversible because they can be reversed only by taking a different path to get back to their original state. A reversible process can take the same path to return to its original state.

questions.question: A thermodynamic process (state i $ \to $ state f) is reversible
questions.question_raw: A thermodynamic process (state i {tex} \to {/tex} state f) is reversible

options.label: A
options.text: if the process can be turned back such that both the system and the surroundings return to their original states, with no other change anywhere else in the universe.

options.label: B
options.text: if the process can be turned back such that both the system and the surroundings return to different states, with lots of change in the universe.

options.label: C
options.text: if the process can be turned back such that both the system and the surroundings return to their different states, with no other change anywhere else in the universe.

options.label: D
options.text: if the process can be turned back such that both the system and the surroundings return to their original states, with lots of change in the universe.

correct_option_texts: if the process can be turned back such that both the system and the surroundings return to their original states, with no other change anywhere else in the universe.
questions.explanation: Irreversible process is one which can be reversed in such a way that all changes taking place in the direct process are exactly replaced in the inverse order and opposite sense and no changes are left behind.

questions.question: The Carnot cycle consists of:
questions.question_raw: The Carnot cycle consists of:

options.label: A
options.text: Two isobaric processes connected by two adiabatic processes

options.label: B
options.text: Two isothermal processes connected by two adiabatic processes

options.label: C
options.text: Two isochoric processes connected by two adiabatic processes

options.label: D
options.text: Two isothermal processes connected by two isobaric processes

correct_option_texts: Two isothermal processes connected by two adiabatic processes
questions.explanation:
The Carnot cycle consists of the following four processes:
 A reversible isothermal gas expansion process
 A reversible adiabatic gas expansion process
 A reversible isothermal gas compression process
 A reversible adiabatic gas compression process

questions.question: An engine operating between two temperatures:
questions.question_raw: An engine operating between two temperatures:

options.label: A
options.text: can have a coefficient of performance greater than that of the Carnot engine

options.label: B
options.text: can have an efficiency greater than that of the Carnot engine

options.label: C
options.text: can not have an efficiency greater than that of the Carnot engine

options.label: D
options.text: efficiency does not depend on the temperature

correct_option_texts: can not have an efficiency greater than that of the Carnot engine
questions.explanation:
The efficiency of an engine is given by
$\eta = 1 - {{{T_L}} \over {{T_H}}}$ 
T_L = is the temperature of the sink
T_H = is the temperature of source
maximum efficiency of the engine depends only on temperature.
so that any engine can not have an efficiency greater than that of the Carnot engine

questions.question: A geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 3.0 liters per minute from 27 ^oC to 77 ^oC. If the geyser operates on a gas burner, what is the rate of consumption of the fuel if its heat of combustion is 4.0 $ \times $ 10^4 J/g?
questions.question_raw: A geyser heats water flowing at the rate of 3.0 liters per minute from 27 oC to 77 oC. If the geyser operates on a gas burner, what is the rate of consumption of the fuel if its heat of combustion is 4.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 104 J/g?
questions.explanation:
Q = ms $\Delta$T = 3000 $\times$ 4.2 $\times$ 50
m = mass of water 
s = specific heat of the water
the heat of combustion is 4.0 $ \times $ 10^4 J/g
rate of consumption of the fuel = Q / Heat of combustion
$ = {{3000 \times 4.2 \times 50} \over {4 \times {{10}^4}}} $ = 16gm/min

questions.question: What amount of heat must be supplied to 2.0 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{-{\rm{2}}}}$ kg of nitrogen (at room temperature) to raise its temperature by 45 ^oC at constant pressure? (Molecular mass of N_2 = 28; R = 8.3 J mol^−1 K^−1.)
questions.question_raw: What amount of heat must be supplied to 2.0 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{-{\rm{2}}}}{/tex} kg of nitrogen (at room temperature) to raise its temperature by 45 oC at constant pressure? (Molecular mass of N2 = 28; R = 8.3 J mol−1 K−1.)
questions.explanation:
$Q = n{C_v}\Delta T = {{2 \times {{10}^{ - 2}} \times {{10}^{ 3}}} \over {28}} \times {7 \over 2} \times 8.3 \times 45 $ = 934J
$n=\frac{\text { wtingm }}{\text { Molecularwt }}$
C_v = $7 \over 2$ R for Diatomic Gas

questions.question: A cylinder with a movable piston contains 3 moles of hydrogen at standard temperature and pressure. The walls of the cylinder are made of a heat insulator, and the piston is insulated by having a pile of sand on it. By what factor does the pressure of the gas increase if the gas is compressed to half its original volume?
questions.question_raw: A cylinder with a movable piston contains 3 moles of hydrogen at standard temperature and pressure. The walls of the cylinder are made of a heat insulator, and the piston is insulated by having a pile of sand on it. By what factor does the pressure of the gas increase if the gas is compressed to half its original volume?
questions.explanation:
$P V^{\gamma}=\text {Const}$
$P V^{\gamma}=P_{f}\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{\gamma}$
For H_2, $\gamma$ = 1.4
P_f = P_2^1.4 = 2.6

questions.question: In changing the state of a gas adiabatically from an equilibrium state A to another equilibrium state B, an amount of work equal to 22.3 J is done on the system. If the gas is taken from state A to B via a process in which the net heat absorbed by the system is 9.35 cal, how much is the net work done by the system in the latter case?
questions.question_raw: In changing the state of a gas adiabatically from an equilibrium state A to another equilibrium state B, an amount of work equal to 22.3 J is done on the system. If the gas is taken from state A to B via a process in which the net heat absorbed by the system is 9.35 cal, how much is the net work done by the system in the latter case?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + W$ 
In the adiabatic process $\Delta Q = 0$
$\Delta U = - W$ 
Given that W = -22.3J
$\Delta U $ = 22.3J
in second process $\Delta Q = 9.35 \times 4.18 $ = 39.083J
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + W$ 
ΔQ = 9.35 $\times$ 4.18 = 39.083J
$\Delta$Q = $\Delta$U + W
39.083 = 22.3+W
W = 16.8J

questions.question: A steam engine delivers 5.4$ \times $ 10^8 J of work per minute and services 3.6 $\times$ 10^9J of heat per minute from its boiler. What is the efficiency of the engine? How much heat is wasted per minute?
questions.question_raw: A steam engine delivers 5.4{tex} \times {/tex} 108 J of work per minute and services 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 109J of heat per minute from its boiler. What is the efficiency of the engine? How much heat is wasted per minute?
questions.explanation:
$\eta=\frac{\text { WorkDone }}{\text { HeatExtracted }}=\frac{5.4 \times 10^{8}}{3.6 \times 10^{9}}$ = 0.15 = 15 %
Heatwasted = Heat Extracted − Work Done
Heat Wasted = 3.6 $\times$ 10^9 − 5.4 $\times$ 10^8 = 3.06 $\times$ 10^9J

questions.question: An electric heater supplies heat to a system at a rate of 100W. If the system performs work at the rate of 75 joules per second, at what rate is the internal energy increasing?
questions.question_raw: An electric heater supplies heat to a system at a rate of 100W. If the system performs work at the rate of 75 joules per second, at what rate is the internal energy increasing?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta$Q = $\Delta$U + W
$\Delta U = \Delta Q - W$
=100 − 75 = 25Watt

questions.question: A thermodynamic system is taken from an original state to an intermediate state by the linear process shown in Figure. Its volume is then reduced to the original value from E to F by an isobaric process. Calculate the total work done by the gas from D to E to F
questions.question_raw: A thermodynamic system is taken from an original state to an intermediate state by the linear process shown in Figure. Its volume is then reduced to the original value from E to F by an isobaric process. Calculate the total work done by the gas from D to E to F
questions.explanation:
Work done by the gas is equal to the area under P-V curve. Total work done by the gas from D to E to F = Area of $\triangle$DEF is given by:-
Area of $\triangle$DEF is given by =($\frac {1}{2}$)DE $\times$ EF
Where,
DF = Change in pressure is
=600N/m^2 - 300N/m^2
= 300N/m^2
FE = Change in volume is
=5.0m^2 - 2.0m^2
= 3.0m^2
Area of $\triangle$DEF is given by = ($\frac {1}{2}$) $\times$ 300 $\times$ 3 = 450J
Therefore, the total work done by the gas from D to E to F is 450J.

in the isothermal process $\Delta U = 0$ 
hence $\Delta Q = W$ 
$Q = nRT{\log _e}{{{V_f}} \over {{V_i}}}$ 
$Q = 1 \times 8.31 \times 273 \times 2.303{\log _{10}}{{10} \over 3} $ = 2.7 $\times$ 10^3J

questions.question: A 1.0-kg bar of copper is heated at atmospheric pressure. If its temperature increases from 20^oC to 50^oC, what is the work done by the copper on the surrounding atmosphere? Given that for Cu $3\alpha $= 5.1 $\times$ 10^-5 /C, ${\rho _{Cu}} $ = 8.92 $\times$ 10^3Kg/m^3.
questions.question_raw: A 1.0-kg bar of copper is heated at atmospheric pressure. If its temperature increases from 20oC to 50oC, what is the work done by the copper on the surrounding atmosphere? Given  that for Cu {tex}3\alpha {/tex}= 5.1  {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 /C, {tex}{\rho _{Cu}} {/tex} = 8.92 {tex}\times{/tex} 103Kg/m3.
questions.explanation:
$\Delta V = 3\alpha {V_i}\Delta T = 5.1 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 30 \times {V_i} = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{V_i}$
$\Delta V = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{m \over {{\rho _{Cu}}}} $ $= 1.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{1 \over {8.92 \times {{10}^3}}} = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 7}}$
W = P$\Delta$V = 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 $\times$ 1.7 $\times$ 10^−7 = 1.7 $\times$10^−2J

questions.question: A 3.00-g copper penny at 25.0^oC drops from a height of 50.0 m to the ground. (a) If 60.0 percent of the change in potential energy goes into increasing the internal energy, what is its final temperature? Specific heat of Cu = 0.385J/g ^0C.

questions.explanation:
mgh = 3 $\times$ 10^−3 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 50 = 1.5J
Heat used in increasing temp 
$Q=1.5 \times \frac{60}{100}$ =0.9 J
Q = ms$\Delta$T
0.9 = 3 $\times$ 0.385 $\times$ (T − 298)
T = 298.8K = 25.8^oC

questions.question: A water heater is operated by solar power. If the solar collector has an area of 6.00 m^2 and the power delivered by sunlight is 550 W/ m^2, how long does it take to increase the temperature of 1.00 m^3 of water from 20.0^oC to 60.0^oC?
questions.question_raw: A water heater is operated by solar power. If the solar collector has an area of 6.00 m2 and the power delivered by sunlight is 550 W/ m2, how long does it take to increase the temperature of 1.00 m3 of water from 20.0oC to 60.0oC?
questions.explanation:
Q = $msΔT$
$ P = {W \over t} = {{ms\Delta T} \over t} $
t = $ {{ms\Delta T} \over P} = {{1000 \times 4186 \times 40} \over {550 \times 6}} $ = 50700sec = 50.7Ksec

questions.question: A sample of an ideal gas is expanded to twice its original volume of 1.00 m^3 in a quasi-static process for which P = $ \alpha {V^2}$with $\alpha $= 5.00atm/m^6. How much work is done by the expanding gas?
questions.question_raw: A sample of an ideal gas is expanded to twice its original volume of 1.00 m3 in a quasi-static process for which P = {tex} \alpha {V^2}{/tex}with {tex}\alpha {/tex}= 5.00atm/m6. How much work is done by the expanding gas?
questions.explanation:
P = $\alpha$V^2
$\alpha$ = 5.00 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 Pa/m^6
V_i = 1.00 m^3
V_f = 2.00 m^3
Work done by the gas W = $\int_{1.00}^{2.00} P . d V=\int_{1.00}^{2.00} \alpha V^{2} \cdot d V=\left[\frac{\alpha V^{3}}{3}\right]_{1.00}^{2.00}$ = ${\alpha \over 3} \times (2^3-1^3)$ =1.18 MJ

Work done by gas = area under P-V curve
 IAF
 Work done by the gas = Area under the graph IA = 4 $\times$ 1.013 $\times$ 10^5 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 10^-3 = 810 J
 IF
 Work done by the gas = area under the graph If = $\frac {1}{2}$(1 + 4) $\times$ 1.013 $\times$10^5 $\times$ 2 $\times$10^-3 = 506J
 IBF
 Work done by the gas= area under the graph BF = 1 $\times$ 1.013 $\times$ 10^5 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 10^-3 = 203 J

questions.question: Consider the cyclic process depicted in Figure If Q is negative for the process BC, and if $\Delta {{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{int}}}}$ is negative for the process CA, what are the signs of Q, W, and $\Delta {{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{int}}}}$ that are associated with AB?
questions.question_raw: Consider the cyclic process depicted in Figure If Q is negative for the process BC, and if {tex}\Delta {{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{int}}}}{/tex} is negative for the process CA, what are the signs of Q, W, and {tex}\Delta {{\rm{E}}_{{\rm{int}}}}{/tex} that are associated with AB?
questions.explanation:
W = +ve (Expansion)
U_c - U_a = -ve
U_b - U_c = -ve
hence U_b - U_A = -ve
U_a - U_b = + ve
$\Delta$Q = $\Delta$U + W
$\Delta$U = + ve
W = + ve
Hence $\Delta$Q = + ve

12.5 = $\Delta$U + 5
$\Delta$U = 7.5KJ

in the isothermal process

questions.question: An ideal gas is carried through a thermodynamic cycle consisting of two isobaric and two isothermal processes, as shown in Figure net work done in the entire cycle is given by the equation?
questions.question_raw: An ideal gas is carried through a thermodynamic cycle consisting of two isobaric and two isothermal processes, as shown in Figure net work done in the entire cycle is given by the equation?
questions.explanation:
W = W_AB + W_BC + W_CD + W_DA
W = $\int_{A}^{B} P \cdot d V+\int_{B}^{C} P . d V+\int_{C}^{D} P . d V+\int_{D}^{A} P . d V$
W = nRT_1$\int_\limits{A}^{B}\left(\frac{d V}{V}\right)+P_{2} \int_\limits{B}^{C} d V+n R T_{2} \int_\limits{C}^{D}\left(\frac{d V}{V}\right)+P_{1} \int_{D} d V$
W = nRT_1log_e($\frac{V_B}{V_1}$) + P_2(V_C - V_B) + nRT_2log_e($\frac{V_2}{V_C}$) + P_1(V_A - V_D)
P_1V_A = P_2V_B
P_2V_C = P_1V_D
$\frac{V_B}{V_1}$ = $\frac{P_1}{P_2}$
$\frac{V_2}{V_c}$ = $\frac{P_2}{P_1}$
W = P_1(V_2 - V_1)log_e($\frac{P_2}{P_1}$)

questions.question: A Carnot engine has a power output of 150 kW. The engine operates between two reservoirs at 20.0^oC and 500^oC. How much energy does it absorb per hour?
questions.question_raw: A Carnot engine has a power output of 150 kW. The engine operates between two reservoirs at 20.0oC and 500oC. How much energy does it absorb per hour?
questions.explanation:
$\eta = {{{T_H} - {T_L}} \over {{T_H}}} $$= {{480} \over {773}} $ = 0.62
$\eta$ = $ {{{\rm{Work (Output)}}} \over {{\rm{Heat Extracted(input)}}}} $ 
Power = Energy output per second = 150KJ
Efficiency = Energy Output / Energy Absorbed
Energy absorbed per second = Energy output / Efficiency = $ {{150 \times {{10}^3}} \over {0.62}}J$ 
Energy absorbed per hour = $ = {{150 \times {{10}^3}} \over {0.62}} \times 3600 $ = 869MJ

questions.question_raw: A Carnot engine has a power output of 150 kW. The engine operates between two reservoirs at 20.0oC and 500oC. How much energy is lost per hour in its exhaust?
questions.explanation:
$\eta=\frac{T_{H}-T_{L}}{T_{H}}=\frac{480}{773}$ = 0.62
$\eta=\frac{\text { Work }(\text { Output })}{\text { HeatExtracted }(\text { input })}$
Power = Energy output per second = 150KJ
Efficiency = Energy Output / Energy Absorbed
Energy absorbed per second = $Energy \ output \over Efficiency$ = $ {{150 \times {{10}^3}} \over {0.62}}J$ 
Energy loss per hour = $ = {{150 \times {{10}^3}} \over {0.62}} $ −150 $\times$ 10^3 = 91935J
Energy loss per hour = 91935 $\times$ 3600 = 330MJ

Assuming that the plant’s efficiency changes in the same proportion as the ideal efficiency, what would be the plant’s efficiency in the winter, when the seawater is at 10.0^oC?
questions.question_raw: A power plant operates at 32.0 percent efficiency during the summer when the seawater for cooling is at 20.0oC. The plant uses 350oC steam to drive turbines. Assuming that the plant’s efficiency changes in the same proportion as the ideal efficiency, what would be the plant’s efficiency in the winter, when the seawater is at 10.0oC?
questions.explanation:
$\eta = {{{T_H} - {T_L}} \over {{T_H}}}$
$\eta \propto \Delta T$
${{{\eta _1}} \over {{\eta _2}}} = {{\Delta {T_1}} \over {\Delta {T_2}}}$
${{32} \over \eta } = {{330} \over {340}}$
$\eta $ = 33%

questions.question_raw: A power plant that would make use of the temperature gradient in the ocean has been proposed. The system is to operate between 5.00oC (water temperature at a depth of about 1 km) and 20.0{tex}^\circ {/tex}C (surface water temperature). If the power output of the plant is 75.0 MW, how much energy is absorbed per hour?
questions.explanation:
$\eta = {{{T_H} - {T_L}} \over {{T_H}}} = {{293 - 278} \over {293}} $ = 0.0512 = 5.12%
Efficiency = $Energy \ output \over Energy \ Absorbed$ 
Energy output per second = Power output = 75 MW
Energy absorbed per second = $Energy \ output \ per \ second \over Efficiency$ 
Energy absorbed per second $ = {{75 \times {{10}^6}} \over {0.0512}} = 1465 \times {10^6}J$ 
Energy absorbed per hour = 1465 $\times$ 10^6 $\times$ 3600 = 5.27 $\times$10^12 J = 5.27TJ/hr

questions.question: A typical dorm room or bedroom contains about 2500 moles of air. Find the change in the internal energy of this much air when it is cooled from 35^oC to 26^oC at a constant pressure of 1.00 atm. Treat the air as an ideal gas with γ = 1.4.
questions.question_raw: A typical dorm room or bedroom contains about 2500 moles of air. Find the change in the internal energy of this much air when it is cooled from 35oC to 26oC at a constant pressure of 1.00 atm. Treat the air as an ideal gas with γ = 1.4.
questions.explanation:
${C_V} = {R \over {\gamma - 1}} = {{8.31} \over {1.4 - 1}} = {{8.31} \over {0.4}}$
$\Delta U = m\times C_V\times \Delta T$
$\Delta U = 2500 \times {{8.31} \over {0.4}} \times (299 - 308) $ = −4.68 $\times$ 10^5J

questions.question: Kinetic theory explains the behavior
questions.question_raw: Kinetic theory explains the behavior

options.label: A
options.text: of solids and liquids based on the idea that they gas consist of rapidly vibrating atoms or molecules

options.label: B
options.text: of solids based on the idea that the solid consists of rapidly vibrating atoms or molecules

options.label: C
options.text: of gases based on the idea that the gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules

options.label: D
options.text: of liquids based on the idea that the liquids consist of rapidly moving atoms or molecules

correct_option_texts: of gases based on the idea that the gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules
questions.explanation:
The theory for ideal gases makes the following assumptions
1. Gases consist of particles in constant, random motion. They continue in a straight line until they collide with something—usually each other or the walls of their container.
2. Particles are point masses with no volume. The particles are so small compared to the space between them, that we do not consider their size in ideal gases.
3. No molecular forces are at work. This means that there is no attraction or repulsion between the particles.
4. Gas pressure is due to the molecules colliding with the walls of the container. All of these collisions are perfectly elastic, meaning that there is no change in energy of either the particles or the wall upon collision. No energy is lost or gained from collisions.
5. The time it takes to collide is negligible compared with the time between collisions.
6. The kinetic energy of a gas is a measure of its Kelvin temperature. Individual gas molecules have different speeds, but the temperature and kinetic energy of the gas refer to the average of these speeds.
7. The average kinetic energy of a gas particle is directly proportional to the temperature. An increase in temperature increases the speed in which the gas molecules move.
8. All gases at a given temperature have the same average kinetic energy.
9. Lighter gas molecules move faster than heavier molecules.

options.label: D

questions.explanation: The kinetic theory describes a gas as a large number of submicroscopic particles (atoms or molecules), all of which are in constant rapid motion that has randomness arising from their many collisions with each other and with the walls of the container. It will apply only to gases.

questions.explanation: Kinetic theory explains the behaviour of gases based on the idea that the gas consists of rapidly moving atoms or molecules. It also relates measurable properties of gases such as viscosity, conduction and diffusion with molecular parameters, yielding estimates of molecular sizes and masses.

questions.explanation:
Avogadro's law states that "equal volumes of all gases, at the same temperature and pressure, have the same number of molecules"
For a given mass of an ideal gas, the volume and amount (moles) of the gas are directly proportional if the temperature and pressure are constant.
V $ \propto $ n
${V \over n} = k$

questions.question: In which case are the atoms relatively rigidly fixed?
questions.question_raw: In which case are the atoms relatively rigidly fixed?
questions.explanation:
Atoms in a gas are well separated with no regular arrangement. Atoms vibrate and move freely at high speeds
Atoms in a liquid are close together with no regular arrangement. Atoms vibrate, move about, and slide past each other.
Atoms in a solid are tightly packed, usually in a regular pattern. Atoms vibrate (jiggle) but generally do not move from place to place.

questions.question: A cubic vessel (with faces horizontal + vertical) contains an ideal gas at NTP. The vessel is being carried by a rocket which is moving at a speed of 500ms^-1 in vertical direction. The pressure of the gas inside the vessel as observed by us on the ground
questions.question_raw: A cubic vessel (with faces horizontal + vertical) contains an ideal gas at NTP. The vessel is being carried by a rocket which is moving at a speed of 500ms-1 in vertical direction. The pressure of the gas inside the vessel as observed by us on the ground

options.label: A
options.text: remains the same because the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules and the walls.

options.label: B
options.text: will be different on the top wall and bottom wall of the vessel.

options.label: C
options.text: will increase by a factor equal to $\frac{\left(V_{\mathrm{rms}}^2+(500)^2\right)}{v^{22}}$ where v_rms was the original mean square velocity of the gas.

options.label: D
options.text: remain the same because 500 ms^-1 is very much smaller than v_rms of the gas.

correct_option_texts: remains the same because the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules and the walls.
questions.explanation:
According to the ideal gas law,
P = $\frac{nRT}{V}$, here the temperature of the vessel remains unchanged hence, the pressure remains the same from that point of view.
Now, let us discuss the phenomenon inside the vessel. The gas molecules keep on colliding among themselves as well as with the walls of containing vessel. These collisions are perfectly elastic.
The number of collisions per unit volume in a gas remains constant. So, the pressure of the gas inside the vessel remains the same because the motion of the vessel as a whole does not affect the relative motion of the gas molecules with respect to the walls.

questions.question: According to Dalton’s law of partial pressures:

questions.explanation:
According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, at constant volume and temperature, the total pressure exerted by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the component gases
P = P_1 + P_2 + .......
P = $\frac{\mu_{1} R T}{V}$ + $\frac{\mu_{2} R T}{V}$ + ........
PV = $(\mu_{1}+\mu_{2}+.....)$ RT
PV = $\mu $RT

questions.question: Considering a gas in a parallelopiped, in a small time interval $\Delta {\rm{t}}$, a molecule with x-component of velocity v_x will hit the wall?
questions.question_raw: Considering a gas in a parallelopiped, in a small time interval {tex}\Delta {\rm{t}}{/tex}, a molecule with x-component of velocity vx will hit the wall?

options.label: A
options.text: If it is outside or beyond the distance v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

options.label: B
options.text: If it is outside or beyond the distance 2v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

options.label: C
options.text: If it is within the distance v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

options.label: D
options.text: If it is within the distance 2v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall

correct_option_texts: If it is within the distance v_x $\Delta {\rm{t}}$ from the wall
questions.explanation:
Collision by molecule to the wall of container assumed to be perfectly elastic.
By the principle of conservation of momentum, the momentum imparted to the wall in the collision = 2mv_x
So the magnitude of the force on the wall from each collision is
$F = {{2m{v_x}} \over {\Delta t}}$ 
distance travelled in time $\Delta t$ 
$x = {v_x}\Delta t$

questions.question: If there are n number of molecules per unit volume and m is the mass of each, v_x is the x-component of velocity, pressure can be written as:
questions.question_raw: If there are n number of molecules per unit volume and m is the mass of each, vx is the x-component of velocity, pressure can be written as:
questions.explanation:
Pressure is due to collision of molecule with the wall of container.
Collision by molecule to the wall of container assumed to be perfectly elastic.
By the principle of conservation of momentum, the momentum imparted to the wall in the collision = 2mv_x 
the number of molecules hitting the wall in time $\Delta t$ = $nA{v_x}\Delta t$ 
n = no. of molecule per unit volume
A = Area of container
But, on the average, half of these are moving towards the wall and the other half away from the wall. Thus, the number of molecules with velocity (v_x , v_y, v_z) hitting the wall in time ∆t is ½A v_x∆t n, where n is the number of molecules per unit volume. The total momentum transferred to the wall by these molecules in time ∆t is: Q = (2mv_x ) (½ n A v_x ∆t )
The total momentum transferred to the wall by these molecules in $\Delta t$ time = $m{v_x}\left( {nA{v_x}\Delta t} \right)$ 
Pressure P = $F\over A$ = rate of momentum transfer per unit area
P = $ {{m{v_x}\left( {nA{v_x}\Delta t} \right)/\Delta t} \over A} $ = $nm{v^2}_x$ 
all molecules in a gas do not have the same velocity; there is a distribution in velocities. The above equation therefore, stands for pressure due to the group of molecules with speed v_x in the x-direction and n stands for the number density of that group of molecules. The total pressure due to all groups 
P = $ nm\overline {v_x^2} $

questions.question: An approximate equation for calculating the mean free path is:
questions.question_raw: An approximate equation for calculating the mean free path is:
questions.explanation:
If the molecules have diameter d, then the effective cross-section for a collision can be A = $ \pi {d^2}$ 
l = distance travelled
n = no. of molecule per unit volume
V = volume of interaction
The mean free path could be taken as the length of the path divided by the number of collisions.
mean free path
$\lambda=\frac{l}{V \times n}=\frac{l}{(A l) \times n}=\frac{\bar{v} t}{\left(\pi d^2 \times \bar{v} t\right) \times n}$
$\lambda=\frac{1}{\pi d^2 n}$

Radius = $1.5 \mathop A\limits^o $ 
molecular volume of oxygen 
$V = {N_A} \times {4 \over 3}\pi {r^3} $ = 6.023 $\times$ 10^23 $\times$ $4\over 3$ $\times$ 3.14 $\times$(1.5 $\times$10^−8)^3 = 8.51cm^3
Actual volume of oxygen at STP = 22400cm^3
ratio of molecular volume to the actual volume is given by = ${{8.51} \over {22400}} = 3.8 \times {10^{ - 4}}$

questions.question: The figure shows a plot of $\frac{PV}{T}$versus P for 1.00 $ \times $ 10^−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. The dotted plot corresponds to
questions.question_raw: The figure shows a plot of {tex}\frac{PV}{T}{/tex}versus P for 1.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. The dotted plot corresponds to
questions.explanation:
For 1 mole of ideal gas, according to ideal gas equation​​​​
PV = RT
$\frac{PV}{T}$ = RT = const.
Hence the graph must be with zero slopes. So that dotted line show ‘ideal’ gas behaviour and the curved line shows deviation from ‘ideal’ gas behaviour.

questions.explanation:
For 1 mole of an ideal gas, according to the ideal gas equation
​​​​​PV = RT
$\frac{PV}{T}$ = R = constant
Hence the graph must be with zero slopes. So that dotted line show ‘ideal’ gas behaviour and the curved line show deviation from ‘ideal’ gas behaviour.
A real gas behaves as an ideal gas at high temperature. Temperature T_1 is close to the dotted line. 
so that T_1 > T_2

questions.question: The figure shows a plot of $\frac{PV}{T}$ versus P for 1.00$ \times $ 10^−3 kg of oxygen gas at two different temperatures. What is the value of$\frac{PV}{T}$ where the curves meet on the y-axis?

questions.explanation:

$\frac{PV}{T}$ = nR = $\frac{1}{32}$ $\times$ 8.314 = 0.26 JK^-1
Hence the value of $\frac{PV}{T}$ where the curves meet on the y-axis is 0.26 J K^−1

questions.question: An oxygen cylinder of volume 30 liters has an initial gauge pressure of 15 atm and a temperature of 27 ^oC. After some oxygen is withdrawn from the cylinder, the gauge pressure drops to 11 atm and its temperature drops to 17 ^oC. Estimate the mass of oxygen taken out of the cylinder (R = 8.31 J mol^-1 K^-1, the molecular mass of O_2 = 32 u).
questions.question_raw: An oxygen cylinder of volume 30 liters has an initial gauge pressure of 15 atm and a temperature of 27 oC. After some oxygen is withdrawn from the cylinder, the gauge pressure drops to 11 atm and its temperature drops to 17 oC. Estimate the mass of oxygen taken out of the cylinder (R = 8.31 J mol-1 K-1, the molecular mass of O2 = 32 u).
questions.explanation:
V_1 = 30lit = 30 $\times$ 10^−3m^3
P_1 = 15atm = 15 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 = 15.15 $\times$ 10^5Pa
T_1 = 27^oC = 300K
P_1V_1 = n_1RT_1
$n_{1}=\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{R T_{1}}=\frac{15.15 \times 10^{5} \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{8.31 \times 300}$ = 18.276
$n_{1}=\frac{m_{1}}{M}$
m_1 = n_1M = 18.276 $\times$ 32 = 584.84gm
When some oxygen is withdrawn from the cylinder 
V_2 = 30lit = 30 $\times$ 10^−3m^3
P_1 = 11atm = 11 $\times$ 1.01 $\times$ 10^5 = 11.11 $\times$ 10^5Pa
T_1 = 17^oC = 300K
P_2V_2 = n_2RT_2
$n_{2}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{R T_{2}}=\frac{11.11 \times 10^{5} \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{8.31 \times 290}$ = 13.86
${n_2} = {{{m_2}} \over M}$ = 
m_2 = n_2M = 13.86 $\times$ 32 = 453.1gm
the mass of oxygen taken out of the cylinder = m_1 − m_2 = 584.84 − 453.1 = 131.74gm = 0.13Kg.

questions.question: An air bubble of volume 1.0 cm^3 rises from the bottom of a lake 40 m deep at a temperature of 12 ^oC. To what volume does it grow when it reaches the surface, which is at a temperature of 35 ^oC?
questions.question_raw: An air bubble of volume 1.0 cm3 rises from the bottom of a lake 40 m deep at a temperature of 12 oC. To what volume does it grow when it reaches the surface, which is at a temperature of 35 oC?
questions.explanation:
P_1 = P_2 +h$\rho$g
V_1 = 1.0cm^3
h = 40m
T_1 = 35^oC = 308K , density of water is = 10^3 kg m^-3
Pressure at surface P_2 = 1atm = 1.013$\times$ 10^5Pa
Pressure at depth of 40 meter = P_1 = 1atm + h$\rho$g = (1.013 $\times$ 10^5) + 40 $\times$ 10^3 $\times$ 9.8 = 493300Pa
${{{P_1}{V_1}} \over {{T_1}}} = {{{P_2}{V_2}} \over {{T_2}}} $
${V_2} = {{{P_1}{V_1}{T_2}} \over {{P_2}{T_1}}} $ = $ {{493300 \times 1.0 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 308} \over {1.013 \times {{10}^5} \times 285}} $ = 5.3 $\times$ 10^−6m^3.

questions.question: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). The number of molecules:
questions.question_raw: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). The number of molecules:

options.label: A
options.text: is the greatest in the vessel with neon

options.label: B
options.text: is the greatest in the vessel with chlorine

options.label: C
options.text: is the same in all three vessels

options.label: D
options.text: is the greatest in the vessel with uranium hexafluoride

correct_option_texts: is the same in all three vessels
questions.explanation: Avogadro's law states that "equal volumes of all gases, at the same temperature and pressure, have the same number of molecules".

questions.question: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). In which case is v_rms the largest?
questions.question_raw: Three vessels of equal capacity have gases at the same temperature and pressure. The first vessel contains neon (monatomic), the second contains chlorine (diatomic), and the third contains uranium hexafluoride (polyatomic). In which case is vrms the largest?

options.label: A
options.text: v_rms is the same in all vessels.

options.label: C
options.text: v_rms is largest for uranium hexafluoride.

options.label: D
options.text: v_rms is the largest for neon.

correct_option_texts: v_rms is the largest for neon.
questions.explanation:
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}}$
${v_{rms}} \propto {1 \over {\sqrt M }}$ 
Neon has smaller molecular wt in comparison to chlorine and uranium hexafluoride. So that v_rms s largest for neon.

questions.question: At what temperature is the root mean square speed of an atom in an argon gas cylinder equal to the rms speed of a helium gas atom at – 20 ^oC? (Atomic mass of Ar = 39.9 u, of He = 4.0 u).
questions.question_raw: At what temperature is the root mean square speed of an atom in an argon gas cylinder equal to the rms speed of a helium gas atom at – 20 oC? (Atomic mass of Ar = 39.9 u, of He = 4.0 u).
questions.explanation:
v_Ar = v_He
$\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{39.9}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 253}{4}}$
T = $ {{39.9 \times 253} \over 4} $ = 2.52 $\times$ 10^3K

questions.question: Estimate the mean free path and collision frequency of a nitrogen molecule in a cylinder containing nitrogen at 2.0 atm and temperature 17^oC. Take the radius of a nitrogen molecule to be roughly 1.0 $\mathop A\limits^0 $.
questions.question_raw: Estimate the mean free path and collision frequency of a nitrogen molecule in a cylinder containing nitrogen at 2.0 atm and temperature 17oC. Take the radius of a nitrogen molecule to be roughly 1.0 {tex}\mathop A\limits^0 {/tex}.
questions.explanation:
$\lambda = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}{n_V}}}$ 
n_V = no.of molecule per unit volume ($N\over V$)
PV = NkT
${n_V} = {N \over V} = {P \over {kT}}$
$\lambda = {{kT} \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}P}} $ = $ {{1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 290} \over {1.41 \times 3.14 \times {{\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}^2} \times \left( {2 \times 1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \right)}}$
$\lambda = 1.11 \times {10^{ - 7}}m$ 
collision frequency${{{v_{rms}}} \over \lambda }$ 
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} = \sqrt {{{3 \times 8.31 \times 290} \over {28 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} $ = 508.26m/sec 
collision frequency $ = {{508.26} \over {1.11 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} $ = 4.58 $\times$ 10^9s^−1

questions.question: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 $ \times $ 10^−3 cm^2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the tensile strain?
questions.question_raw: In constructing a large mobile, an artist hangs an aluminum sphere of mass 6.0 kg from a vertical steel wire 0.50 m long and 2.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−3 cm2 in a cross-sectional area. On the bottom of the sphere, he attaches a similar steel wire, from which he hangs a brass cube of mass 10.0 kg. Compute the tensile strain?
questions.explanation:
Given mass of aluminium sphere m_sph = 6.0 KG
length of steel wire L_steel = 0.50 m area of steel wire A_steel = 2.5 $\times$ 10^-3 cm^2
mass of cube m_cube = 10 KG
net restoring force in lower wire i.e. tension T_2 will be equal to weight
of cube i.e. T_2 = m_cube = 10g
net restoring force in upper wire i.e. tension T_1
T_1 = m_sph + T_2 = 6g + 10g = 16g
standard value for young modulus of steel y = 2 $\times$ 10^11 N/m^2
y = $\frac{\text { tensile stress }}{\text { tensile strain }}$ for lower wire
$\operatorname{strain}_{l w}=\frac{\text {tensile stress}}{y}=\frac{10 \times 9.8}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
$\text {strain}_{l w} = 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-3} $ $\rightarrow$(1)
now for upper wire
$\operatorname{strain}_{u p} = \frac{\text {tensile stress}}{y} = \frac{16 \times 9.8}{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
strain_up = 3.14 $\times$ 10^−3 $\Rightarrow$ (2)

questions.question: Air at 20.0 ^oC in the cylinder of a diesel engine is compressed from an initial pressure of 1.00 atm and a volume of 800.0 cm^3 to a volume of 60.0 cm^3. Assume that air behaves as an ideal gas with $\gamma $ = 1.4 and that the compression is adiabatic. Find the final pressure of the air.
questions.question_raw: Air at 20.0 oC in the cylinder of a diesel engine is compressed from an initial pressure of 1.00 atm and a volume of 800.0 cm3 to a volume of 60.0 cm3. Assume that air behaves as an ideal gas with {tex}\gamma {/tex} = 1.4 and that the compression is adiabatic. Find the final pressure of the air.
questions.explanation:
$P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}=P_{2} V_{2}^{\gamma}$
P_2 = $P_{1}\left(\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right)^{\gamma}=1 \times\left(\frac{800}{60}\right)^{1.4}$ = 37.6atm

no. of molecule per unit volume 
n_V = ${N \over V} = {P \over {kT}} = {{1.01 \times {{10}^5}} \over {1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 293}} $ = 2.5 $\times $ 10^25
mean free path

questions.question: Approximate the air around you as a collection of nitrogen molecules, each of which has a diameter of 2.00 $ \times $ 10^−10 m. On average, how frequently does one molecule collide with another at 298K?
questions.question_raw: Approximate the air around you as a collection of nitrogen molecules, each of which has a diameter of 2.00 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−10 m. On average, how frequently does one molecule collide with another at 298K?
questions.explanation:
collision frequency $ = {1 \over \tau }$ = $ {{{v_{rms}}} \over \lambda }$ 
no. of molecule per unit volume 
n_V = $ {N \over V} = {P \over {kT}} = {{1.013 \times {{10}^5}} \over {1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 293}} $ = 2.5 $\times$ 10^25
mean free path 
$\lambda $ = $ {1 \over {\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}{n_V}}} = {1 \over {1.41 \times 3.14 \times {{\left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}} \right)}^2} \times 2.5 \times {{10}^{25}}}} $ = 2.25 $\times$ 10^−7m
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {{{3RT} \over M}} = \sqrt {{{3 \times 8.31 \times 298} \over {28 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} $= 515m/sec
 hence, collision frequency is given by $ = {1 \over \tau } = {{{v_{rms}}} \over \lambda } = {{515} \over {2.25 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}} $ = 2.28 $\times$ 10^9/sec

questions.question: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m^2 at an angle of 45.0^o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what are the average force and pressure on the window?
questions.question_raw: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m2 at an angle of 45.0o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what are the average force and pressure on the window?
questions.explanation:
$F = 500 \times {{\Delta p} \over {\Delta t}} $
$= 500 \times {{2mv\cos 45^\circ } \over {\Delta t}} $
$= {{500 \times 2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 8 \times 1} \over {30 \times 1.41}} $
F = 0.943N

questions.question: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m^2 at an angle of 45.0^o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what is the pressure on the window?
questions.question_raw: In a 30.0-s interval, 500 hailstones strike a glass window with an area of 0.600 m2 at an angle of 45.0o to the window surface. Each hailstone has a mass of 5.00 g and a speed of 8.00 m/s. If the collisions are elastic, what is the pressure on the window?
questions.explanation:
$ P = {F \over A}$
$F = 500 \times {{\Delta p} \over {\Delta t}} $
$= 500 \times {{2mv\cos 45^\circ } \over {\Delta t}} $
$= {{500 \times 2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 8 \times 1} \over {30 \times 1.41}}$
A = 0.6 m^2
P = $ {{500 \times 2 \times 5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 8 \times 1} \over {30 \times 1.41 \times 0.6}} $ = 1.57Pa

questions.question: A 14.5 kg mass, fastened to the end of a steel wire of unstretched length 1.0 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of 2 rev/s at the bottom of the circle. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.065 cm^2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path.
questions.question_raw: A 14.5 kg mass, fastened to the end of a steel wire of unstretched length 1.0 m, is whirled in a vertical circle with an angular velocity of 2 rev/s at the bottom of the circle. The cross-sectional area of the wire is 0.065 cm2. Calculate the elongation of the wire when the mass is at the lowest point of its path.
questions.explanation:
given mass m = 14.5 kg
length of steel wire L = 1.0m
angular velocity $\omega$ = 2rev/s $\Rightarrow$ 2 $\times$ 2$\pi$rad/s = 4$\pi$rad/s cross sectional area of steel wire = 0.065cm^2 = 0.065 $\times$10^−4m^2
let us consider $\Delta$L be the elongation in steel wire when mass is at lowest point during its motion in vertical circle and T be the tension in wire apply in g_net force = centripetal force
T − mg = m$\omega$^2r
T = (14.5 $\times$ 9.8) + 14.5 $\times$ (4$\pi$)^2 $\times$ 1
T = 2429.5N now young modulus of steel wire y = 2.0 $\times$ 10^11pa
also $\mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{stress}}{\operatorname{strain}}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \Delta \mathrm{L}}$
$\Delta \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{T} \times \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{y}}=\frac{2429.5 \times 1.0}{0.065 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11}}$
$\Delta$L = 1.9 $\times$ 10^−3m = 1.9mm

questions.question: A spherical balloon with a volume of 4 .0 x 10^3 cm^3 contains helium at an (inside) pressure of 1.20 $ \times $ 10^5 Pa. How many moles of helium are in the balloon if each helium atom has an average kinetic energy of 3.60 $ \times $ 10^-22 J?
questions.question_raw: A spherical balloon with a volume of 4 .0 x 103 cm3 contains helium at an (inside) pressure of 1.20 {tex} \times {/tex} 105 Pa. How many moles of helium are in the balloon if each helium atom has an average kinetic energy of 3.60 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-22 J?
questions.explanation:
We know that ,$P = {2 \over 3}{N \over V}\left( {\bar K} \right)$
$N = {3 \over 2}{{PV} \over {\left( {\bar K} \right)}} = {3 \over 2} \times {{\left( {1.20 \times {{10}^5}} \right)\left( {4.00 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)} \over {3.60 \times {{10}^{ - 22}}}} $ = 2 $\times$ 10^24
n = $ {N \over {{N_A}}} = {{2 \times {{10}^{24}}} \over {6.023 \times {{10}^{23}}}} $ = 3.32mole

questions.question: Anvils made of single crystals of diamond, with the shape as shown in Figure, are used to investigate the behaviour of materials under very high pressures. Flat faces at the narrow end of the anvil have a diameter of 0.50 mm, and the wide ends are subjected to a compression force of 50,000 N. What is the pressure at the tip of the anvil?
questions.question_raw: Anvils made of single crystals of diamond, with the shape as shown in Figure, are used to investigate the behaviour of materials under very high pressures. Flat faces at the narrow end of the anvil have a diameter of 0.50 mm, and the wide ends are subjected to a compression force of 50,000 N. What is the pressure at the tip of the anvil?
questions.explanation:
Given compression force F = 50000 N
Radius will be r = $\frac{diameter}{2}$ = $\frac{0.50}{2}$ = 0.25 mm = 0.25 $\times$ 10^-3 m
Area at which compression is applied A = $\pi$r^2 = 3.14 $\times$ (0.25 $\times$ 10^-3)^2 = 0.2 $\times$ 10^-6 m^2 
Pressure at the tip P = $\frac{Force}{area}$ = $\frac{50000}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}$
P = 2.5 $\times$ 10^11 pa

questions.question: A house has well-insulated walls. It contains a volume of 100 m^3 of air at 300 K. Calculate the energy required to increase the temperature of this diatomic ideal gas by 1.00 ^oC. $\left(C_P=\frac{7}{2} R\right)$

questions.explanation:
Q = $ n{C_P}\Delta T $ = $\frac 7 2 Rn\Delta T $ = $\frac{7}{2}\left(\frac{P V}{T}\right) \Delta T$ (because, PV=nRT thus n = $\frac {PV}{ RT}$)
Q = $\frac{7}{2} \times\left(\frac{1.01 \times 10^5 \times 100}{300}\right) \times 1$ = 118KJ

questions.question: A house has well-insulated walls. It contains a volume of 100 ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{3}}}$ of air at 300 K.
