$\lambda$ = 1.8m
distance between two antinodes is = $\lambda\over 2$= $1.8\over 2$= 0.9

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Waves

questions.question: A fork gives 5 beats with a 40 cm length of sonometer wire. If the length of the wire is shortened by 1 cm, the number of beats is still the same. The frequency of the fork is:
questions.question_raw: A fork gives 5 beats with a 40 cm length of sonometer wire. If the length of the wire is shortened by 1 cm, the number of beats is still the same. The frequency of the fork is:
questions.explanation:
According to the law of length
$f \propto \frac{1}{L}$
Let the frequency of fork is f if 5 beats produced initially
f_1 = f + 5
later also 5 beats produced 
f_2 = f - 5
thus 
f_1L_2_ = f_2L_1
(f + 5)39 = (f - 5)40
on solving f = 395Hz

questions.question: A tuning fork produces 4 beats/sec. with 50 cm and 40 cm of a stretched wire, of a sonometer. The frequency of fork is:
questions.question_raw: A tuning fork produces 4 beats/sec. with 50 cm and 40 cm of a stretched wire, of a sonometer. The frequency of fork is:
questions.explanation:
the beat frequency is given by
f_beat = f_1 − f_2
4 = f_1 − f_2 $\rightarrow$ (1)
also frequency
f $\propto$ $1 \over L$
$\frac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\frac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}$
$\frac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\frac{40}{50}$ $\rightarrow$ (2)
on solving equation 1 and 2 
f_1 = 16 Hz
f_2 = -20 Hz
|f_1 - f_2| = 36 Hz

questions.question: Two waves represented by Y_1 = a_1 sin $\omega$t and Y_2 = a_2 (sin $\omega$t +$\Delta$) and $\Delta = \frac \pi 2$ are superimposed at a point at a particular instant. The amplitude of the resultant wave is:
questions.question_raw: Two waves represented by Y1 = a1 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t and Y2 = a2 (sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t +{tex}\Delta{/tex}) and {tex}\Delta = \frac \pi 2{/tex} are superimposed at a point at a particular instant. The amplitude of the resultant wave is:
questions.explanation:
Resultant amplitude is given by 
$A^{2}=a^{2}_{1}+a^{2}_{2}+2 a_{1} a_{2} \cos \Delta$
where $\Delta $ is phase difference between two waves.
here $\Delta=\frac{\pi}{2}$
thus A^2 =$\;\;{a^2}_1+{a^2}_2$
A = $\sqrt {a_1^2 +a_2^2}$

questions.question: The fundamental frequency of an open pipe is N keeping the pipe vertical. It is submerged in water so that half of its length is filled with water. The fundamental frequency of the air column is:
questions.question_raw: The fundamental frequency of an open pipe is N keeping the pipe vertical. It is submerged in water so that half of its length is filled with water. The fundamental frequency of the air column is:
questions.explanation: f_n = $\frac{nv}{2L}$ Thus frequency does not depend on the medium.

questions.question: A tuning fork of frequency 500 Hz is sounded on a resonance tube. The first & second resonances are obtained at 17 cm and 34 cm. The velocity of sound in m/s is:
questions.question_raw: A tuning fork of frequency 500 Hz is sounded on a resonance tube. The first & second resonances are obtained at 17 cm and 34 cm. The velocity of sound in m/s is:
questions.explanation:
$f=\frac{nv}{2L}$
$500=\frac{1\times v\times100}{2\times17}$
v = 170m/s
For n = 2 and L = 34 cm 
V will also come out 170 m/s

questions.question: A whistling engine is approaching a stationary observer with a velocity 110 m/s. The velocity of sound is 330 m/s. The ratio of frequencies as heard by an observer at the time of approaching and passing of engine is:
questions.question_raw: A whistling engine is approaching a stationary observer with a velocity 110 m/s. The velocity of sound is 330 m/s. The ratio of frequencies as heard by an observer at the time of approaching and passing of engine is:
questions.explanation:
After passage, v is the velocity of sound and u is the velocity of whistling engine and f is the frequency 
$f_1=\frac v{v+u}\times f$
$\frac{f_1}f=\frac{330}{440}$
before passing while approaching
$f_2=\frac v{v-u}\times f$
$\frac{f_2}f=\frac{330}{220}$
the ratio
$\frac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}$ = 2:1

questions.question: When a wave undergoes reflection at rarer medium then it undergoes a phase difference of:
questions.question_raw: When a wave undergoes reflection at rarer medium then it undergoes a phase difference of:
questions.explanation: When a wave travels from a denser medium to rarer medium, the phase change of wave is zero. But from rarer medium to denser medium it is $\pi$.

questions.question: The frequency of the third harmonic of a closed organ pipe is equal to which of the overtones?
questions.question_raw: The frequency of the third harmonic of a closed organ pipe is equal to which of the overtones?
questions.explanation:
Frequencies that are an integral multiple of the fundamental frequency are called harmonics or overtones.
$\begin{array}{l}f_n=\frac{nv}{2L}\\\end{array}$ 
n = 1, is called the fundamental frequency 
n = 2, 1st overtone or 2nd harmonic
n = 3, 2nd overtone or 3rd harmonic

questions.question: A stretched string fixed at both ends has n nodes then the length of the string in terms of wavelength is:
questions.question_raw: A stretched string fixed at both ends has n nodes then the length of the string in terms of wavelength is:
questions.explanation:
To satisfy boundary conditions 
kL = n$\pi$
$\frac{2\pi L}\lambda$ = $n\pi$ 
L = $\frac{n \lambda}{2}$ 
Where n can be 1, 2, 3, ....

questions.question: An echo repeats two syllables. If the velocity of sound is 330 m/s, then the distance of reflecting surface is:
questions.question_raw: An echo repeats two syllables. If the velocity of sound is 330 m/s, then the distance of reflecting surface is:
questions.explanation:
Two syllables means sound takes 0.4 s for producing the echo
So the distance of reflecting surface from the sound source is 
= $\frac{speed\;\times\;time}2$ ​​​​​​ = $\frac{330\;\times\;0.4}2$ = 66 m

questions.question: A boat at anchor is rocked by waves whose crests are 100 cm apart and whose velocity is 25 cm/sec. These waves reach the boat once every:
questions.question_raw: A boat at anchor is rocked by waves whose crests are 100 cm apart and whose velocity is 25 cm/sec. These waves reach the boat once every:
questions.explanation:
Here, velocity = 25 cm/s & wavelength = 100 cm
As velocity = frequency $\times$ wavelength
25 = frequency X 100 $\Rightarrow$ frequency = 0.25
Hence time period = 1/frequency = 4 s

questions.question: A certain broadcasting station broadcasts at a frequency of 12 Mega Hz. The wavelength of waves emitted is:
questions.question_raw: A certain broadcasting station broadcasts at a frequency of 12 Mega Hz. The wavelength of waves emitted is:
questions.explanation:
Here, frequency = 12 MHz = 12000000 Hz
Speed of wave = 300000000 m/s
Wavelength = speed/frequency = $300000000 \over12000000$ = 25 m

questions.question: A sound wave has a frequency of 500 Hz and a velocity of 350 m/s. What is the distance between the two particles having a phase difference of 60^o.
questions.question_raw: A sound wave has a frequency of 500 Hz and a velocity of 350 m/s. What is the distance between the two particles having a phase difference of 60o.
questions.explanation:
Here Frequency = 500 Hz
Velocity = 350 m/s
Phase difference = 600
As wavelength = velocity /frequency = 350/500 = 0.7 m
Now, path difference = $(\frac{\lambda}{2\pi})$ phase difference
Path difference =$(\frac{0.7}{2\pi})$$(\frac{\pi}{3})$=0.116 m = 11.6 cm

questions.question: There are three sources of the sound of equal intensities with frequencies 400, 401, and 402 Hz. The number of beats per seconds is:
questions.question_raw: There are three sources of the sound of equal intensities with frequencies 400, 401, and 402 Hz. The number of beats per seconds is:
questions.explanation:
Beat frequency formula for calculating the beat between two overlapping sound waves is f_b = f_1 - f_2 (f_1 & f_2 are two incident sound waves).
_f1 = 402 - 401 = 1 Hz
_f2 = 402 - 400 = 2 Hz
F_b = 2 - 1 = 1 Hz

questions.question: The velocity of sound in gas in which two waves of wavelength 1.0 m and 0.01 m produces 4 beats/sec. is:
questions.question_raw: The velocity of sound in gas in which two waves of wavelength 1.0 m and 0.01 m produces 4 beats/sec. is:
questions.explanation:
Let’s start with what we are given, assign some names.
$\lambda_0 $ = 1.00m 
$\lambda_1 $ = 1.01m = $\lambda_0 $ + 0.01m
We will use f_0 and f_1 for the frequencies corresponding to the two wavelength. Since the two frequencies superimposed produce a variation of 4 beats in 1 second we also have:
f_0 - f_1 = 4
f_1 = f_0 - 4
in general, we have v = λf and in particular:
$\lambda_0 $f_0 = $\lambda_1 $f_1. Substituting gives:
$\lambda_0 $f_0 = ($\lambda_0 $ + 0.01)(f_0 - 4) Simplifying gives:
0 = - 4$\lambda_0 $ + 0.01f_0 - 0.04
we have:
0 = 0.01f_0 - 4.04,so
f_0 = 4.04/0.01 = 404 . Since $\lambda_0 $ = 1.00m we have: v = $\lambda_0 $f_0
v = 404 m/s

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Waves

questions.question: A fork of unknown frequency, when sounded with another fork of frequency 256 Hz, produces 4 beats/sec. The first fork is loaded with wax. It again produces 4 beats/sec. When sounded together with the fork of 256 Hz frequency, then the frequency of the first tuning fork is:
questions.question_raw: A fork of unknown frequency, when sounded with another fork of frequency 256 Hz, produces 4 beats/sec. The first fork is loaded with wax. It again produces 4 beats/sec. When sounded together with the fork of 256 Hz frequency, then the frequency of the first tuning fork is:
questions.explanation:
An unknown tuning fork i is producing 4 beats/sec with the fork of frequency 256Hz. This means that their frequencies differ by 4. The unknown fork might have frequency either 252 Hz or 260 Hz.
On the application of wax, the number of beats remains to 4 per second which means they differ only by 4 and it is only possible when the unknown fork has a greater frequency.
Hence the unknown tuning fork has a frequency of 260Hz.

questions.question: Two tuning forks of frequency 250 Hz and 256Hz produce beats. If a maximum is produced just now, after how much time the minimum is produced at the same place:
questions.question_raw: Two tuning forks of frequency 250 Hz and 256Hz produce beats. If a maximum is produced just now, after how much time the minimum is produced at the same place:
questions.explanation:
The time interval between two consecutive maxima and minima is 
$\triangle t=\frac1{n_1-n_2}$ 
For consecutive maxima and minima, the time interval will be $\frac{1}{4}$ s or 0.25s
Thus time interval between maxima and minima at the same place will be $\frac{1}{4}$ s or 0.25s

questions.question: 56 tuning forks are arranged in a series that each fork gives 4 beats/sec, with the previous one. The frequency of the last fork is 3 times that of first. Then the frequency of the first fork is:
questions.question_raw: 56 tuning forks are arranged in a series that each fork gives 4 beats/sec, with the previous one. The frequency of the last fork is 3 times that of first. Then the frequency of the first fork is:
questions.explanation:
Frequency of beats is given by
fbeat = |f_1 − f_2|
given fbeat = 4
thus 4 = f_1 − f_2
4 = f_2 − f_3
and so on
4 = f_55 − f_56
we get 55such equations on adding all equations we get
220 = f_1 − f_56
given f_56 = 3f_1
on substituting we get
f_1 = 110Hz

questions.question: Speed of Sound is given by $V = \sqrt {\frac {B}{\rho }} $ where:
questions.question_raw: Speed of Sound is given by {tex}V = \sqrt {\frac {B}{\rho }} {/tex} where:

options.label: A
options.text: B is compressibility and $\rho $ the specific volume of the medium

options.label: B
options.text: B is bulk modulus and $\rho $ the density of the medium

options.label: C
options.text: B is compressibility and $\rho $ the density of the medium

options.label: D
options.text: B is bulk modulus and $\rho $ the specific volume of the medium.

correct_option_texts: B is bulk modulus and $\rho $ the density of the medium
questions.explanation: The speed of sound increases with the stiffness (the resistance of an elastic body to deformation by an applied force) or Bulk modulus of the material and decreases with an increase in density.

questions.question: When two or more waves traverse the same medium, the displacement of any element of the medium is the vector sum of the displacements due to each wave. This is known as:
questions.question_raw: When two or more waves traverse the same medium, the displacement of any element of the medium is the vector sum of the displacements due to each wave. This is known as:

options.label: A
options.text: The principle of superposition of waves

options.label: C
options.text: The principle of reciprocity of waves

options.label: D
options.text: The principle of conservation of waves

correct_option_texts: The principle of superposition of waves
questions.explanation:
The principle of superposition may be applied to waves ​​​whenever two (or more) waves travelling through the same medium at the same time. The waves pass through each other without being disturbed. The net displacement of the medium at any point in space or time is simply the sum of the individual wave displacements.
As Displacement of the resultant wave is given by
y = y_1 + y_2 (for same direction)
y = y_1 - y_2 (for opposite direction)

questions.question: Two sinusoidal waves on the same string exhibit interference, adding or cancelling according to the principle of superposition. If the two are travelling in the same direction and have the same amplitude a and frequency but differ in phase by a phase constant $\varphi $, the result is a single wave with the same frequency $\omega $, If $\varphi $ = 0 or an integral multiple of 2 $\pi $, The waves are exactly:
questions.question_raw: Two sinusoidal waves on the same string exhibit interference, adding or cancelling according to the principle of superposition. If the two are travelling in the same direction and have the same amplitude a and frequency but differ in phase by a phase constant {tex}\varphi {/tex}, the result is a single wave with the same frequency {tex}\omega {/tex}, If {tex}\varphi {/tex} = 0 or an integral multiple of 2 {tex}\pi {/tex}, The waves are exactly:

options.label: A
options.text: in-phase and the interference is constructive.

options.label: B
options.text: out of phase and the interference is destructive.

options.label: C
options.text: in phase and the interference is destructive.

options.label: D
options.text: out of phase and the interference is constructive.

correct_option_texts: in-phase and the interference is constructive.
questions.explanation: As two waves are in the same phase (vibration is in the same direction) so their amplitudes are added together & hence interference will be constructive. The condition that arises from the superposition principle for constructive interference is $\begin{array}{l}\phi=2n\pi\\\end{array}$

questions.question: A travelling wave, at a rigid boundary or a closed-end _______.
questions.question_raw: A travelling wave, at a rigid boundary or a closed-end _______.

options.label: A
options.text: is reflected with the same phase

options.label: B
options.text: is reflected with a phase difference of 30^o

options.label: C
options.text: is reflected with a phase difference of 70^o

options.label: D
options.text: is reflected with a phase reversal

correct_option_texts: is reflected with a phase reversal
questions.explanation: In reflection of this kind, there must be a node at the support as the string is fixed there. The reflected and incident pulses must have opposite signs, so as to cancel each other at that point. Thus in case of a travelling wave, the reflection at a rigid boundary will take place with a phase reversal or with a phase difference of 180^o.

questions.question: Standing waves are characterized by fixed locations of zero displacement called nodes and fixed locations of maximum displacements called antinodes. The separation between two consecutive nodes or antinodes is:
questions.question_raw: Standing waves are characterized by fixed locations of zero displacement called nodes and fixed locations of maximum displacements called antinodes. The separation between two consecutive nodes or antinodes is:
questions.explanation: At locations x = 0,$\frac{\lambda}{2}$, $\lambda $, $\frac{3\lambda}{2}$, ... called the nodes the amplitude is always zero, whereas at locations x = $\frac{\lambda}{4}, \frac{3 \lambda}{4}, \frac{5 \lambda}{4}$ ... called the anti-nodes, the amplitude is maximum. The distance between two consecutive nodes or anti-nodes is $\frac{\lambda}{2}$.

questions.question: A pipe of length l with one end closed and another end open (such as air columns) vibrates in the fundamental mode at:
questions.question_raw: A pipe of length l with one end closed and another end open (such as air columns) vibrates in the fundamental mode at:
questions.explanation:
In the first mode of vibration in the closed organ pipe, an antinode is formed at the open end and a node is formed at the close end. If ‘L’ be the length of pipe and λ be the wavelength of wave emitted in this mode of vibration.
Then,
$l = \frac{{{\lambda _0}}}{4}$
$\lambda = 4l$ 
if ‘v’ be the velocity of sound and to be the frequency of the wave in this mode of vibration.
${f_0} = \frac{v}{{{\lambda _0}}} = \frac{v}{{4l}}$ 
This is the fundamental frequency of 1^st harmonics.

questions.question: A string of length L fixed at both ends or an air column closed at one end and open at the other end, vibrates with frequencies called its normal modes. Each of these frequencies is:
questions.question_raw: A string of length L fixed at both ends or an air column closed at one end and open at the other end, vibrates with frequencies called its normal modes. Each of these frequencies is:

options.label: A
options.text: a sub-harmonic frequency of the system

options.label: B
options.text: a forced frequency of the system

options.label: C
options.text: a resonant frequency of the system

options.label: D
options.text: an excitation frequency of the system

correct_option_texts: a resonant frequency of the system
questions.explanation: A resonant frequency is a natural frequency of vibration determined by the physical parameters of the vibrating object. It is easy to get an object to vibrate at its resonant frequencies, hard to get it to vibrate at other frequencies. A string of length L fixed at both ends or an air column closed at one end and open at the other end, vibrates with frequencies called its normal modes. Each of these frequencies is called the resonant frequency of the system.

questions.question: Beats arise when two waves having slightly different frequencies, ${\nu _{\rm{1}}}$ and ${\nu _{\rm{2}}}$ and comparable amplitudes, are superimposed. The beat frequency is:
questions.question_raw: Beats arise when two waves having slightly different frequencies, {tex}{\nu _{\rm{1}}}{/tex} and {tex}{\nu _{\rm{2}}}{/tex} and comparable amplitudes, are superimposed. The beat frequency is:
questions.explanation: The beat frequency is always equal to the difference in frequency of the two notes that interfere to produce the beats. So if two sound waves with frequencies of 256 Hz and 254 Hz are played simultaneously, a beat frequency of 2 Hz will be detected.

questions.question: The human ear is most sensitive to sound in the frequency range of:
questions.question_raw: The human ear is most sensitive to sound in the frequency range of:
questions.explanation: Humans are most sensitive to (i.e. able to discern at lowest intensity) frequencies between 2,000 and 5,000 Hz. The human ear can respond to minute pressure variations in the air if they are in the audible frequency range, roughly 20 Hz - 20 kHz. This incredible sensitivity is enhanced by an effective amplification of the sound signal by the outer and middle ear structures.

questions.question: in transverse waves, the constituents of the medium:
questions.question_raw: in transverse waves, the constituents of the medium:

options.label: A
options.text: oscillate at 40$^\circ $ to the direction of wave propagation

options.label: B
options.text: oscillate at 30^o to the direction of wave propagation

options.label: C
options.text: oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation

options.label: D
options.text: oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation

correct_option_texts: oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation
questions.explanation: In transverse wave motion, medium particles oscillate or vibrate in a direction perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation to form crest & trough in the wave motion.

questions.question: in longitudinal waves, the constituents of the medium:
questions.question_raw: in longitudinal waves, the constituents of the medium:

options.label: A
options.text: oscillate along the direction of wave propagation

options.label: B
options.text: oscillate at 40$^\circ $ to the direction of wave propagation

options.label: C
options.text: oscillate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation

options.label: D
options.text: oscillate at 30$^\circ $ to the direction of wave propagation

correct_option_texts: oscillate along the direction of wave propagation
questions.explanation: In longitudinal wave motion, medium particles oscillate or vibrate in the same direction of wave propagation to produce compression & rarefaction in wave propagation.

questions.question: Travelling or progressive wave:
questions.question_raw: Travelling or progressive wave:

options.label: A
options.text: does not move from one point of the medium to any other

options.label: B
options.text: does not move from one point of the medium to all others

options.label: C
options.text: travels from one point of the medium to another

options.label: D
options.text: does not move from one point of the medium to another

correct_option_texts: travels from one point of the medium to another
questions.explanation: As progressive wave means a wave propagating in some onward direction in the medium.

tests.category_full_name: CBSE > Class 11 > Physics > Waves

questions.question: The waves on the surface of the water are of two kinds:
questions.question_raw: The waves on the surface of the water are of two kinds:
questions.explanation:
As capillary & gravity waves are elastic waves or mechanical waves which require a medium for their propagation.
Hence they are using the elastic behaviour of water.
Hence, the waves on the surface of the water are of two kinds: capillary waves and gravity waves

questions.question: In the same medium transverse and longitudinal waves:
questions.question_raw: In the same medium transverse and longitudinal waves:

options.label: B
options.text: travel changing transverse wave to longitudinal wave

options.label: C
options.text: travel changing longitudinal wave to transverse wave

questions.explanation:
As the speed of transverse & longitudinal waves depends on the different modulus of elasticity (Young's modulus, Bulk modulus, Modulus of Rigidity) of the medium.
So in the same medium, transverse and longitudinal waves travel at different speeds.

questions.question: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $) represents a wave function:
questions.question_raw: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + {tex}\omega {/tex}t + {tex}\varphi {/tex}) represents a wave function:

options.label: B
options.text: the equation does not tell the direction

options.label: D
options.text: direction of travel is sometimes –ve x and +x at other times

questions.explanation:
On comparing above equation with
y(x, t) = a sin (-kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $), we get
Distance traveled by wave is along - x axis
Hence y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $) represents a wave function
travelling in the -ve x direction

questions.question: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ ) represents a wave function then ‘a’ is:
questions.question_raw: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + {tex}\omega {/tex}t + {tex}\varphi {/tex} ) represents a wave function then ‘a’ is:
questions.explanation:
In the equation
y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ )
the term before the trigonometric function is called amplitude
Hence a (term before trigonometric function) is the amplitude.

questions.question: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ ) represents a wave function then ‘k’ is:
questions.question_raw: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + {tex}\omega {/tex}t + {tex}\varphi {/tex} ) represents a wave function then ‘k’ is:
questions.explanation:
In the equation 
y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ )
the term with distance travelled 'x' is called angular wavenumber or propagation constant
Hence k is the angular wavenumber.

questions.question: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ ) represents a wave function then ‘$\omega $’ is:
questions.question_raw: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + {tex}\omega {/tex}t + {tex}\varphi {/tex} ) represents a wave function then ‘{tex}\omega {/tex}’ is:
questions.explanation:
In the equation 
y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ )
the term with time 't' is called angular velocity or angular frequency
Hence, $\omega $ is the angular frequency.

questions.question: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ ) represents a wave function then ‘$\varphi $’ is:
questions.question_raw: If y(x, t) = a sin (kx + {tex}\omega {/tex}t + {tex}\varphi {/tex} ) represents a wave function then ‘{tex}\varphi {/tex}’ is:
questions.explanation:
In the equation
y(x, t) = a sin (kx + $\omega $t + $\varphi $ )
The term $\varphi $ represents the initial phase or epoch
Hence the term $\varphi $ is the initial phase angle.

questions.question: The speed of propagation of a sinusoidal wave is given by v = $ \nu \lambda $ where:
questions.question_raw: The speed of propagation of a sinusoidal wave is given by v = {tex} \nu \lambda {/tex} where:

options.label: A
options.text: v is the reciprocal of the period and $\lambda $ is the wave number

options.label: B
options.text: v is the angular frequency and $\lambda $ is the wavelength

options.label: C
options.text: v is the reciprocal of the period and $\lambda $ is the dispersion

options.label: D
options.text: $\nu $ is the reciprocal of the time period and $\lambda $ is the wavelength

correct_option_texts: $\nu $ is the reciprocal of the time period and $\lambda $ is the wavelength
questions.explanation:
$\nu$ is here frequency
we know that
Using Speed = distance /time
For one cycle, distance is given by = $\lambda $ ,and time is given by = T
Hence v= $\lambda \over T$
$v = \nu \lambda $

questions.question: The speed of transverse waves on a stretched string is $V = \sqrt {\frac {T} {\mu }} $ where:
questions.question_raw: The speed of transverse waves on a stretched string is {tex}V = \sqrt {\frac {T} {\mu }} {/tex} where:

options.label: A
options.text: T is the tension in the string and $\mu $ the mass per unit length

options.label: B
options.text: T is the gravitational force and $\mu $ the mass per unit length

options.label: C
options.text: T is the initial energy and $\mu $ the mass per unit length

options.label: D
options.text: T is the tension in the string and $\mu $ the mass of the string

correct_option_texts: T is the tension in the string and $\mu $ the mass per unit length
questions.explanation: The speed of a wave along a stretched string depends only on the tension and the linear mass density of the wire. Speed of transverse wave on stretched string is v= $\sqrt {\frac {T}{\mu}}$ where T is the tension of the string and $\mu$ is the mass per unit length of the string.

questions.question: In wave propagation:
questions.question_raw: In wave propagation:

options.label: A
options.text: there is a flow of matter and there is no movement of disturbance.

options.label: B
options.text: there is no flow of matter and there is no movement of disturbance.

options.label: C
options.text: there is a flow of matter but there is the movement of disturbance.

options.label: D
options.text: there is no flow of matter but there is the movement of disturbance.

correct_option_texts: there is no flow of matter but there is the movement of disturbance.
questions.explanation: Because in wave propagation particles only moves in periodic motion, particles get collide with their neighbouring particles then transfer energy to them and come back to their normal stage. Now next particle which gains energy gets into an excited state and starts moving periodically and gets collide to the next adjacent particle and so on. Thus in wave propagation particles only transfer their kinetic energy and momentum. Hence particles do not move therefore there is no flow of matter but there is the movement of disturbance.

questions.question: When we make a mobile telephone call to a friend:
questions.question_raw: When we make a mobile telephone call to a friend:

options.label: A
options.text: the friend's mobile receives gravity waves containing your audio.

options.label: B
options.text: the friend's mobile receives acoustic waves containing your audio.

options.label: C
options.text: the friend's mobile receives electromagnetic waves containing your audio.

options.label: D
options.text: the friend's mobile generates possible electrical signals.

correct_option_texts: the friend's mobile receives electromagnetic waves containing your audio.
questions.explanation: Because mobile communication is a space communication and in space communication basically electromagnetic waves are used (as carrier waves as in case of radio communication) because of the modulation ( frequency, amplitude) operations that can be performed on EM waves. Thus when our friend receives the call, he also receives EM waves which are the carrier of our audio signals.

questions.question: Electromagnetic waves are different from sound waves in that:
questions.question_raw: Electromagnetic waves are different from sound waves in that:

options.label: A
options.text: they need medium and are transverse.

options.label: B
options.text: they need no medium and are transverse.

options.label: C
options.text: they need no medium and are longitudinal.

options.label: D
options.text: they need medium and are longitudinal.

correct_option_texts: they need no medium and are transverse.
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic waves are transverse waves. The EM waves are the waves that are created due to oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular mutually and also to the direction of motion and EM waves(Electromagnetic waves) can travel in a vacuum, thus doesn't require any medium also.

questions.question: Matter waves are useful in:
questions.question_raw: Matter waves are useful in:
questions.explanation: Matter waves are also termed as De Broglie waves because they were initially introduced by him. All matters behave like a wave (during motion) and in their propagation, there is the action of forces due to collision between the particles, some time torque also exists, hence all the matters waves were considered to be a helpful part for understanding quantum.

questions.question: The quantity similar to extension or compression of the spring in sound wave propagation (air) is:
questions.question_raw: The quantity similar to extension or compression of the spring in sound wave propagation (air) is:
questions.explanation: As in airwave propagates in the form of compression (increase in density of air) and rarefaction (decrease in density of air).

questions.question: Two sinusoidal waves on the same string exhibit interference, adding or cancelling according to the principle of superposition. If the two are travelling in the same direction and have the same amplitude a and frequency but differ in phase by a phase constant $\varphi $, the result is a single wave with the same frequency $\omega $, If $\varphi $ = 0 or an integral multiple of 2 $\pi $, The waves are exactly:
questions.question_raw: Two sinusoidal waves on the same string exhibit interference, adding or cancelling according to the principle of superposition. If the two are travelling in the same direction and have the same amplitude a and frequency but differ in phase by a phase constant {tex}\varphi {/tex}, the result is a single wave with the same frequency {tex}\omega {/tex}, If {tex}\varphi {/tex} = 0 or an integral multiple of 2 {tex}\pi {/tex}, The waves are exactly:

options.label: A
options.text: out of phase and the interference is constructive.

options.label: B
options.text: out of phase and the interference is destructive.

options.label: C
options.text: in phase and the interference is destructive.

options.label: D
options.text: in-phase and the interference is constructive.

correct_option_texts: in-phase and the interference is constructive.
questions.explanation: As two waves are in the same phase (vibration is in the same direction) so their amplitudes are added together & hence interference will be constructive. The condition that arises from the superposition principle for constructive interference is $\begin{array}{l}\phi=2n\pi\\\end{array}$
